rule: A UTF-8 String Pair is a data structure used in MQTT v5 to represent name-value pairs, consisting of two consecutive UTF-8 Encoded Strings where the first string serves as the name and the second string contains the value. This structure applies when encoding user properties, subscription identifiers, or other name-value data in MQTT packets. Both strings MUST comply with the UTF-8 encoding requirements defined in the specification. If a receiver (Client or Server) encounters a UTF-8 String Pair where either string violates these encoding rules, it MUST treat the entire packet as malformed and follow the error handling procedures specified in section 4.13.

The payload in an MQTT PUBLISH packet carries the Application Message, which contains the actual data being transmitted between clients. This rule applies when a client or server sends a PUBLISH packet to distribute application data, and the payload is optional depending on the QoS level and retained message settings. The expected outcome is that the receiving client or server processes the Application Message according to the topic and QoS specified in the packet header. Prerequisites include establishing a valid MQTT connection and ensuring the PUBLISH packet structure complies with the MQTT specification, including variable header fields like Topic Name and Packet Identifier (if required by QoS).

The packet size of an MQTT Control Packet is defined as the total number of bytes comprising the entire packet, calculated as the sum of the Fixed Header length and the Remaining Length value. This rule applies during both packet construction by senders and packet parsing by receivers, ensuring consistent interpretation of packet boundaries across all MQTT control packet types. The expected outcome is accurate determination of packet boundaries for proper protocol processing, with the prerequisite that the Remaining Length has been correctly encoded as a Variable Byte Integer representing only the bytes in the Variable Header and Payload (excluding the Remaining Length encoding bytes themselves).

When encoding MQTT v5 control packets, if a packet's variable header contains no properties, the Property Length field MUST be set to zero to explicitly indicate the absence of any properties. This applies during packet construction when the protocol requires a variable header with properties but none are present. The Property Length is encoded as a Variable Byte Integer and represents only the length of the Properties data (excluding its own encoding bytes). Following this rule ensures proper packet parsing and prevents misinterpretation of subsequent packet fields. This requirement applies to all MQTT v5 control packets that include a Properties structure in their variable header.
req_type: all MQTT v5 control packets that include a Properties structure in their variable header

rule: The MQTT v5 protocol specifies that certain control packets must include a payload as part of their structure, while others are prohibited from carrying one. This rule applies during packet construction and parsing, ensuring proper protocol compliance. The CONNECT, SUBSCRIBE, SUBACK, UNSUBSCRIBE, and UNSUBACK packets must contain a payload (marked Required), while PUBLISH may optionally include one. All other control packets (CONNACK, PUBACK, PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP, PINGREQ, PINGRESP, DISCONNECT, AUTH) must not contain any payload. The expected outcome is correct packet formatting that enables successful protocol operations such as connection establishment, message publishing, subscription management, and acknowledgment handling.

In the MQTT v5 protocol, the CONNECT packet contains an optional Will Properties field within its Payload section, which follows the Variable Header. This field allows clients to specify additional properties for the Will Message that will be published if the connection is unexpectedly terminated. The Will Properties must be encoded as a set of properties consisting of a Property Length followed by the actual properties, and they are only applicable if the CONNECT packet includes a Will Message (indicated by the Will Flag being set to 1 in the Fixed Header). When present, these properties govern the behavior and metadata of the Will Message publication, such as content type, response topic, or user properties.

The UTF-8 String Pair data type, consisting of two UTF-8 Encoded Strings, is used to hold name-value pairs where the first string serves as the name and the second string contains the value. This rule applies whenever UTF-8 String Pairs are transmitted in MQTT v5 protocol packets, such as in user properties, subscription options, or other name-value contexts. Both strings MUST comply with UTF-8 Encoded String requirements, including valid UTF-8 encoding and character restrictions. If a receiver (Client or Server) receives a UTF-8 String Pair that violates these requirements, it MUST treat the packet as malformed and follow the error handling procedures specified in section 4.13. The expected outcome when this rule is followed is proper interpretation and processing of name-value pairs throughout the protocol exchange.

The Property Length field in the MQTT v5 packet variable header is encoded as a Variable Byte Integer. This length value represents the total byte count of all Properties that follow, but excludes the bytes occupied by the Property Length field itself. This rule applies when encoding or decoding any MQTT v5 packet that contains properties in its variable header. When followed correctly, implementations will accurately parse or construct property sections by reading/writing exactly the number of bytes specified. A prerequisite is that the packet type supports properties in its variable header. If no properties are present, the Property Length MUST be set to zero to indicate an empty property section [MQTT-2.2.2-1].
req_type: any MQTT v5 packet that contains properties in its variable header

rule: A UTF-8 String Pair consists of two UTF-8 Encoded Strings where the first serves as the name and the second as the value. Both strings MUST comply with the UTF-8 Encoded String requirements defined in the MQTT v5 specification. This rule applies whenever a receiver (Client or Server) processes any packet containing UTF-8 String Pairs during protocol communication. If either string in the pair contains invalid UTF-8 encoding, violates string length restrictions, or includes disallowed Unicode code points, the receiver MUST treat the entire packet as Malformed. The expected outcome is immediate termination of the network connection as specified in section 4.13, ensuring protocol integrity and preventing processing of malformed data. This validation MUST occur before any semantic processing of the string pair content.
req_type: any packet containing UTF-8 String Pairs

rule: The Packet Identifier field, located within the Variable Header between the Fixed Header and Payload, is a shared component across multiple MQTT Control Packet types. This field is used to uniquely identify packets that require acknowledgment or tracking, such as PUBLISH (with QoS > 0), PUBACK, PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP, SUBSCRIBE, SUBACK, UNSUBSCRIBE, and UNSUBACK. Its presence enables reliable message delivery and request-response correlation by allowing clients and servers to match related packets. The Packet Identifier must be included when the packet type requires it and must be a non-zero 16-bit value that is unique among in-flight packets for a given client session.
res_type: PUBACK, PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP, SUBACK, UNSUBACK

rule: The set of Properties, which appears as the last field in the Variable Header of CONNECT, CONNACK, PUBLISH, PUBACK, PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP, SUBSCRIBE, SUBACK, UNSUBSCRIBE, UNSUBACK, DISCONNECT, and AUTH packets (and optionally in the CONNECT packet's Will Properties field), must be structured as a Property Length field followed by the actual Properties. This structure applies whenever properties are included in these packet types, ensuring proper parsing and interpretation of the variable-length property data. The Property Length field specifies the total remaining length of the Properties data, allowing receivers to correctly extract and process all subsequent property entries.
req_type: CONNECT, CONNACK, PUBLISH, PUBACK, PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP, SUBSCRIBE, SUBACK, UNSUBSCRIBE, UNSUBACK, DISCONNECT, AUTH

rule: In MQTT v5, the Variable Header of all control packets (CONNECT, CONNACK, PUBLISH, PUBACK, PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP, SUBSCRIBE, SUBACK, UNSUBSCRIBE, UNSUBACK, DISCONNECT, and AUTH) must conclude with a Properties field, which appears as the final component of the Variable Header. This Properties field consists of a Property Length indicator followed by zero or more individual properties, enabling extensible metadata exchange between client and server. This rule applies during packet construction and parsing phases, ensuring consistent property handling across all packet types. Note that CONNECT packets additionally support a separate Will Properties field within the payload, which follows the same structure but serves different semantic purposes.
req_type: CONNECT, CONNACK, PUBLISH, PUBACK, PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP, SUBSCRIBE, SUBACK, UNSUBSCRIBE, UNSUBACK, DISCONNECT, AUTH

rule: After establishing a Network Connection to a Server, the Client MUST send exactly one CONNECT packet as the first packet in the session. Any subsequent CONNECT packet sent over the same Network Connection constitutes a Protocol Error, and the Server MUST close the connection immediately. This rule applies from the moment the Network Connection is established and governs the initial handshake phase of the MQTT session.

The Fixed Header byte 1 bits [3-0] contain packet-type-specific flags as defined in Table 2-2. Any flag bits marked as Reserved must be set to their specified values [MQTT-2.1.3-1]. During packet parsing, if any flag bits are set to values not permitted for that packet type (including invalid Reserved bit values), the receiving entity MUST treat the packet as Malformed and follow the error handling procedures specified in section 4.13. This validation applies to all MQTT Control Packet types immediately upon receiving the Fixed Header.

After establishing a network connection, the Client MUST send exactly one CONNECT packet as the first packet in the session [MQTT-3.1.0-1]. If the Server receives any subsequent CONNECT packet from the same Client on the same network connection, it MUST treat this as a protocol violation, immediately close the network connection, and terminate the session [MQTT-3.1.0-2]. This rule applies throughout the entire connection lifecycle and is not affected by the content or configuration of the CONNECT packet payload.

In the MQTT v5 protocol, bits [3-0] of byte 1 in the Fixed Header contain control packet-specific flags as defined in Table 2-2. When a flag bit is designated as Reserved in the specification, it is reserved for future protocol versions and MUST be set to the value specified in the current standard [MQTT-2.1.3-1]. This rule applies to all MQTT Control Packets during both packet construction by senders and validation by receivers. Following this requirement ensures forward compatibility and prevents protocol misinterpretation. If a receiver detects any Reserved flag set to an incorrect value, the packet MUST be considered malformed and handled according to the error procedures specified in section 4.13.
req_fields: bits [3-0] of byte 1 in the Fixed Header

rule: The DUP, QoS, and RETAIN flags in the PUBLISH packet are located in bits [3-0] of the Fixed Header byte 1 and govern specific message delivery behaviors. This rule applies when processing PUBLISH packets during message transmission and reception. The DUP flag indicates whether the packet is a duplicate delivery attempt, the QoS flag specifies the Quality of Service level (0, 1, or 2) for message delivery assurance, and the RETAIN flag determines if the message should be stored by the server as the last retained message for the topic. These flags must be interpreted according to their defined meanings in section 3.3.1, and any invalid flag combinations constitute a Malformed Packet requiring error handling as specified in section 4.13.

Upon successful establishment of a Network Connection from a Client to a Server, the Client MUST initiate the MQTT session by sending a CONNECT packet as the very first control packet [MQTT-3.1.0-1]. This rule applies immediately after the underlying transport connection is established and before any other MQTT control packets are exchanged. The expected outcome is proper session initialization, allowing the Server to authenticate the Client and configure session parameters. The prerequisite is that a valid network-level connection must exist between Client and Server. Note that the CONNECT packet can only be sent once per connection; any subsequent CONNECT packet constitutes a protocol error and must result in connection termination by the Server [MQTT-3.1.0-2].

When establishing an MQTT v5 connection, the CONNECT packet's Variable Header must be constructed with fields in the following strict sequence: Protocol Name, Protocol Level, Connect Flags, Keep Alive, and Properties. This rule applies during the initial connection handshake when a client sends a CONNECT packet to a broker. Following this structure ensures proper protocol identification, feature negotiation, and session configuration. The Protocol Name and Level fields verify MQTT v5 compatibility, Connect Flags control connection behavior (such as clean start and will message settings), Keep Alive defines the maximum time between client messages, and Properties carry extended session parameters encoded as defined in section 2.2.2. Any deviation from this field order constitutes a protocol violation and must result in connection termination with an appropriate error code.

The CONNECT packet Fixed Header structure governs the initial byte-level format that must be used when establishing an MQTT v5 connection. This rule applies during the connection initiation phase when a client sends a CONNECT packet to a broker. Byte 1 (bits 7-0) must contain the MQTT Control Packet type value 1 (indicating CONNECT) in bits 7-4, with bits 3-0 reserved and set to 0. Byte 2 contains the Remaining Length field encoded as a Variable Byte Integer, which represents the total length of both the Variable Header and Payload sections that follow. The expected outcome is proper packet framing that enables the broker to correctly parse the connection request. Prerequisites include the client's intent to establish an MQTT session and proper encoding of the Remaining Length field according to the Variable Byte Integer specification.
req_fields: Fixed Header Byte 1 (bits 7-4: Packet Type, bits 3-0: Reserved)

rule: In the MQTT v5 protocol, the Fixed Header of a PUBLISH packet uses bits [3-0] of byte 1 to convey specific control flags: the DUP flag indicates whether the packet is a duplicate delivery attempt, the QoS field specifies the Quality of Service level for message delivery, and the RETAIN flag determines if the message should be stored as a retained message by the server. These flags apply when a PUBLISH packet is constructed by the sender and processed by the receiver, and they must be set according to the packet's intended behavior. If invalid flag values are received, the packet is considered malformed and must be handled as specified in the protocol error handling section. Proper interpretation of these flags ensures correct message delivery semantics, duplicate handling, and retained message management as defined in section 3.3.1.

The MQTT v5 protocol specifies that certain control packets must not contain any payload data. Specifically, CONNACK, PUBACK, PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP, PINGREQ, PINGRESP, DISCONNECT, and AUTH packets are defined to have no payload section. This applies throughout the protocol lifecycle: CONNACK during connection establishment, PUBACK/PUBREC/PUBREL/PUBCOMP during QoS message flows, PINGREQ/PINGRESP for keep-alive, DISCONNECT for connection termination, and AUTH for enhanced authentication. When implemented correctly, these packets should consist only of fixed header and variable header sections. The rule is unconditional - these packet types must never include payload data regardless of context or session state. Any payload received in these packet types constitutes a protocol violation and should result in connection termination as specified in the protocol error handling procedures.
req_type: CONNACK, PUBACK, PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP, PINGREQ, PINGRESP, DISCONNECT, AUTH

rule: During the initial CONNECT packet exchange after establishing a network connection, the payload may contain multiple optional fields including Will Topic, Will Payload, User Name, and Password. While the Client Identifier field is mandatory, all other payload fields are optional and their presence or absence is indicated by corresponding flags (Will Flag, User Name Flag, Password Flag) set in the Variable Header's Connect Flags byte. The server must parse these flags to determine which optional fields are present in the payload and process them accordingly, while ensuring the Client Identifier is always provided as required by the protocol specification.

The Fixed Header of every MQTT v5 Control Packet includes four flag bits (bits 3-0 of byte 1) whose meaning and valid values are specific to the packet type. This rule applies during the parsing of any incoming or construction of any outgoing MQTT Control Packet, including CONNECT, CONNACK, PUBLISH, PUBACK, PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP, SUBSCRIBE, SUBACK, UNSUBSCRIBE, UNSUBACK, PINGREQ, PINGRESP, DISCONNECT, and AUTH. The expected outcome is correct interpretation and handling of packet-specific flags: for example, in PUBLISH packets, these flags control duplicate delivery (DUP), Quality of Service level (QoS), and message retention (RETAIN). Reserved flag bits must be set to their specified values, and any deviation from the defined flag patterns results in a Malformed Packet, which must be handled according to the protocol's error handling procedures. Prerequisites include having a valid packet type identifier in the same Fixed Header and knowledge of the specific flag definitions provided in Table 2-2 and related sections (e.g., section 3.3.1 for PUBLISH flags).
req_type: All MQTT Control Packets (CONNECT, CONNACK, PUBLISH, PUBACK, PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP, SUBSCRIBE, SUBACK, UNSUBSCRIBE, UNSUBACK, PINGREQ, PINGRESP, DISCONNECT, AUTH)

rule: The Protocol Version byte is a one-byte unsigned value in the CONNECT packet variable header that identifies the revision level of the MQTT protocol implemented by the Client. This rule applies during connection establishment when the Server processes the CONNECT packet to determine protocol compatibility. For MQTT v5.0, this value must be 5 (0x05). If the Server supports multiple protocol versions, it uses this field to select the appropriate version handling. When the Protocol Version is unsupported and the Server rejects the connection, it MAY send a CONNACK packet with Reason Code 0x84 (Unsupported Protocol Version) and MUST subsequently close the Network Connection. The Client must set this field before initiating the connection request, and the Server must validate it upon receiving the CONNECT packet.

During the CONNECT packet processing phase, when a Server supports multiple MQTT protocol versions, it MUST examine the Protocol Version byte in the variable header to identify which specific MQTT version the Client intends to use. This determination governs subsequent protocol behavior and version-specific feature handling. If the Server does not support the specified protocol version, it MAY respond with a CONNACK packet containing Reason Code 0x84 (Unsupported Protocol Version) and MUST then terminate the Network Connection. This rule applies immediately upon receiving the CONNECT packet and requires that the Protocol Version field be properly formatted as a one-byte unsigned value.

During the CONNECT packet processing in the MQTT v5 protocol, the server must validate that bit 0 (the reserved flag) of the Connect Flags byte is set to 0, as this bit is reserved for future use and must not be used in the current protocol version. If this reserved flag contains any value other than 0, the packet is malformed and the server must close the connection with an appropriate error code as specified in section 4.13. This validation occurs immediately after receiving the CONNECT packet's variable header, before any further connection setup processing.

In the CONNECT packet's Connect Flags (bits 4 and 3), the Will QoS field specifies the Quality of Service level for publishing the Will Message. When the Will Flag is set to 0, indicating no Will Message is present, the Will QoS field MUST be set to 0 (0x00) to maintain protocol consistency [MQTT-3.1.2-11]. This validation occurs during CONNECT packet processing, and non-compliance results in a Malformed Packet error as specified in section 4.13.

The Connect Flags byte in the CONNECT packet's Variable Header contains multiple bit-level parameters that define the operational behavior of the MQTT connection and indicate which optional fields are present in the Payload. This rule applies during the initial connection establishment phase when the client sends a CONNECT packet to the server. The server MUST validate that bit 0 (the reserved flag) is set to 0; if this bit is not 0, the packet is considered malformed and the server must close the connection as specified in section 4.13. This validation is a prerequisite for processing any other connection parameters and ensures protocol compatibility between client and server implementations.

During the processing of a CONNECT packet, the server MUST validate that bit 0 of the Connect Flags byte (the reserved flag) is set to 0, as this bit is reserved for future use and must not be used in MQTT version 5. This validation occurs immediately upon receiving the CONNECT packet, before establishing any connection state. If the reserved flag is not 0, the server MUST treat this as a Malformed Packet and close the network connection without performing any further processing of the packet, as specified in section 4.13 for protocol error handling.

During the connection establishment phase, when a Client sends a CONNECT packet, the Server MUST first validate the Protocol Version field in the variable header. If the Protocol Version is not 5 (indicating a version other than MQTT v5) and the Server is configured not to accept connections using that version, the Server MAY respond with a CONNACK packet containing Reason Code 0x84 (Unsupported Protocol Version) to inform the Client of the version mismatch. Following this, the Server MUST terminate the Network Connection to prevent further communication on an unsupported protocol version. This rule applies specifically when the Server supports MQTT v5 but chooses not to accept other protocol versions, ensuring clear version negotiation and secure connection handling.

The Connect Flags byte in the CONNECT packet's Variable Header specifies various connection parameters and additionally determines which optional fields are present in the Payload. This rule applies during connection establishment when the client sends a CONNECT packet, and the server must parse the Connect Flags to correctly interpret the Payload structure. The expected outcome is that both client and server consistently identify the presence or absence of fields such as Will Properties, User Name, and Password based on the corresponding flag bits. This interpretation must occur only after validating that the reserved flag (bit 0) is set to 0, as required by [MQTT-3.1.2-3]; otherwise, the packet is malformed and should be handled per section 4.13 error procedures.

The MQTT Control Packet type field, located at byte 1 bits 7-4 as a 4-bit unsigned value, defines the fundamental operation type of each MQTT packet. This rule applies to every MQTT control packet exchanged during protocol communication and governs how endpoints interpret and process incoming packets based on their type. 

The expected outcome is proper packet identification and handling according to the specified directionality: client-to-server packets (CONNECT, SUBSCRIBE, etc.) initiate operations, while server-to-client packets (CONNACK, SUBACK, etc.) provide responses, with bidirectional packets (PUBLISH, DISCONNECT, etc.) supporting message flow in both directions. 

Prerequisites include valid MQTT packet structure and proper session state. The Reserved type (value 0) must never be used, and all implementations must reject packets with this value. Each packet type corresponds to specific protocol flows - for example, QoS 1/2 PUBLISH sequences require the appropriate PUBACK/PUBREC/PUBREL/PUBCOMP exchanges, while connection management uses CONNECT/CONNACK and PINGREQ/PINGRESP pairs for keep-alive functionality.

The Will QoS field, located in bits 4 and 3 of the CONNECT packet's Connect Flags, determines the Quality of Service level for publishing the Will Message when the client connection is unexpectedly terminated. This rule applies during connection establishment when the Will Flag is enabled. If the Will Flag is 0, Will QoS must be 0; if the Will Flag is 1, valid values are 0, 1, or 2 (representing QoS 0, 1, and 2 respectively). A value of 3 is invalid and results in a Malformed Packet error, requiring the connection to be closed as specified in section 4.13. The selected QoS level governs the delivery assurance for the Will Message publication.

The Protocol Version field in the CONNECT packet variable header is a one-byte unsigned value that identifies the MQTT protocol revision level. For MQTT protocol version 5.0, this field MUST be set to the value 5 (0x05). This rule applies during the connection establishment phase when a Client sends a CONNECT packet to initiate a session with the Server. The Server uses this value to determine protocol compatibility and version-specific behavior. If the Server supports MQTT v5.0 and receives the correct protocol version value, it proceeds with v5.0 protocol processing. If the Server does not support the provided protocol version, it MAY respond with a CONNACK packet containing Reason Code 0x84 (Unsupported Protocol Version) and MUST close the network connection. This field validation occurs immediately upon receiving the CONNECT packet before any other protocol processing.

When the Will Flag is set to 1 in the CONNECT packet's Connect Flags (bits 4 and 3), the Will QoS field specifies the Quality of Service level for publishing the Will Message upon unexpected client disconnection. The valid Will QoS values are 0 (at most once), 1 (at least once), or 2 (exactly once). Any other value, particularly 3 (0x03), constitutes a Malformed Packet and must be handled as a protocol error as specified in section 4.13. This rule applies during the connection establishment phase when the client includes a Will Message in its CONNECT request.

During the connection establishment phase when processing a CONNECT packet, if the Clean Start flag is set to 0 (indicating the client wishes to resume an existing session) and the server has a valid session state associated with the Client Identifier, the server MUST resume communications using the stored session state, including restoring subscriptions and undelivered messages, rather than creating a new session. This ensures session continuity when clients reconnect after temporary disconnections.

When a client sends a CONNECT packet with the Clean Start flag set to 1, the server MUST discard any existing session and establish a new session. As a direct consequence of this clean session initialization, the Session Present flag in the server's CONNACK response MUST always be set to 0, indicating that no previous session state is being resumed. This behavior ensures that both client and server operate with a clean session state when Clean Start is requested, regardless of any prior session existence.

During the CONNECT packet processing, the User Name Flag (bit 7 of the Connect Flags) determines whether authentication credentials are included. If this flag is set to 0, indicating no user name is provided, the payload MUST NOT contain a User Name field. Conversely, if the flag is set to 1, a User Name MUST be present. This rule applies when parsing the CONNECT packet variable header and payload, and non-compliance MUST result in a protocol violation, causing the connection to be rejected.

During the connection establishment phase when processing a CONNECT packet, if the Clean Start flag (bit 1 of the Connect Flags byte) is set to 1, both the Client and Server MUST immediately discard any existing Session State associated with the Client Identifier and initialize a completely new Session. This rule applies regardless of whether a previous Session existed, and as a direct consequence, the Session Present flag in the subsequent CONNACK packet MUST always be set to 0. The Clean Start flag specifically governs session continuity decisions and takes precedence over any existing session state when establishing the MQTT connection.

This rule governs session establishment behavior when a client requests to resume a previous session but no session exists. It applies during the CONNECT packet processing phase when: (1) the Clean Start flag is set to 0 (indicating the client wants to resume an existing session), and (2) the server has no stored session state for the provided Client Identifier. Under these conditions, the server MUST create a new session rather than returning an error, ensuring the connection can proceed while maintaining the client's intent to establish persistent session state. This behavior enables robust session recovery scenarios where clients may reconnect after server restarts or session expiration events.

The Clean Start flag is located at bit 1 of the Connect Flags byte in the CONNECT packet variable header. This flag determines whether the connection initiates a new session or continues an existing one. When Clean Start is set to 1, both client and server must discard any existing session and establish a new session, resulting in the Session Present flag in CONNACK always being 0. When Clean Start is set to 0, the server must resume the existing session if one exists for the client identifier, or create a new session if none exists. This rule applies during the connection establishment phase and governs session persistence behavior across connections.

The Clean Start flag (bit 1 of the Connect Flags byte) determines whether a connection initiates a new session or continues an existing one. This rule applies during the CONNECT packet processing phase when establishing a connection. When Clean Start is set to 1, both client and server must discard any existing session state (as defined in section 4.1) and begin a new session, resulting in the Session Present flag in CONNACK always being 0. When Clean Start is 0 and a session exists for the Client Identifier, the server must resume the existing session; if no session exists, the server must create a new one. The prerequisite is that the client must correctly set the Clean Start flag based on whether it intends to start fresh or resume previous communications.

During the CONNECT packet processing, the User Name Flag (bit 7 of the Connect Flags) determines whether authentication credentials are provided. When this flag is set to 1, the protocol requires that a User Name field MUST be included in the Payload section of the CONNECT packet. This rule applies during the initial connection establishment phase and ensures proper authentication handshake between client and broker. If the flag is set to 1 but no User Name is present, the connection MUST be rejected as malformed. This requirement only applies when the client chooses to provide authentication credentials and sets the corresponding flag. [MQTT-3.1.2-17]

During the CONNECT packet processing phase, if the Server identifies the protocol name as "MQTT" but determines that the protocol version or other connection parameters are incompatible and chooses to disclose its MQTT Server identity, the Server MAY respond with a CONNACK packet containing Reason Code 0x84 (Unsupported Protocol Version). Following this response, the Server MUST immediately terminate the Network Connection to ensure proper protocol state management and prevent further invalid communication attempts. This behavior applies specifically when the Server validates the protocol name but rejects the connection due to version incompatibility while maintaining protocol transparency.

During the connection establishment phase when processing the CONNECT packet's variable header, a Server that supports multiple protocols MUST examine the Protocol Name field to identify MQTT traffic. The Protocol Name MUST be exactly the UTF-8 encoded string "MQTT" (capitalized as shown). If the Protocol Name matches "MQTT", the Server proceeds with MQTT protocol processing; if it does not match, and the Server wishes to indicate it supports MQTT, it MAY send a CONNACK packet with Reason Code 0x84 (Unsupported Protocol Version) before closing the network connection. This identification mechanism enables proper protocol discrimination and allows network infrastructure components to recognize MQTT traffic.

In MQTT v5, each property within a Control Packet's variable header consists of an Identifier (encoded as a Variable Byte Integer) and a corresponding value. This rule governs the validation of property Identifiers and their associated values against the protocol specification. It applies during packet parsing when a Control Packet is received, before any semantic processing occurs. A Control Packet must be considered a Malformed Packet if it contains either: (1) a property Identifier that is not defined as valid for that specific packet type according to Table 2-4, or (2) a property value that does not conform to the data type specified for that Identifier. When this rule is violated, the receiver must respond with a CONNACK or DISCONNECT packet containing Reason Code 0x81 (Malformed Packet) as specified in section 4.13, and must not process the malformed packet further. This validation is prerequisite to any property-based processing and ensures structural integrity of Control Packets.

When processing MQTT v5 packets, if a received packet contains properties with invalid identifiers for its packet type or values that do not match the specified data types, it must be considered a Malformed Packet. Upon detecting such a malformed packet, the receiving implementation MUST respond by sending either a CONNACK packet (during connection establishment) or a DISCONNECT packet (during an active session) with Reason Code 0x81 (Malformed Packet), following the error handling procedures detailed in section 4.13. This rule applies specifically to property validation during packet parsing and ensures consistent error reporting across implementations while maintaining protocol integrity.
req_type: any MQTT v5 packet with properties

rule: When a Control Packet contains multiple Properties in its Variable Header, the order in which Properties with different Identifiers appear has no semantic meaning. This rule applies during both packet construction and parsing, meaning implementations MUST NOT depend on any specific ordering of Properties with distinct Identifiers. The expected behavior is that all Properties MUST be processed regardless of their position in the Properties list, and receivers MUST NOT interpret Property order as carrying any protocol meaning. This rule only applies when all Properties are valid for the packet type and correctly formatted according to their data type specifications; if any Property is invalid or malformed, the packet MUST be treated as a Malformed Packet according to section 4.13 error handling procedures.

During the CONNECT packet processing, the Will Retain flag (bit 5 of the Connect Flags) determines whether the Will Message should be published as retained. If the client indicates no Will Message exists by setting the Will Flag to 0, then the Will Retain flag MUST be set to 0 as it becomes irrelevant. This ensures protocol consistency by preventing invalid flag combinations when the Will Message feature is disabled. [MQTT-3.1.2-13]

The Password Flag is located at bit 6 of the Connect Flags in the CONNECT packet variable header. This flag governs the presence of password data in the payload: when set to 0, no password field is permitted in the payload; when set to 1, a password field must be included. This rule applies during the connection establishment phase when a client sends a CONNECT packet to a server. Unlike MQTT v3.1.1, this version allows sending a password without a user name, accommodating various authentication credentials beyond traditional passwords.

When processing a CONNECT packet, if the Will Flag (bit 2 of the Connect Flags) is set to 1 indicating the presence of a Will Message, and the Will Retain flag (bit 5) is also set to 1, the Server MUST publish the Will Message as a retained message upon detecting an abnormal connection termination. This ensures that the Will Message persists on the topic and is delivered to future subscribers, but only applies after successful connection establishment and subsequent ungraceful disconnection. The Server MUST NOT apply this retention behavior if the Will Flag is 0, regardless of the Will Retain flag value [MQTT-3.1.2-15].

When a client establishes a connection with the Will Flag enabled (set to 1) in the CONNECT packet, and the Will Retain flag is explicitly set to 0, the Server MUST publish the Will Message as a non-retained message upon detecting an abnormal connection termination. This ensures that the Will Message is delivered only to current subscribers at the time of publication and is not stored by the broker for future subscribers. This rule applies specifically during the handling of the client's Will Message publication triggered by connection loss, and requires that both the Will Flag is enabled and the Will Retain flag is disabled as prerequisites. [MQTT-3.1.2-14]

During the CONNECT packet processing, the Password Flag (bit 6 of the Connect Flags) determines whether authentication credentials are included. If the Password Flag is set to 0, indicating no password is provided, the Payload MUST NOT contain a Password field [MQTT-3.1.2-18]. This rule applies when parsing the CONNECT packet variable header and payload, and violation constitutes a protocol error. Note that MQTT v5 permits sending a Password without a User Name, unlike previous versions, as passwords may represent various authentication tokens beyond traditional user credentials.

The Password Flag (bit 6 of Connect Flags) controls password presence in the CONNECT payload. When set to 0, no password is permitted; when set to 1, a password must be included. Unlike MQTT v3.1.1, this version permits sending a password without a corresponding User Name, reflecting expanded use cases where passwords may serve as standalone credentials (e.g., API keys or tokens) rather than traditional username-password pairs. This flexibility applies during CONNECT packet construction and validation, provided the Password Flag is properly set to indicate password presence.

During the CONNECT packet processing, the Password Flag (bit 6 of the Connect Flags) determines password field presence in the payload. When this flag is set to 1, the payload MUST contain a Password field, and the server MUST treat its absence as a protocol violation [MQTT-3.1.2-19]. Conversely, when the flag is set to 0, the payload MUST NOT contain any Password field [MQTT-3.1.2-18]. Note that MQTT v5 permits sending a Password without a User Name, unlike MQTT v3.1.1, reflecting broader credential usage patterns.

Table 2-4 defines the valid Properties for MQTT v5 packets, specifying each Property's identifier, name, data type, and applicable packet types. This rule applies when constructing or parsing any MQTT v5 control packet that includes Properties in its variable header. The expected outcome is that implementations must use only Properties valid for the specific packet type and ensure values match the specified data types. Prerequisites include understanding that Properties consist of a Variable Byte Integer identifier followed by a value, and that while identifiers are encoded as Variable Byte Integers, all current Property identifiers are one byte long. Any packet containing an invalid Property identifier for its type or a mismatched data type constitutes a Malformed Packet, requiring error handling with CONNACK or DISCONNECT packets using Reason Code 0x81.
req_type: any MQTT v5 control packet that includes Properties

rule: The Password Flag (bit 6 of the Connect Flags) controls whether a Password field is included in the CONNECT packet payload. When set to 1, a Password MUST be present; when set to 0, no Password is permitted. Unlike MQTT v3.1.1, this version allows sending a Password without a User Name. This flexibility accommodates common implementations where the Password field carries authentication credentials beyond traditional passwords, such as tokens, keys, or other security data. The rule applies during CONNECT packet construction and validation, ensuring proper handling of authentication payloads while maintaining protocol compliance.

The Properties field in the CONNECT packet's Variable Header must be preceded by its length, encoded as a Variable Byte Integer according to the MQTT v5 specification. This rule applies during the initial connection establishment phase when a client sends a CONNECT packet to a broker. The encoded length must accurately represent the total byte count of all subsequent properties, enabling the receiver to correctly parse the property data. This encoding must follow the Variable Byte Integer format defined in the MQTT v5 specification, where the length is represented using 1 to 4 bytes with continuation bits.

When constructing a PUBLISH packet with QoS level 0 (fire-and-forget delivery), the packet MUST NOT include a Packet Identifier in its variable header. This rule applies specifically to QoS 0 PUBLISH packets where message delivery is best-effort without acknowledgement requirements. The expected outcome is reduced packet overhead and simplified processing, as QoS 0 messages don't require the tracking mechanisms that Packet Identifiers provide for reliable message delivery. This requirement is conditional on the QoS field being set to 0 and applies to both Client and Server implementations when sending PUBLISH packets. For QoS 1 and QoS 2 PUBLISH packets, a Packet Identifier MUST be included as specified in [MQTT-2.2.1-3] and [MQTT-2.2.1-4].

Reason Codes in MQTT v5 are one-byte unsigned values that indicate the outcome of protocol operations, where values less than 0x80 (0-127) signify successful completion. This rule applies when processing CONNACK, PUBACK, PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP, DISCONNECT, AUTH, SUBACK, and UNSUBACK packets, where Reason Codes appear either in the Variable Header or Payload. When a Reason Code below 0x80 is received, the client or server should proceed with normal protocol flow, as the operation has succeeded. The standard success code is 0, while other values below 0x80 may indicate specific successful states. This interpretation requires that the packet type supports Reason Codes and that the Reason Code value is valid per the protocol specification table.
res_type: CONNACK, PUBACK, PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP, DISCONNECT, AUTH, SUBACK, UNSUBACK

rule: Reason Code values of 0x80 (128 in decimal) or greater indicate a failed operation, while values below 0x80 indicate success (with 0 being the standard success code). This rule applies to CONNACK, PUBACK, PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP, DISCONNECT, and AUTH packets in their Variable Headers, and to SUBACK and UNSUBACK packets where multiple Reason Codes may appear in the Payload. When a client or server receives a Reason Code ≥ 0x80, it must handle the failure according to the specific code—for example, retrying the operation, resetting the session, or diagnosing implementation issues—as defined in the protocol's Reason Code table.
res_type: CONNACK, PUBACK, PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP, DISCONNECT, AUTH, SUBACK, UNSUBACK

rule: The CONNACK, PUBACK, PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP, DISCONNECT, and AUTH control packets must each include exactly one Reason Code byte within their Variable Header, which indicates the success or failure outcome of the corresponding operation. This rule applies when these packets are sent in response to connection establishment, message publication flows, session termination, or authentication exchanges. The Reason Code value (0x00-0x7F for success, 0x80-0xFF for failure) provides standardized feedback about the operation result, enabling proper protocol state transitions and error handling. This requirement does not apply to SUBACK and UNSUBACK packets, which instead carry multiple Reason Codes in their payload to indicate subscription-specific outcomes.
res_type: CONNACK, PUBACK, PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP, DISCONNECT, AUTH

rule: A Wildcard Subscription is a Subscription where the Topic Filter contains one or more wildcard characters (either '+' for single-level matching or '#' for multi-level matching). This subscription type applies when a Client wants to subscribe to multiple topics using pattern matching rather than exact topic names. The Server MUST match incoming Application Messages against these wildcard patterns and deliver messages where the Topic Name matches the pattern. Prerequisites include valid wildcard placement according to MQTT topic structure rules, and the subscription becomes active once the Server processes the SUBSCRIBE packet. The expected outcome is that the Client receives all Application Messages published to topics matching the wildcard pattern, subject to the subscription's QoS level.

Reason Codes in MQTT v5 use a standardized set of values to indicate operation outcomes across multiple control packets. These codes apply during the processing of CONNACK, PUBACK, PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP, DISCONNECT, AUTH, SUBACK, and UNSUBACK packets, where they communicate success (values below 0x80) or failure (values 0x80 and above). The expected outcome is that clients and servers interpret these codes consistently to determine subsequent actions—for example, retrying operations, resetting sessions, or handling errors. This rule applies once a control packet containing a Reason Code is received, and it requires that both communicating parties support MQTT v5 and adhere to the defined code meanings as specified in the protocol's common value table.
res_type: CONNACK, PUBACK, PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP, DISCONNECT, AUTH, SUBACK, UNSUBACK

rule: A Subscription is a client's request to receive Application Messages matching a specified Topic Filter, comprising both the Topic Filter expression and a maximum QoS level that defines the highest Quality of Service at which the Server can send matching messages. This rule applies when a Client sends a SUBSCRIBE packet to establish message filtering preferences during an active Session. The expected outcome is that the Server will forward Application Messages whose Topic Names match the Subscription's Topic Filter, using a QoS level not exceeding the specified maximum QoS. Prerequisites include an established Network Connection between Client and Server, an active Session, and the Client having appropriate authorization to subscribe to the specified Topic Filter. Each Subscription is uniquely associated with a single Session and must have a distinct Topic Filter within that Session.

In the MQTT v5 protocol, SUBACK and UNSUBACK packets must include a payload containing a list of one or more Reason Codes, where each code corresponds to the subscription or unsubscription result for a specific topic filter requested in the preceding SUBSCRIBE or UNSUBSCRIBE packet. This rule applies after a client sends a SUBSCRIBE or UNSUBSCRIBE packet containing one or more topic filters, and the server responds with the corresponding acknowledgment packet. Each Reason Code in the list must be a single byte value indicating success (values 0x00-0x7F) or failure (values 0x80-0xFF) for the respective topic filter, maintaining the same order as the requests in the original packet. The number of Reason Codes must match the number of topic filters in the request, and the client must process these codes to determine the outcome of each individual subscription operation.

Reason Codes are one-byte unsigned values used in MQTT v5 to indicate the outcome of protocol operations, where values below 0x80 denote success and values 0x80 or higher indicate failure. This rule applies when processing CONNACK, PUBACK, PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP, DISCONNECT, AUTH, SUBACK, and UNSUBACK packets, with the latter two containing multiple Reason Codes in their payloads. Table 2-6 provides the standardized mapping of Reason Code values (from 0 to 162) to their decimal/hexadecimal representations, descriptive names, and applicable packet types, ensuring consistent interpretation across implementations. Developers must reference this table to correctly handle responses, such as retrying operations or resetting sessions for specific failure codes like 0x91 (Packet identifier in use).
res_type: CONNACK, PUBACK, PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP, DISCONNECT, AUTH, SUBACK, UNSUBACK

rule: The Reason Code table defines standardized result codes for MQTT v5 operations, spanning values from 0 (Success) to 162 (Wildcard Subscriptions not supported), with each code explicitly mapped to the specific control packets where it is valid. This rule governs how operations are acknowledged and errors are reported throughout the protocol lifecycle. It applies when processing CONNACK, PUBACK, PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP, DISCONNECT, AUTH, SUBACK, and UNSUBACK packets - with SUBACK and UNSUBACK containing multiple Reason Codes in their payloads. The expected outcome is that implementations will use these standardized codes to consistently indicate operation success (codes < 0x80) or failure (codes ≥ 0x80), enabling interoperable error handling and recovery. Prerequisites include proper packet formatting and session state management, as some codes (like 0x91 - Packet identifier in use) require specific recovery actions such as session reset or implementation validation.
res_type: CONNACK, PUBACK, PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP, DISCONNECT, AUTH, SUBACK, UNSUBACK

rule: Topic Name: A string label that MUST be attached to every Application Message when published by a Client. This label is used by the Server during message forwarding to match against the Topic Filters in active Subscriptions. The matching process determines which subscribed Clients will receive the Application Message. Topic Names MUST conform to the syntax rules defined in section 4.7 and MUST NOT contain wildcard characters. The matching occurs after a Client successfully publishes an Application Message and before the Server forwards it to interested subscribers.

The Packet Identifier is a two-byte integer field used in MQTT control packets to uniquely identify messages and commands during protocol exchanges. This rule governs which packet types require a Packet Identifier, how identifiers are assigned and managed, and the conditions for their reuse.

The rule applies during the following protocol flows:
- When sending QoS 1 or 2 PUBLISH packets for message delivery tracking
- During SUBSCRIBE/UNSUBSCRIBE operations for subscription management
- In PUBACK, PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP, SUBACK, and UNSUBACK packets for correlating responses

Expected outcomes when following this rule:
- QoS 0 PUBLISH packets never contain Packet Identifiers
- Each new SUBSCRIBE, UNSUBSCRIBE, or QoS > 0 PUBLISH packet gets a unique non-zero identifier
- Response packets (PUBACK, PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP, SUBACK, UNSUBACK) echo the same identifier as their corresponding request
- Packet Identifiers become available for reuse only after processing the appropriate acknowledgement

Prerequisites and conditions:
- Client and Server maintain independent identifier spaces within a session
- Identifiers must be unique and non-zero when assigned
- QoS level determines Packet Identifier requirement for PUBLISH packets
- Identifiers can be reused only after the corresponding acknowledgement is processed (PUBACK for QoS 1, PUBCOMP or PUBREC with error for QoS 2, SUBACK/UNSUBACK for subscription operations)

This ensures reliable message delivery, proper command-response correlation, and prevents identifier conflicts in concurrent MQTT operations.
res_type: PUBACK, PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP, SUBACK, UNSUBACK

rule: A Topic Filter MAY include wildcard characters when used in Subscription requests to indicate interest in multiple topics matching a pattern. This applies during the SUBSCRIBE packet processing phase, where the Server MUST interpret wildcards according to the rules defined in section 4.7. The expected outcome is that the Subscription will match all Topic Names that satisfy the wildcard pattern, enabling efficient topic-based message routing. This capability requires that the Topic Filter conforms to the wildcard syntax rules and is used within a valid Session context.

When processing MQTT Control Packets, all text fields are encoded as UTF-8 strings prefixed by a two-byte length field. If a Client or Server receives any MQTT Control Packet containing UTF-8 encoded character data that is ill-formed according to the Unicode specification (RFC 3629), including invalid byte sequences, overlong encodings, or code points between U+D800 and U+DFFF, the entire packet MUST be considered a Malformed Packet. This rule applies during packet parsing and validation, and upon detection, the receiver MUST follow the error handling procedures defined in Section 4.13, which may include closing the network connection.

A Wildcard Subscription is a Subscription whose Topic Filter contains one or more wildcard characters (either '+' or '#') as defined in section 4.7. This subscription type enables a single subscription to match multiple Topic Names that conform to the wildcard pattern, allowing clients to receive Application Messages published to various topics without requiring individual subscriptions for each topic. The rule applies during the subscription processing phase when a Client sends a SUBSCRIBE packet to the Server, and the Server must evaluate incoming PUBLISH packets against all active subscriptions, including wildcard subscriptions, to determine message routing. For this matching to occur correctly, the Topic Filter must be syntactically valid according to the wildcard rules specified in section 4.7, and the Session must be active. When properly implemented, the Server will deliver any Application Message whose Topic Name matches the wildcard pattern to the subscribing Client, subject to the subscription's maximum QoS setting.

When processing MQTT Control Packets that contain UTF-8 encoded string fields, if a receiver (Server or Client) encounters the null character U+0000 anywhere within the UTF-8 encoded character data, the entire packet MUST be considered a Malformed Packet. This rule applies during the packet parsing phase before any protocol-specific processing occurs and requires that the receiver validate all UTF-8 string content against the Unicode specification. The expected outcome is that the receiver MUST close the network connection as specified in section 4.13, as the presence of U+0000 violates the requirement for well-formed UTF-8 strings in MQTT protocol data exchange.

When the Will Flag is set to 1 in the CONNECT packet, indicating that a Will Message must be stored by the server, the Will Message is defined by three mandatory fields in the CONNECT Payload: Will Properties, Will Topic, and Will Payload. These fields must be present and collectively form the complete Will Message that will be published under specified abnormal disconnection conditions, such as network failure or Keep Alive timeout, after the Will Delay Interval has elapsed or the session ends, unless overridden by a normal disconnection.

When processing UTF-8 encoded strings in MQTT Control Packets, the byte sequence 0xEF 0xBB 0xBF MUST always be interpreted as the Unicode character U+FEFF ("ZERO WIDTH NO-BREAK SPACE") and MUST be preserved as part of the string content. This rule applies during packet reception when parsing any UTF-8 encoded string field, regardless of its position within the string. Packet receivers MUST NOT skip, strip, or otherwise remove this sequence during processing. This requirement ensures consistent interpretation of string content across implementations and prevents unintended modification of transmitted data. The rule is specifically identified as MQTT-1.5.4-3 and applies to all MQTT Control Packets containing UTF-8 encoded strings, provided the strings are well-formed UTF-8 and do not contain other prohibited characters as defined in the specification.
req_type: all MQTT Control Packets containing UTF-8 encoded strings

rule: When the Will Flag is set to 1 in the CONNECT packet, the Server MUST store a Will Message associated with the client session. This Will Message MUST be published after the Network Connection is closed and either the Will Delay Interval has elapsed or the Session ends, unless deleted by a normal DISCONNECT (Reason Code 0x00) or overridden by a new connection. The rule specifies that publication occurs in scenarios where the Server detects abnormal termination conditions, including but not limited to: I/O errors, network failures, client Keep Alive timeouts, or ungraceful connection closures without proper DISCONNECT packets. This ensures that subscribers are notified of the client's unexpected disconnection according to the predefined Will Message content.

During the CONNECT packet processing phase, when the Will Flag (bit 2 of the Connect Flags) is set to 1, the client signals its intent to establish a Will Message mechanism. This requires the server to store the Will Message—comprising Will Properties, Will Topic, and Will Payload fields—and associate it with the current session. The Will Message must be retained until either it is published upon an ungraceful connection termination (such as network failure, keep-alive timeout, or abrupt closure without a DISCONNECT packet with Reason Code 0x00) after the Will Delay Interval elapses or the session ends, or it is deleted if the client disconnects normally or reconnects before the Will Delay Interval expires. This rule ensures reliable last-will notification in case of unexpected client disconnection.

When processing MQTT Control Packets containing UTF-8 encoded strings, if a receiver (Server or Client) encounters any of the disallowed Unicode code points (including U+0001 to U+001F control characters, U+007F to U+009F control characters, Unicode non-characters such as U+0FFFF, and the UTF-8 sequence 0xEF 0xBB 0xBF representing U+FEFF), the receiver MAY treat the packet as a Malformed Packet. This rule applies during packet parsing after verifying the string length prefix and UTF-8 structural validity, but before processing the packet's semantic content. The expected outcome is that receivers have discretion to reject packets containing these code points, though they are not required to do so. This validation occurs as part of the broader UTF-8 compliance checking defined in section 1.5.4, which mandates that all UTF-8 strings must be well-formed and must not contain null characters (U+0000) or surrogate pairs (U+D800 to U+DFFF).

When the Will Flag is set to 1 in the CONNECT packet, the Server MUST publish the stored Will Message if the Network Connection is closed without first receiving a DISCONNECT packet with Reason Code 0x00 (Normal disconnection). This applies to scenarios where the Server initiates connection closure due to protocol violations, internal errors, or other abnormal conditions. The Will Message publication occurs after the Network Connection closure and the expiration of the Will Delay Interval, unless the Session ends earlier. This ensures that subscribers are notified of the client's unexpected disconnection, maintaining the expected quality of service behavior for Will Messages.

When the Will Flag is set to 1 in the CONNECT packet, the Server must store a Will Message and publish it after the Network Connection is closed and the Will Delay Interval has elapsed, unless the Will Message is deleted by the Server upon receiving a DISCONNECT packet with Reason Code 0x00 (Normal disconnection). This rule specifically applies when the Client terminates the Network Connection without first sending a DISCONNECT packet with Reason Code 0x00. In such cases, the Server must publish the stored Will Message after the connection closure and expiration of the Will Delay Interval, unless a new Network Connection for the same ClientID is established before the interval elapses. The expected outcome is that the Will Message is delivered to notify other clients of the abnormal disconnection, while sending DISCONNECT with Reason Code 0x00 prevents this publication.

When the Will Flag (bit 2 of the Connect Flags) is set to 1 during the CONNECT packet transmission, indicating that a Will Message must be stored on the server for the session, the Will Properties, Will Topic, and Will Payload fields MUST be included in the CONNECT packet payload. This requirement ensures that all necessary components for the Will Message are provided when the client requests its storage, enabling the server to properly handle the message publication in cases of unexpected client disconnection, such as network failures, keep-alive timeouts, or abnormal connection closures.

The Receive Maximum property (identifier 33/0x21) in the CONNECT packet variable header specifies the maximum number of concurrent QoS 1 and QoS 2 publications the client can handle. This property must appear at most once in the CONNECT packet properties. If present, the two-byte integer value must be greater than 0. Including this property multiple times or specifying a value of 0 constitutes a Protocol Error, requiring the connection to be closed. When absent, the value defaults to 65,535. This flow control mechanism applies only to the current network connection and does not restrict QoS 0 publications.

When the Will Flag is set to 1 in the CONNECT packet, the Server stores a Will Message that MUST be published after the Network Connection closes and either the Will Delay Interval elapses or the Session ends, unless deleted by normal disconnection or a new connection. In the event of Server shutdown or failure, publication of Will Messages MAY be deferred until Server restart, potentially causing a delay between the failure occurrence and actual Will Message publication. This applies specifically to abnormal Server termination scenarios and does not affect the requirement for prompt publication under normal operating conditions once the Will Delay Interval has passed or the Session has ended.

The Client MUST use the Receive Maximum property value (if present) to limit the number of concurrent QoS 1 and QoS 2 publications it is willing to process from the Server. This rule applies during the connection establishment phase when the Client includes the Receive Maximum property in the CONNECT packet, and governs flow control throughout the session. When followed, the Server MUST NOT exceed the specified limit of unacknowledged QoS 1 or QoS 2 publications, preventing Client resource exhaustion. This rule applies only if the Client includes a valid Receive Maximum value (1-65,535) in the CONNECT packet; if absent, the default value of 65,535 applies. Note that this limit does not apply to QoS 0 publications and is valid only for the current network connection.

When a client establishes a connection with the Will Flag set to 1, the server must store the Will Message as part of the session state. This rule governs the cleanup of the stored Will Message and applies in two specific scenarios: (1) after the Will Message has been successfully published to subscribers, or (2) when the server receives a DISCONNECT packet with Reason Code 0x00 (Normal disconnection) from the client. The expected outcome is that the server immediately removes the Will Message from the session state in either case, ensuring it will not be published again. This cleanup is mandatory and occurs regardless of whether the Will Message was triggered by abnormal disconnection scenarios (such as network failures or keep-alive timeouts) or prevented by a normal disconnection. The prerequisite for this rule is that a valid CONNECT packet with Will Flag set to 1 was previously processed and the Will Message was stored in the session state. [MQTT-3.1.2-10]

The Request Response Information property (identifier 0x19) in the CONNECT packet variable header is followed by a single byte value that must be either 0 or 1. When this property is not included by the Client in the CONNECT packet, the Server MUST interpret this as equivalent to a value of 0. A value of 0 explicitly instructs the Server that it MUST NOT include Response Information in the CONNACK packet [MQTT-3.1.2-28]. This rule applies during the connection establishment phase when processing the CONNECT packet properties, and the absence of this property triggers the default behavior of value 0, ensuring backward compatibility and consistent protocol handling.

The Request Response Information property (identifier 25/0x19) in the CONNECT packet variable header allows the Client to request whether the Server should include Response Information in the CONNACK packet. This property consists of the identifier byte followed by a single byte value that MUST be either 0 or 1. The Server MUST treat it as a Protocol Error if this property appears more than once or contains any value other than 0 or 1. When absent, the property defaults to value 0. A value of 0 explicitly prohibits the Server from including Response Information in the CONNACK [MQTT-3.1.2-28], while a value of 1 permits but does not require the Server to include it. The Server maintains discretion to omit Response Information even when requested by the Client. This property applies during the connection establishment phase and governs the optional inclusion of additional response metadata as detailed in section 4.10.

The Request Response Information property (identifier 0x19) in the CONNECT packet's variable header MUST be included at most once and MUST have a value of either 0 or 1. This rule applies during the connection establishment phase when the Client sends the CONNECT packet to the Server. If the property is absent, the Server MUST treat it as having a value of 0. A value of 0 indicates that the Server MUST NOT include Response Information in the CONNACK packet [MQTT-3.1.2-28], while a value of 1 indicates that the Server MAY optionally include Response Information. Violating this rule by including the property multiple times or with an invalid value constitutes a Protocol Error, requiring the Server to close the connection.

When the Client includes a Request Response Information property with value 1 in the CONNECT packet, the Server MAY optionally include Response Information in the CONNACK packet. This applies during the connection establishment phase after the Server validates the CONNECT packet. The Server is not required to provide Response Information even when requested, and the absence of this property defaults to value 0, which prohibits the Server from including Response Information. This rule governs the optional exchange of additional response context between Client and Server, supporting request/response patterns as defined in section 4.10.

In the MQTT v5 CONNECT packet's variable header properties, the Request Response Information property (identifier 0x19) is followed by a single byte value that must be either 0 or 1. When the Client sets this value to 0 (either explicitly or implicitly when the property is absent, as absence defaults to 0), the Server is explicitly prohibited from including any Response Information in the subsequent CONNACK packet [MQTT-3.1.2-28]. This rule applies during the connection establishment phase when processing the CONNECT packet, and ensures that the Server respects the Client's preference to not receive additional response data, thereby optimizing bandwidth usage when such information is not required by the Client.

The Topic Alias Maximum property in the CONNECT packet is identified by the byte value 34 (0x22), followed by a Two Byte Integer specifying the maximum Topic Alias value the Client will accept from the Server. This property governs the Server's ability to use Topic Aliases in PUBLISH packets on this connection. If absent, the default value is 0, indicating no Topic Aliases are permitted. The Server MUST NOT send a Topic Alias exceeding this value [MQTT-3.1.2-26] and MUST NOT send any Topic Aliases if the value is absent or zero [MQTT-3.1.2-27]. Including this property more than once constitutes a Protocol Error.

The Client includes the Request Response Information property in the CONNECT packet to indicate whether it requests the Server to include Response Information in the CONNACK packet. This property consists of a single byte following the identifier 0x19, which MUST be either 0 or 1. A value of 0 explicitly instructs the Server not to return Response Information, while a value of 1 permits the Server to optionally include it. The Server retains discretion to omit Response Information even when requested. This property MUST NOT appear more than once in the CONNECT properties, and any value other than 0 or 1 constitutes a Protocol Error. If the property is absent, the Server MUST treat it as equivalent to a value of 0.

In the CONNECT packet's Variable Header Properties, after the Topic Alias Maximum identifier (0x22), the next two bytes MUST contain a Two Byte Integer representing the Topic Alias Maximum value. This value specifies the maximum Topic Alias the Server is permitted to assign when publishing to this Client on the current connection. The Client uses this to control how many Topic Aliases it will store. If this property is omitted, the default value is 0, prohibiting the Server from using any Topic Aliases. The Server MUST NOT send a PUBLISH packet with a Topic Alias exceeding this specified maximum, and including this property more than once constitutes a Protocol Error.
req_fields: Two Byte Integer representing the Topic Alias Maximum value

rule: In the CONNACK packet variable header properties, if the Topic Alias Maximum property (identifier 0x22) is not included by the Server, it defaults to a value of 0. This default applies when the property is omitted entirely, and indicates that the Server will not accept any Topic Aliases from the Client on this connection. Consequently, the Client MUST NOT include any Topic Alias in PUBLISH packets sent to this Server. This rule governs the Server's capability advertisement for topic alias support during connection establishment and ensures proper protocol behavior when the property is absent.

In the CONNACK packet's variable header properties, the Topic Alias Maximum property (identifier 0x22) MUST NOT be included more than once. This rule applies when the Server is sending the CONNACK packet to the Client during the connection establishment phase. If the Server includes multiple Topic Alias Maximum properties, the Client MUST treat this as a Protocol Error and close the network connection. This rule ensures unambiguous communication of the maximum topic alias value that the Client can use in subsequent PUBLISH packets. The property value, when present, MUST be a two-byte integer indicating the highest acceptable topic alias value, with a default value of 0 if the property is absent.

During the CONNECT handshake, if the Client either omits the Topic Alias Maximum property (defaulting to 0) or explicitly sets it to 0, the Server MUST NOT include any Topic Aliases in PUBLISH packets sent to the Client for the duration of the connection. This ensures that the Client's preference to avoid topic alias processing is respected, and any attempt by the Server to use topic aliases under these conditions would constitute a protocol violation.

The Topic Alias Maximum property, when present in the CONNECT packet, specifies the upper limit for Topic Alias values that the Server may assign and use in PUBLISH packets sent to the Client over this connection. This value applies immediately upon successful connection establishment and governs all subsequent PUBLISH packets from the Server. The Server MUST NOT assign or use any Topic Alias value exceeding this maximum, and if the property is absent or set to zero, the Server MUST NOT use Topic Aliases at all. This mechanism allows the Client to control its resource allocation for storing topic alias mappings while enabling efficient topic name compression in the protocol flow.

In the AUTH packet's variable header properties, the User Property (identifier 0x26) may be included multiple times to represent distinct name-value pairs, where each occurrence consists of a UTF-8 string pair. This allows the sender to provide multiple diagnostic or informational attributes. The rule applies when constructing AUTH packets for authentication exchange, provided that including all User Properties does not cause the packet size to exceed the receiver's specified Maximum Packet Size. When followed, the recipient must process all name-value pairs, including those with duplicate names, as valid authentication-related metadata.

During the MQTT connection establishment, the Client may specify a Topic Alias Maximum value in the CONNECT packet's properties to indicate the highest Topic Alias value it is willing to accept from the Server. This rule applies when the Server sends PUBLISH packets to the Client after the connection is established. The Server MUST NOT assign or send any Topic Alias in a PUBLISH packet that exceeds the Topic Alias Maximum value provided by the Client. If the Topic Alias Maximum is absent or set to zero, the Server MUST NOT send any Topic Aliases at all. Adherence to this rule ensures that the Client is not required to manage Topic Aliases beyond its declared capacity, maintaining protocol integrity and preventing potential resource or processing errors.

In the UNSUBACK packet's variable header properties, the User Property (identifier 0x26) may contain multiple name-value pairs where the same property name can be repeated with different or identical values. This allows for representing multiple instances of diagnostic or supplementary information under the same property name. This rule applies when the Server includes User Properties in the UNSUBACK response, provided that the total packet size does not exceed the Client's specified Maximum Packet Size. When implemented, receivers must process all occurrences of the repeated property name rather than treating duplicates as errors.

In the SUBACK packet's variable header, when the User Property identifier (0x26) is present, it must be immediately followed by a UTF-8 String Pair consisting of a name and value. This property enables the server to include supplementary diagnostic or informational metadata in the subscription acknowledgement. The rule applies during SUBACK packet construction when the server includes user properties, but only if their inclusion does not cause the packet size to exceed the client-specified Maximum Packet Size. Multiple User Property entries are permitted within a single SUBACK packet, including duplicate names, to convey multiple name-value pairs. The UTF-8 String Pair must maintain proper encoding and length formatting as specified by the MQTT v5 string data type requirements.

The Authentication Data property (identifier 0x16) MUST be followed by Binary Data containing authentication information specific to the authentication method being used. This property MUST NOT appear more than once in an AUTH packet, as including it multiple times constitutes a Protocol Error. The contents and interpretation of this binary data are defined by the authentication method negotiated between client and server, and implementers should refer to section 4.12 for detailed guidance on extended authentication flows. This property applies when performing enhanced authentication exchanges and provides the necessary data to continue or complete the authentication process.

The User Property field in the CONNECT packet variable header allows clients to send custom connection-related metadata to the server as UTF-8 string name-value pairs. This rule applies during the connection establishment phase when the client sends the CONNECT packet, and permits multiple occurrences of the same property name to support multiple values. The server must accept and process these properties, though their semantic meaning is implementation-specific and not defined by the MQTT v5 specification. This mechanism requires that the CONNECT packet includes a Properties section and that the User Property identifier (0x26) is properly encoded followed by valid UTF-8 string pairs.

In the CONNECT packet payload, the presence of optional fields is determined by flags set in the Variable Header. When present, these fields MUST be serialized in the strict order: Client Identifier, Will Properties, Will Topic, Will Payload, User Name, Password. This ordering ensures proper packet parsing and interoperability between MQTT clients and brokers. The rule applies during CONNECT packet construction and validation, and compliance results in successful connection establishment while non-compliance MUST result in connection termination with a Malformed Packet error. [MQTT-3.1.3-1]

In the MQTT v5 AUTH packet, the Authentication Data property (identifier 0x16) must appear at most once in the variable header. Including this property more than once constitutes a Protocol Error, which requires the receiver to close the network connection as specified in section 4.13. This rule applies during the extended authentication exchange when the client or server sends an AUTH packet, and the authentication method in use defines the format and interpretation of the Authentication Data.

The Authentication Data property (identifier 0x16) in the CONNECT packet variable header must only be present when an Authentication Method property has been specified. If Authentication Data is included without a corresponding Authentication Method, this constitutes a Protocol Error. This validation occurs during CONNECT packet processing, and the connection must be terminated with an appropriate error code when this condition is detected. Additionally, the Authentication Data property must not appear more than once in the CONNECT properties, and its content format is defined by the authentication method specified in the Authentication Method property.

The Payload of the CONNECT packet contains one or more length-prefixed fields, whose presence is determined by the flags in the Variable Header. These fields, if present, MUST appear in the order Client Identifier, Will Properties, Will Topic, Will Payload, User Name, Password [MQTT-3.1.3-1].
