This rule governs the structure and composition of the CONNECT packet payload, ensuring proper interpretation of optional authentication and Last Will and Testament features. It applies during the connection establishment phase when a client initiates communication with a broker. The expected outcome is successful packet parsing and connection setup when the payload fields are correctly formatted and ordered according to the specified flags. Prerequisites include valid Variable Header flags that indicate which optional fields are present, and the Client Identifier field must always be included as the first mandatory field.

The Authentication Data property (identifier 0x16) contains binary data whose structure and interpretation are exclusively determined by the negotiated authentication method. This rule applies during the AUTH packet exchange when extended authentication is in progress, and the contents must conform to the requirements of the specific authentication mechanism being used (e.g., SCRAM, OAuth). Implementations must not include this property more than once in a single AUTH packet, as doing so constitutes a Protocol Error. For detailed specifications of supported authentication methods, refer to section 4.12.

The Authentication Method property (identifier 0x15) in the CONNECT packet variable header is a UTF-8 encoded string that specifies the method for extended authentication. If this property is absent from the CONNECT packet, the client and server must not perform extended authentication as defined in section 4.12. This rule applies during connection establishment and governs whether the extended authentication flow is initiated. When the Authentication Method is absent, the connection proceeds with standard authentication only, and no AUTH packets are exchanged. The absence of this property indicates that the client does not require extended authentication and the server must not request it.

The Authentication Method property (identifier 0x15) in the CONNECT packet variable header MUST appear at most once. This UTF-8 encoded string specifies the method for extended authentication as defined in section 4.12. Including this property multiple times constitutes a protocol error, requiring the connection to be terminated. If the property is absent, extended authentication is not performed. When present, the client is restricted to sending only AUTH or DISCONNECT packets until receiving a CONNACK packet [MQTT-3.1.2-30]. This rule applies during the initial connection handshake when processing CONNECT packet properties.

When a Client initiates a connection by including the Authentication Method property in the CONNECT packet, it enters an extended authentication phase. During this phase, the Client MUST restrict its outgoing packets to only AUTH (for continuing authentication exchanges) or DISCONNECT (to abort the connection) until it receives a CONNACK packet from the Server. This ensures that the authentication process is not disrupted by other control packets and maintains protocol integrity. The rule applies from the moment the CONNECT packet is sent and remains in effect until the CONNACK is received, confirming whether the connection (and authentication) proceeds or fails.

The Authentication Method property (identifier 0x15) in the CONNECT packet's variable header MUST be followed by a UTF-8 encoded string specifying the name of the authentication method for extended authentication. This rule applies during the connection establishment phase when enhanced security mechanisms are required. The expected outcome is that the client and broker can proceed with the specified authentication exchange. Prerequisites include: the property must appear at most once (multiple occurrences constitute a protocol error), and if absent, extended authentication is not performed. Additionally, once a client includes this property in CONNECT, it MUST NOT send any packets other than AUTH or DISCONNECT until receiving a CONNACK packet [MQTT-3.1.2-30], ensuring proper authentication flow control as defined in section 4.12.
req_fields: UTF-8 encoded string specifying authentication method name

rule: The length of the Properties field within the Will Properties section of a CONNECT packet MUST be encoded as a Variable Byte Integer. This rule applies after the Will Properties identifier byte and before the actual property data, ensuring proper parsing of the subsequent properties. The encoded length MUST accurately represent the total number of bytes occupied by all Will Properties, allowing receivers to correctly identify property boundaries. This encoding mechanism follows the Variable Byte Integer format defined in section 1.5.5 of the MQTT v5 specification, supporting efficient packet processing while maintaining protocol compatibility.

In the CONNECT packet's variable header properties, the Request Problem Information property (identifier 0x17) must appear at most once and must have a value of either 0 or 1. This rule applies during connection establishment when the Client is sending the CONNECT packet to the Server. If the property is included multiple times or contains an invalid value, the Server must treat this as a Protocol Error and close the connection. When the property is absent, the Server assumes a default value of 1. The value determines whether the Server is permitted to send Reason String or User Properties in response packets for error conditions: a value of 0 restricts these properties to CONNACK, DISCONNECT, and PUBLISH packets only, while a value of 1 allows them in any packet where normally permitted.

The Request Problem Information property in the CONNECT packet allows the Client to control whether the Server may include Reason String or User Properties in failure responses. This property must be a single byte with value 0 or 1 (defaulting to 1 if absent). When set to 0, the Server is restricted from sending Reason String or User Properties except in PUBLISH, CONNACK, or DISCONNECT packets. If the Server violates this restriction, the Client must respond with a DISCONNECT packet containing Reason Code 0x82 (Protocol Error). When set to 1, the Server may include these diagnostic fields in any packet where permitted by the specification. This rule applies during connection establishment and governs subsequent error handling throughout the session.

During the CONNECT packet exchange, if the Client omits the Maximum Packet Size property (identifier 0x27) in the CONNECT variable header, the Server MUST NOT impose any additional packet size restrictions beyond the inherent protocol limits defined by the remaining length encoding (maximum 268,435,455 bytes) and fixed header sizes. This rule applies specifically to the CONNECT packet processing phase and governs how the Server should handle packet size limitations when this optional property is absent. The expected outcome is that the Server may send packets of any size up to the protocol's maximum capacity, provided all other protocol requirements are met. This behavior is conditional on the Client not including the Maximum Packet Size property and assumes the CONNECT packet is otherwise valid according to the protocol specification.

When the Client sets the Request Problem Information property to 0 in the CONNECT packet, it explicitly restricts the Server from including Reason String or User Properties in any packet type except PUBLISH, CONNACK, or DISCONNECT. This restriction applies throughout the entire connection session after successful connection establishment. If the Client receives a packet containing either Reason String or User Properties in any other packet type (such as SUBACK, UNSUBACK, PINGRESP, etc.), it MUST treat this as a protocol violation and immediately terminate the connection by sending a DISCONNECT packet with Reason Code 0x82 (Protocol Error), following the error handling procedures specified in section 4.13. This rule ensures that Clients can control when they receive diagnostic information to optimize bandwidth usage and processing overhead.

When a Client sets the Request Problem Information property to 0 in the CONNECT packet, it explicitly restricts the Server's ability to include diagnostic information. In this case, the Server is permitted to include Reason Strings or User Properties only in CONNACK or DISCONNECT packets, and additionally in PUBLISH packets (which inherently support these properties regardless of this setting). However, the Server MUST NOT include Reason Strings or User Properties in any other packet types (such as SUBACK, UNSUBACK, or PINGRESP). If the Client receives such prohibited properties in non-allowed packets, it MUST treat this as a protocol error and respond with a DISCONNECT packet containing Reason Code 0x82 (Protocol Error). This rule applies throughout the connection lifetime once established with Request Problem Information set to 0. [MQTT-3.1.2-29]

The Maximum Packet Size property in the CONNACK packet specifies the largest MQTT Control Packet (defined in section 2.1.4 as the total number of bytes including fixed header, variable header, and payload) that the Server will accept. This rule applies during connection establishment when the Server includes this property in the CONNACK response. If present, the Client MUST NOT send any packet exceeding this size limit. If the Server receives an oversized packet, it MUST treat this as a Protocol Error and disconnect with Reason Code 0x95 (Packet too large). The property must appear at most once with a non-zero value; multiple occurrences or a zero value constitute Protocol Errors. When absent, packet size is only constrained by protocol encoding limits.

During the CONNECT packet processing, when the Client includes the Maximum Packet Size property (identifier 0x27) in the Variable Header Properties, the value must be a positive four-byte integer. This rule applies specifically to the CONNECT packet validation phase. The Server must validate that this property appears at most once and that its value is non-zero. If either condition is violated, the Server must treat this as a Protocol Error and close the connection. This validation ensures unambiguous packet size limits and prevents invalid configurations that would make communication impossible, as a zero-sized maximum would prohibit all packet transmission. The rule applies regardless of whether other CONNECT properties are valid, and the Maximum Packet Size property is optional - its absence indicates no Client-imposed packet size restrictions beyond protocol-defined limits.

The Client MUST include the Maximum Packet Size property in the CONNECT packet to specify the maximum allowable size (in bytes) of any complete MQTT Control Packet it will accept from the Server. This rule applies during connection establishment and governs all subsequent packet transmissions from the Server to the Client. If specified, the Server MUST NOT send any packet exceeding this limit and MUST discard oversized packets without transmission. When the Client receives an oversized packet, it MUST treat this as a protocol error and disconnect with Reason Code 0x95 (Packet too large). The property value MUST be a positive four-byte integer and MUST NOT appear more than once in the CONNECT properties. If omitted, packet size is only constrained by protocol encoding limits.

When the Server attempts to send an MQTT Control Packet to a Client that exceeds the Maximum Packet Size limit established in the CONNECT properties, the Server MUST discard the packet without transmission and proceed as though the Application Message was successfully delivered [MQTT-3.1.2-25]. This rule applies after the Client has specified a valid Maximum Packet Size during connection establishment and the Server is processing outbound messages. The expected outcome is that the Server treats the discarded message as fully handled, without retransmission or error notification to the Client, while ensuring no packet exceeding the size limit is ever transmitted. This behavior is mandatory only when the Client has explicitly set a Maximum Packet Size and the Server is operating within the normal message distribution flow.

During the CONNECT handshake, if the Client specifies a Maximum Packet Size property (0x27) with a non-zero value, the Server MUST ensure that all MQTT control packets it sends to that Client do not exceed this specified size limit [MQTT-3.1.2-24]. This restriction applies to all subsequent protocol interactions after the CONNECT packet is processed and persists for the duration of the connection. When this rule is followed, the Client will receive only packets that fit within its declared processing capacity. The rule applies only when the Client explicitly includes the Maximum Packet Size property in the CONNECT packet and the Server accepts the connection. If the Server needs to send a packet that exceeds this limit, it MUST discard the packet without transmission and handle it as described in the application message completion logic.

When the Will Flag in the CONNECT packet is set to 1, indicating that a Will Message is configured, the Will Properties field must immediately follow in the payload. This field specifies the properties of the Will Message, including application message attributes and conditions for its publication, and consists of a Property Length followed by the actual Properties data. This rule applies during the connection setup phase when a client includes a Will Message in its CONNECT request, ensuring the broker correctly interprets and stores the Will Message configuration for potential future publication.

When a Client specifies a Maximum Packet Size in the CONNECT packet (property identifier 0x27), this establishes an upper bound on the total size of any MQTT Control Packet the Server may send. This rule applies during normal protocol operation after the connection is established. If the Server transmits a packet exceeding this negotiated limit, the Client MUST treat this as a Protocol Error and immediately terminate the connection by sending a DISCONNECT packet with Reason Code 0x95 (Packet too large), as specified in section 4.13. This enforcement mechanism ensures that Clients can protect themselves from resource exhaustion due to oversized packets. The rule applies only when the Client has explicitly set a non-zero Maximum Packet Size during connection establishment; if no limit is specified, the Client must accept packets up to the protocol's inherent size limitations.

When the Will Flag is set to 1 in the CONNECT packet, the Will Properties field MUST immediately follow in the payload. This field defines both the publication properties for the Will Message (such as content type, response topic, or user properties) and the conditions for publishing the Will Message (such as delay intervals). The Will Properties structure MUST consist of a Variable Byte Integer Property Length followed by the encoded Properties themselves, where the Property Length indicates the total number of bytes in the Properties section. This structure enables the client to specify how and when the server should publish the Will Message upon unexpected disconnection.

When a Client establishes an MQTT connection with a non-zero Keep Alive value, it must maintain regular communication with the Server within the specified time interval. Specifically, if the Client has not transmitted any other MQTT Control Packets during the Keep Alive period, it MUST send a PINGREQ packet to demonstrate liveness [MQTT-3.1.2-20]. This requirement applies continuously after connection establishment and ensures the Client meets its obligation to prevent the Server from closing the connection due to inactivity. The Client should send PINGREQ sufficiently before the Keep Alive timeout expires to account for network latency and processing delays.

If the Will Flag is set to 1 in the CONNECT packet, the Will Properties field must immediately follow in the payload. This field specifies two categories of properties: (1) Application Message properties (such as Content Type, Response Topic, or User Properties) that will be included when the Will Message is published posthumously, and (2) control properties (notably Will Delay Interval) that determine the timing conditions for publishing the Will Message. The field structure consists of a Property Length followed by the actual Properties data, and these properties govern both the content characteristics and publication timing of the Will Message that the server will publish if the client disconnects unexpectedly.

The Client MAY send a PINGREQ packet at any time during an active MQTT session, regardless of the negotiated Keep Alive value, to proactively verify connectivity. Upon sending PINGREQ, the Client MUST wait for a corresponding PINGRESP packet from the Server. If PINGRESP is received, this confirms that both the network path and Server are operational. This mechanism operates independently of the mandatory Keep Alive timer requirements and provides an on-demand liveness check capability. The Client SHOULD close the Network Connection if no PINGRESP is received within a reasonable timeout period after sending PINGREQ.

During the connection establishment phase, when a Client sends a CONNECT packet with a Keep Alive value and receives a CONNACK packet containing a Server Keep Alive property, the Client MUST immediately adopt and use the Server's specified Keep Alive value for all subsequent timing calculations and PINGREQ transmissions, overriding the original value it proposed. This rule ensures that the Server's configured session timeout preferences take precedence over Client requests, maintaining protocol consistency and allowing Servers to enforce their own connection management policies. The rule applies specifically when the Server includes the Server Keep Alive property in the CONNACK response, and the Client must implement this value change before starting any Keep Alive monitoring or sending subsequent control packets.

In the MQTT v5 CONNECT packet payload, the Will Properties section may include a Content Type property (identifier 0x03) followed by a UTF-8 encoded string describing the Will Message content. This property must appear at most once within the Will Properties. If the Content Type property is included more than once in the same Will Properties, this constitutes a Protocol Error. The actual value and interpretation of the Content Type are defined by the sending and receiving applications, but the protocol requires strict adherence to the single-occurrence rule during packet validation.

The Keep Alive mechanism ensures persistent connection monitoring between MQTT clients and servers. When a Client specifies a non-zero Keep Alive value during connection establishment, it commits to sending MQTT Control Packets (including PINGREQ packets) at intervals not exceeding this value. The Server MUST monitor the elapsed time since the last received Control Packet from the Client. If this interval exceeds one and a half times the negotiated Keep Alive period, the Server MUST treat this as a connectivity failure and immediately close the Network Connection to the Client. This timeout detection applies continuously after successful connection establishment and serves as the Server's primary mechanism for identifying unresponsive Clients. The rule ensures network resources are promptly released when Clients become non-communicative, while allowing reasonable network latency through the 1.5x grace period.

During an active MQTT session where the Keep Alive mechanism is enabled (non-zero Keep Alive value), if a Client sends a PINGREQ packet to verify connectivity and does not receive a corresponding PINGRESP packet from the Server within a reasonable time period (typically determined by application requirements and network conditions), the Client SHOULD treat this as a connectivity failure and close the Network Connection to the Server. This rule applies specifically to the client-side keep-alive monitoring process and ensures that clients can detect when the Server becomes unresponsive, allowing them to take appropriate recovery actions. The Keep Alive value negotiated during connection establishment defines the expected responsiveness, but the "reasonable amount of time" for PINGRESP receipt may be shorter than the Keep Alive interval and should account for network latency and application tolerance for unresponsiveness.

In the MQTT v5 CONNECT packet payload, within the Will Properties section, the Message Expiry Interval property (identifier 0x02) must appear at most once. If this property is included multiple times in the same Will Properties block, it constitutes a Protocol Error. This rule applies during the parsing of the CONNECT packet when processing Will Properties, and the expected outcome is that the receiving entity (typically the Server) must treat this as a malformed packet and close the network connection as specified for Protocol Errors. The prerequisite for this rule is that the client includes Will Properties in the CONNECT packet; if no Will Properties are present, this rule does not apply.

In the CONNECT packet's Will Properties section, if the Message Expiry Interval property (identifier 0x02) is included, its four-byte integer value defines the maximum lifetime in seconds for the Will Message. This value must be transmitted exactly once; duplicate inclusions constitute a Protocol Error. When the Server subsequently publishes the Will Message due to an unexpected client disconnection, it MUST include this value as the Publication Expiry Interval in the PUBLISH packet properties. If this property is omitted from the Will Properties, the Server MUST NOT include a Publication Expiry Interval when publishing the Will Message, indicating the message should persist indefinitely unless removed by other means.

The Message Expiry Interval property (identifier 0x02) is optional in the CONNECT packet's Will Properties. If this property is included, its four-byte integer value defines the lifetime in seconds for the Will Message, which the Server must send as the Publication Expiry Interval when publishing the Will Message. If this property is absent from the Will Properties, the Server MUST NOT include any Message Expiry Interval when publishing the Will Message, meaning the Will Message will not have an expiration time set by the client. This rule applies specifically to the handling of Will Message properties during CONNECT packet processing and subsequent Will Message publication.

The Response Topic property (identifier 0x08) in the CONNECT packet's Will Properties must appear at most once. If this property is included multiple times, the receiver MUST treat this as a Protocol Error and close the network connection. This rule applies during the parsing of the CONNECT packet payload when processing Will Properties. The Response Topic, when present, indicates that the Will Message is a request message, enabling request/response communication patterns as defined in section 4.10. The property value must be a valid UTF-8 encoded string representing the topic name for response messages.

The Response Topic property (identifier 0x08) in a PUBLISH packet MUST be followed by a UTF-8 Encoded String that serves as the Topic Name for response messages. This property applies when implementing request/response patterns in MQTT v5, where the presence of a Response Topic identifies the message as a request. The expected outcome is that recipients of such messages will publish their responses to this specified topic. Prerequisites include: the Response Topic MUST be a valid UTF-8 string per section 1.5.4, MUST NOT contain wildcard characters, MUST appear only once in the properties, and servers MUST forward it unaltered to all subscribers. If Correlation Data is present in the request, it SHOULD be included in the response publication for message correlation.

When the Will Flag in the CONNECT packet is set to 1, indicating that a Last Will and Testament (LWT) message is configured, the Will Topic MUST immediately follow in the payload as the next field. This field MUST be encoded as a UTF-8 string, as specified in section 1.5.4 of the MQTT v5 specification [MQTT-3.1.3-11]. This rule applies during the connection establishment phase and ensures that the broker can correctly identify the topic for publishing the LWT message if the client disconnects unexpectedly.

When the Will Flag is set to 1 in the CONNECT packet, the Will Topic field MUST be present in the payload and MUST be encoded as a UTF-8 string as specified in section 1.5.4. This encoding requirement applies during the connection establishment phase when the client includes a Last Will and Testament message. Proper UTF-8 encoding ensures that the topic name can be correctly interpreted by the broker and other clients when the will message is published, preventing malformed topic names that could cause protocol errors or message delivery failures. [MQTT-3.1.3-11]

The Payload Format Indicator property in the CONNECT packet's Will Properties specifies the encoding format of the Will Message payload. When this property is included, it must contain either: 0x00 to indicate the Will Message consists of unspecified bytes (equivalent to omitting the property entirely), or 0x01 to indicate UTF-8 encoded character data that must conform to RFC 3629. This rule applies during connection establishment when a Will Message is specified, and the property must not appear more than once. If the server validates the payload format and finds it inconsistent with the indicator, it must respond with CONNACK reason code 0x99 (Payload format invalid).

The Response Topic property (identifier 0x08) in the CONNECT packet's Will Properties, when present, designates the associated Will Message as a Request message. This property MUST be a single UTF-8 encoded string specifying the topic for response messages, and including it more than once constitutes a Protocol Error. This designation applies during the processing of the Will Message publication, which occurs when the client's connection terminates without a DISCONNECT packet. When this rule is followed, the Will Message is treated as a Request, enabling response routing according to the Request/Response pattern defined in section 4.10. The property is only valid when included in the Will Properties of a CONNECT packet and requires that the Will Message itself is also present.

In the MQTT v5 CONNECT packet's Will Properties section, the Payload Format Indicator property (identifier 0x01) MUST NOT appear more than once. This rule applies when parsing the Will Properties during connection establishment, and if violated, the Server MUST treat it as a Protocol Error and close the network connection. The property is optional and, when present, indicates whether the Will Message contains unspecified bytes (0x00) or UTF-8 encoded character data (0x01). If included, the Server MAY validate that the Will Message conforms to the specified format and respond with a CONNACK packet containing Reason Code 0x99 (Payload format invalid) if validation fails, but duplicate instances of the property are strictly prohibited and result in immediate connection termination.

In the SUBACK packet's variable header, when the User Property identifier (0x26) is present, it must be immediately followed by a UTF-8 String Pair consisting of a name and value. This property enables the server to include supplementary diagnostic or informational metadata in the subscription acknowledgement. The rule applies during SUBACK packet construction when the server includes user properties, but only if their inclusion does not cause the packet size to exceed the client-specified Maximum Packet Size. Multiple User Property entries are permitted within a single SUBACK packet, including duplicate names, to convey multiple name-value pairs. The UTF-8 String Pair must maintain proper encoding and length formatting as specified by the MQTT v5 string data type requirements.

When a CONNECT packet includes a Will Properties section with a Payload Format Indicator property, the Server MAY optionally validate that the actual Will Message content conforms to the specified format. If the Payload Format Indicator value is 0x01 (indicating UTF-8 encoded character data), the Server MAY verify that the Will Message contains well-formed UTF-8 data as defined by RFC 3629. If validation is performed and the Will Message format does not match the indicated format, the Server MUST send a CONNACK packet with Reason Code 0x99 (Payload format invalid) and close the connection. This validation applies only when the client has successfully established a connection and provided a Will Message with a valid Payload Format Indicator property that appears exactly once in the Will Properties.

In the AUTH packet's variable header properties, the User Property (identifier 0x26) may be included multiple times to represent distinct name-value pairs, where each occurrence consists of a UTF-8 string pair. This allows the sender to provide multiple diagnostic or informational attributes. The rule applies when constructing AUTH packets for authentication exchange, provided that including all User Properties does not cause the packet size to exceed the receiver's specified Maximum Packet Size. When followed, the recipient must process all name-value pairs, including those with duplicate names, as valid authentication-related metadata.

When processing the CONNECT packet payload, if the Password Flag in the Variable Header is set to 1, the Password field must immediately follow the User Name field in the payload. The Password field contains binary data that may represent authentication credentials or other security information. This rule applies during CONNECT packet parsing when the client requests password-based authentication, and the server must interpret this field as the next sequential data element after verifying the Password Flag is set.

When the Password Flag in the CONNECT packet is set to 1, the Password field MUST be formatted as Binary Data, consisting of a two-byte length followed by the specified number of data bytes. This field immediately follows the User Name field (if present) in the payload and may contain any authentication credentials, not limited to traditional passwords. The client MUST include this field only when the Password Flag is 1, and the server MUST interpret it as binary data according to the MQTT binary data format specification.

When the Will Flag in the CONNECT packet is set to 1, the Will Payload MUST immediately follow the Will Properties field in the packet payload. This field contains the binary data that will be published as the Application Message Payload to the Will Topic if the client disconnects unexpectedly. The Will Payload is only present and processed when the Will Flag is enabled, and its content is subject to the Will QoS and Will Retain settings defined in the CONNECT packet header.

When a Client includes multiple User Properties in the CONNECT packet's Will Properties, the Server MUST preserve the exact order of these name-value pairs when publishing the Will Message. This ordering requirement applies specifically during the publication of the Will Message triggered by an unexpected Client disconnection. The Server MUST NOT reorder, group, or otherwise alter the sequence of User Properties as received from the Client, ensuring that applications relying on the specific arrangement of these properties can correctly interpret their meaning and priority. This rule applies only when the Will Message is actually published and requires that all User Properties from the original CONNECT packet's Will Properties section be maintained in their original transmission order. [MQTT-3.1.3-10]

When the Will Flag is set to 1 in the CONNECT packet, the Will Payload field must immediately follow in the payload structure. This field contains the Binary Data that constitutes the Application Message Payload to be published to the Will Topic if the client unexpectedly disconnects, as specified in section 3.1.2.5. The presence and content of this payload are conditional upon the Will Flag being enabled and the Will Topic being specified, ensuring that the broker can deliver the predefined message when connection loss is detected.

When the Will Flag is set to 1 in the CONNECT packet, the Will Payload field MUST be included as the next component in the payload structure. This field contains Binary Data that defines the Application Message Payload to be published to the Will Topic upon unexpected client disconnection, as specified in section 3.1.2.5. The binary data format allows for arbitrary content including text, encrypted data, or structured formats, and its length is determined by the Will Properties and preceding length indicators in the packet structure.

During CONNECT packet processing, if the User Name Flag in the Connect Flags byte is set to 1, the User Name field must immediately follow the preceding fields in the CONNECT payload. This field MUST be encoded as a UTF-8 string as specified in section 1.5.4 [MQTT-3.1.3-12]. The server may utilize this User Name for authentication and authorization purposes. This rule applies only when the User Name Flag is explicitly set to 1, and the User Name field must be present and properly formatted in that case.

When the User Name Flag is set to 1 in the CONNECT packet, the User Name field MUST be present in the payload and encoded as a UTF-8 string as specified in section 1.5.4. This encoding requirement applies during the connection establishment phase when the client transmits the CONNECT packet to the server. Proper UTF-8 encoding ensures the server can correctly interpret the User Name for authentication and authorization purposes. The rule is conditional upon the User Name Flag being set to 1, and non-compliance MUST result in the server treating the packet as malformed and closing the connection [MQTT-3.1.3-12].

When the User Name Flag is set to 1 in the CONNECT packet, the User Name field MUST be present as a UTF-8 Encoded String in the payload. This field is optional but, when provided, the Server MAY use it for authentication to verify client identity and for authorization to determine access rights to specific topics or operations. The Server's specific authentication and authorization mechanisms are implementation-defined and not specified by the protocol.

The Will Delay Interval property (identifier 0x18) MUST be included at most once in the CONNECT packet's Will Properties. If this property appears multiple times, it constitutes a Protocol Error. This rule applies during the parsing of the CONNECT packet payload when processing Will Properties. When followed correctly, the server will interpret the single Will Delay Interval value (a 4-byte integer in seconds) to determine the delay before publishing the Will Message after client disconnection. If the property is absent, the default value of 0 (no delay) is used. The rule applies specifically when the CONNECT packet includes Will Properties and is enforced during the initial packet validation phase before any session state changes occur.

In the MQTT v5 CONNECT packet's Will Properties section, the Will Delay Interval property (identifier 0x18) specifies a delay in seconds before publishing the Will Message after the client's connection is lost. If this property is omitted from the Will Properties, the default value of 0 seconds applies, meaning the Will Message is published immediately upon connection loss detection, without any delay. This rule applies when processing the CONNECT packet payload during connection establishment and governs the timing behavior of Will Message publication. The absence of the Will Delay Interval property must be handled by setting the interval to 0, ensuring immediate Will Message publication unless overridden by session continuation scenarios where a new connection is established before the delay period expires.

The Property Length field in the CONNACK packet Variable Header specifies the total byte length of the Properties that follow, encoded as a Variable Byte Integer. This rule applies when a CONNACK packet includes optional Properties, which occurs after the server processes a CONNECT request and prepares the acknowledgement. The expected outcome is that the client can correctly parse the Properties section by first reading the Variable Byte Integer to determine its length, ensuring proper interpretation of subsequent property data. This encoding must be used only if Properties are present; if no Properties are included, the Property Length must be zero.

The Variable Header of the CONNACK Packet, which is sent by the server in response to a client's CONNECT packet, must contain the following fields in the specified order: Connect Acknowledge Flags (indicating session status), Connect Reason Code (specifying connection outcome), and Properties (containing optional session and connection parameters). This structure applies when establishing an MQTT v5 session and ensures proper interpretation of the connection result. The Properties field must follow the encoding rules defined in section 2.2.2, and this packet format is mandatory for all CONNACK responses regardless of connection success or failure.

In the MQTT v5 PUBLISH packet properties, the Correlation Data property (identifier 0x09) must appear at most once. This property, which consists of binary data following the identifier byte, is used by the request sender to correlate response messages with their original requests. If a PUBLISH packet contains multiple Correlation Data properties, this constitutes a Protocol Error. The rule applies during packet validation when processing PUBLISH packets, particularly those used in request-response patterns. When followed correctly, the server must forward the Correlation Data unaltered to all subscribers [MQTT-3.3.2-16], and responders should include it unchanged in their response publications. This rule is conditional on the presence of Correlation Data in a PUBLISH packet and is essential for maintaining the integrity of request-response correlation mechanisms.

This rule governs the behavior when a client reconnects using the same Client Identifier as an existing connection. When a new network connection is established with the same Client Identifier, the Will Message from the previous connection will be published unless both of the following conditions are met: (1) the new connection specifies Clean Start = 0 (indicating session continuation), and (2) the Will Delay Interval property is present with a value greater than zero. This mechanism allows temporary network interruptions to be handled gracefully without triggering Will Messages, provided the client reconnects within the specified delay period. If either condition is not met (Clean Start = 1 or Will Delay Interval = 0), the Will Message will be published immediately upon the previous connection's termination.

When a client specifies a Will Delay Interval in its CONNECT packet, the server must delay publishing the Will Message until either the specified interval (in seconds) elapses or the session terminates, whichever occurs first. This rule applies specifically when a client establishes a new network connection to resume an existing session before the Will Delay Interval has expired. In such cases, the server MUST NOT publish the Will Message, as the client's reconnection indicates it has not permanently disconnected. This behavior requires that the session is persistent (Clean Start = 0) and the Will Delay Interval is greater than zero. If the interval is zero or the session ends (e.g., due to Clean Start = 1), the Will Message is published immediately upon disconnection. [MQTT-3.1.3-9]

In the CONNACK packet's variable header, Byte 1 contains the Connect Acknowledge Flags where Bit 0 represents the Session Present Flag. This flag indicates whether the server is resuming an existing session (value 1) or starting a new session (value 0) based on the client's Clean Start flag and server session state. The rule applies during connection establishment when the server sends the CONNACK packet in response to a CONNECT packet. All other bits (7-1) must be set to 0 as reserved. The expected outcome is proper session state communication between client and server, enabling correct session management behavior throughout the MQTT communication.

The CONNACK packet is the mandatory response packet that a Server MUST send to a Client after receiving a CONNECT packet, initiating the MQTT session establishment. This rule applies immediately following the Client's CONNECT packet transmission and governs the initial handshake phase. The Server MUST send exactly one CONNACK packet per network connection, containing a Reason Code indicating connection success (0x00) or failure, and MUST send this before any other packet type except AUTH. The Client MUST wait for this CONNACK response and SHOULD close the connection if not received within a reasonable application-dependent timeframe. Prerequisites include a valid CONNECT packet from the Client and an established network connection between Client and Server.

After establishing a network connection, when a server receives a CONNECT packet from a client, it MUST send exactly one CONNACK packet in response. This CONNACK packet, which may contain either a success or error indication, must be the only CONNACK sent over that network connection. If the client does not receive this single CONNACK within a reasonable time, it should close the connection. This rule ensures the connection handshake completes cleanly and prevents protocol confusion from multiple connection acknowledgments. [MQTT-3.2.0-2]

This rule illustrates the structure and content of a CONNECT packet's variable header in MQTT v5, as shown in Figure 3-6. It applies when a client initiates a connection to a broker and must format the variable header according to the protocol specification. The expected outcome is a correctly encoded header that includes: a 4-byte Protocol Name "MQTT", Protocol Version 5, Connect Flags indicating authentication (User Name and Password Flags set), a Will message with QoS 1 (no retain), Clean Start enabled, a Keep Alive value of 10 seconds, and Properties such as Session Expiry Interval (identifier 17, value 10). Prerequisites include using MQTT v5 and ensuring all flag combinations are valid (e.g., Will Flag set enables Will QoS/Retain). This example aids in verifying byte-level encoding during implementation.

After receiving a CONNECT packet from a client, the server MUST first send a CONNACK packet with a Reason Code of 0x00 (Success) to indicate successful connection establishment before transmitting any other packet types, with the sole exception of AUTH packets which may be sent during enhanced authentication flows. This sequencing requirement ensures the client receives proper connection confirmation before handling subsequent protocol operations. The rule applies specifically during the initial connection handshake phase and is conditional upon the server accepting the connection request.

After sending a CONNECT packet to initiate a connection, if the Client does not receive a CONNACK packet response from the Server within a reasonable time period (which varies based on application type and communication infrastructure), the Client SHOULD close the underlying Network Connection. This rule applies specifically during the connection establishment phase before any other packets (except AUTH) are exchanged, and ensures that clients do not remain indefinitely waiting for a connection acknowledgment that may never arrive due to network issues or server unavailability.

During the CONNECT packet processing, if the Session Expiry Interval property is not included in the variable header, the Server MUST treat the session as having an expiry interval of 0 seconds. This default value applies when the property identifier (0x11) is absent from the CONNECT properties. A value of 0 indicates that the session terminates immediately when the Network Connection closes, and no session state is preserved. This behavior is equivalent to setting CleanSession to 1 in MQTT v3.1.1 and ensures backward compatibility while maintaining the protocol's session management semantics.

In the CONNECT packet variable header properties, the Session Expiry Interval property (identifier byte 0x11) must be followed by a four-byte integer representing the session duration in seconds. This rule applies during connection establishment when the client includes this optional property. The value determines how long the server must maintain session state after network disconnection: 0 indicates immediate expiration when the connection closes, 0xFFFFFFFF (UINT_MAX) indicates no expiration, and any other positive value specifies the exact duration. The property must appear at most once; including it multiple times constitutes a protocol error. If absent, the session expiry interval defaults to 0. Both client and server must store session state for the specified duration when the value is greater than 0 [MQTT-3.1.2-23].

During the CONNECT packet processing phase, if the Session Expiry Interval property is explicitly set to 0 or omitted (which defaults to 0), the MQTT session state must be terminated immediately upon network connection closure. This rule applies when establishing a new connection and governs session lifecycle management, requiring both client and server to discard all session-related state (including subscriptions, unacknowledged messages, and QoS flow state) once the TCP/IP connection is terminated. The expected outcome is a clean session state upon subsequent reconnections, with no persistence of previous session data. This behavior is mutually exclusive with non-zero Session Expiry Interval values, which would require session state preservation.

In the DISCONNECT packet variable header properties, the Session Expiry Interval property (identifier 0x11) must appear at most once. Including this property multiple times constitutes a Protocol Error. This rule applies during packet validation when processing DISCONNECT packets, regardless of whether the packet is sent by client or server. When violated, the receiver must treat the packet as malformed and respond with a DISCONNECT packet containing Reason Code 0x82 (Protocol Error) as specified in section 4.13. Note that servers are additionally prohibited from including this property in DISCONNECT packets entirely, and clients cannot set a non-zero Session Expiry Interval in DISCONNECT if the original CONNECT specified a zero value.

The Session Expiry Interval property in the CONNECT packet is identified by byte 17 (0x11) and must appear at most once in the properties list. This property applies during connection establishment and governs how long the server should maintain session state after network disconnection. The value is encoded as a four-byte integer representing seconds, with specific behaviors: absence or value 0 indicates immediate session termination upon disconnection, 0xFFFFFFFF indicates no expiration, and any other positive value defines the retention period. Both client and server must store session state when the interval is greater than 0. This property interacts with Clean Start to determine session persistence behavior, and clients should use the Session Present flag in CONNACK to verify session status upon reconnection.

When a Client intends to process messages exclusively during active connections without persisting session state, it MUST set Clean Start to 1 and Session Expiry Interval to 0 in the CONNECT packet. This configuration ensures the Server immediately discards any existing session state upon connection establishment and maintains no session state after network disconnection. The Client will not receive messages published before connection and must resubscribe to topics on each new connection. This behavior applies during the initial connection handshake and provides equivalent functionality to CleanSession=1 from MQTT v3.1.1.

When a Client includes the Session Expiry Interval property (identifier 0x11) in the CONNECT packet with the value 0xFFFFFFFF (UINT_MAX), this indicates that the session state must be preserved indefinitely and will not expire, regardless of the duration of disconnection. This rule applies during the connection establishment phase when processing the CONNECT packet properties. The Server MUST honor this request by maintaining all session state (including subscriptions and undelivered messages) permanently until explicitly cleared by a subsequent connection with Clean Start set to 1 or a Session Expiry Interval of 0. This behavior requires that both Client and Server support persistent session state storage and is subject to the constraint that the Session Expiry Interval property appears only once in the CONNECT packet, as duplicate instances constitute a Protocol Error.

During the CONNECT packet processing phase, when the Session Expiry Interval property is present and set to a value greater than 0 (including 0xFFFFFFFF for indefinite duration), both the Client and Server MUST preserve the complete Session State after the Network Connection is terminated. This requirement applies immediately upon successful connection establishment and persists until either the Session Expiry Interval elapses or the Session is explicitly terminated. The stored Session State must include all necessary information to resume the session, such as subscriptions and undelivered messages, enabling subsequent reconnections to continue from the previous state. This rule is conditional upon the Session Expiry Interval being explicitly set to a non-zero value in the CONNECT packet properties and takes precedence over any default session handling behavior.

When a Client connects to a Server over an intermittently available network, it MAY set a short Session Expiry Interval in the CONNECT packet properties to ensure the Server maintains session state only for a limited duration after disconnection. This allows the Client to reconnect when network connectivity is restored and resume reliable message delivery using the preserved session state. The Client MUST set the Session Expiry Interval to a non-zero value (less than 0xFFFFFFFF) and MUST NOT include this property more than once in the CONNECT packet. If the Client fails to reconnect before the Session Expiry Interval elapses, the Server will discard the session state and any undelivered Application Messages will be lost.

When establishing a connection, if a client specifies a long Session Expiry Interval (greater than 0, particularly values approaching UINT_MAX), it must have the explicit intention to reconnect to the same server and resume the session state after disconnection. This applies during the CONNECT packet transmission when setting the Session Expiry Interval property. The expected outcome is that server resources are only allocated for persistent sessions when there is a genuine need for session resumption, preventing unnecessary server state retention for clients that don't require persistent sessions. This rule applies when the client sets Clean Start to 0 or when the Session Expiry Interval is explicitly configured to a non-zero value, and the client must subsequently monitor the Session Present flag in CONNACK to verify session state availability upon reconnection.

When establishing an MQTT v5 connection, if the Client sets Clean Start to 0 and omits the Session Expiry Interval property (which defaults to 0), this configuration is functionally equivalent to setting CleanSession to 0 in MQTT v3.1.1. This behavior applies during the CONNECT packet processing phase, where the Server must preserve the Session State (including subscriptions and undelivered messages) after the Network Connection closes, enabling the Client to resume the same session upon reconnection. The expected outcome is that the Server maintains persistent session state, and the Client receives the Session Present flag in the CONNACK packet indicating whether previous session data is available. This rule applies only when the Client intends to use persistent sessions and requires that both Clean Start is explicitly set to 0 and no Session Expiry Interval property is provided in the CONNECT packet properties.

When a Client determines that it no longer requires the Server to maintain its Session State for future reconnections, it should explicitly signal this intent by disconnecting with a Session Expiry Interval property set to 0. This applies during the normal connection termination process when the Client initiates a clean disconnection. The expected outcome is that the Server will immediately discard all Session State (including subscriptions and undelivered messages) upon network connection closure, ensuring no residual session data persists. This rule applies specifically when the Client has an active MQTT session and wants to ensure complete session cleanup, and requires that the Client includes the Session Expiry Interval property with value 0 in its DISCONNECT packet.

When establishing a connection, the Client MUST use the Session Present flag in the CONNACK packet to determine whether the Server has retained valid Session State from a previous connection. This applies after the Client sends a CONNECT packet with Clean Start set to 0 and receives the CONNACK response. The expected outcome is that the Client can accurately assess whether existing subscriptions and queued messages are available, enabling proper session resumption behavior. This determination is essential regardless of any locally stored session expiry information the Client might maintain, as the Server's Session Present flag provides the authoritative indication of session state availability.

When a Client establishes a connection with a Session Expiry Interval greater than 0, it can rely on the Session Present flag in the CONNACK packet from the Server to determine whether the previous session state was preserved or had expired, rather than implementing its own session expiration tracking mechanism. This applies during the connection establishment phase after the Server processes the CONNECT packet. The expected outcome is that the Client receives clear session state information without needing to maintain local timing logic. This approach requires that the Client properly sets the Session Expiry Interval in the CONNECT properties and that the Server accurately maintains and reports session state according to the specified interval.

The Client Identifier (ClientID) must uniquely identify each Client to the Server during connection establishment. This rule applies when a Client sends a CONNECT packet, where the ClientID must be present as the first field in the payload and encoded as a UTF-8 string. When followed, the Server can properly associate the Client with its session state and process the connection request. If the ClientID is not unique (except when the Server assigns one for a zero-byte ClientID), the Server may reject the connection with Reason Code 0x85 and close the network connection. Prerequisites include the ClientID meeting length and character set requirements unless the Server explicitly allows exceptions.

The CONNACK packet Fixed Header MUST consist of exactly two bytes, as defined in the MQTT v5 protocol specification. This rule applies when a server sends a CONNACK packet in response to a client's CONNECT request during connection establishment. The first byte MUST contain the MQTT Control Packet Type (value 2) in bits 7-4 and reserved bits set to 0 in bits 3-0. The second byte MUST contain the Remaining Length field, encoded as a Variable Byte Integer, indicating the length of the Variable Header that follows. This fixed structure ensures proper packet parsing and protocol interoperability between MQTT clients and servers.

The Client Identifier (ClientID) MUST be encoded as a UTF-8 string as defined in section 1.5.4, and this encoding requirement applies when the ClientID is included as the first field in the CONNECT packet payload [MQTT-3.1.3-4]. This rule governs the fundamental format of the ClientID that uniquely identifies each client session to the server. When followed, the ClientID will be properly interpreted by both clients and servers for session state management. The rule applies unconditionally to all ClientID values, regardless of length or character composition, and serves as a prerequisite for subsequent server validation of ClientID content and length requirements.

The Client Identifier (ClientID) serves as the primary key for identifying and managing session state between a Client and Server throughout the MQTT session lifecycle. This rule applies during the CONNECT packet exchange, where the ClientID must be provided as the first field in the CONNECT payload. Both Clients and Servers must use this ClientID to associate all session-related state, including subscriptions, message queues, and connection parameters. The expected outcome is consistent session state management, enabling features like persistent sessions and message delivery guarantees. Prerequisites include the ClientID being a valid UTF-8 encoded string that meets the Server's length and character requirements, and the Client must establish a successful connection using this identifier. [MQTT-3.1.3-2]

The Client Identifier (ClientID) is a mandatory UTF-8 encoded string that uniquely identifies a Client to the Server within an MQTT session. This identifier must be the first field in the CONNECT packet payload and serves as the primary key for associating session state between the Client and Server. The Server must accept ClientIDs between 1-23 bytes containing only alphanumeric characters, though it may permit longer or more complex identifiers. If a zero-byte ClientID is provided, the Server must assign a unique identifier and return it in the CONNACK packet. Invalid ClientIDs result in connection rejection with Reason Code 0x85 and network closure. Client implementations should ensure unique ClientIDs to prevent session conflicts while avoiding long-lived orphaned sessions when using automatically generated identifiers.

During the CONNECT packet processing phase, when a Client provides a Client Identifier (ClientID) that is between 1 and 23 UTF-8 encoded bytes in length and contains only alphanumeric characters (0-9, a-z, A-Z), the Server MUST accept this ClientID as valid. This requirement applies specifically to the ClientID field, which must be present as the first field in the CONNECT packet payload and encoded as a UTF-8 string. The Server may optionally accept ClientIDs outside these constraints, but compliance with this rule ensures basic interoperability by guaranteeing acceptance of standard alphanumeric identifiers within the specified length range. If the ClientID does not meet these criteria, the Server may reject the connection with appropriate error handling. [MQTT-3.1.3-5]

During the CONNECT packet processing phase, the Server MAY accept ClientID values that exceed 23 UTF-8 encoded bytes in length, provided that the ClientID is a valid UTF-8 Encoded String and is present as the first field in the CONNECT packet payload. This optional extension to the base requirement (which mandates support for 1-23 byte ClientIDs) allows for implementation flexibility while maintaining session identification integrity. If the Server chooses to support longer ClientIDs, it must still validate them according to its implementation policies and may reject invalid ClientIDs with CONNACK Reason Code 0x85 (Client Identifier not valid) followed by network connection closure.

The Client Identifier (ClientID) is mandatory and must be positioned as the first field in the CONNECT packet Payload. This rule applies during the connection establishment phase when a client initiates an MQTT session with a server. The ClientID serves as the primary identifier for session state management between the client and server. When this rule is followed, the server can properly identify the client and associate it with the correct session state. The ClientID must be a UTF-8 encoded string, and while servers typically accept ClientIDs of 1-23 bytes containing alphanumeric characters, implementations may support longer lengths or additional characters. If a zero-byte ClientID is provided and accepted, the server must assign a unique ClientID and return it in the CONNACK packet. Failure to provide a valid ClientID may result in connection rejection with Reason Code 0x85 and termination of the network connection. [MQTT-3.1.3-3]

When a Client connects with a zero-byte ClientID and the Server permits this configuration, the Server MUST generate and assign a unique ClientID for that Client. The Server MUST then process the CONNECT packet using this assigned ClientID as if it had been originally provided by the Client, and MUST include the Assigned Client Identifier in the CONNACK packet response to inform the Client of its assigned identity [MQTT-3.1.3-7].

During the CONNECT packet processing phase, the Server MAY accept ClientID values containing characters beyond the restricted set of alphanumeric characters (0-9, a-z, A-Z) specified in [MQTT-3.1.3-5]. This optional extension applies when validating the ClientID format after confirming it is a UTF-8 encoded string and meets other basic requirements. When implemented, the Server should process such extended ClientIDs normally while maintaining session state association. This flexibility is conditional upon the Server's implementation policy and must not compromise the fundamental requirement that each ClientID remains unique within the Server's session management system. The Server MUST still reject invalid ClientIDs by sending CONNACK with Reason Code 0x85 and closing the network connection as specified in [MQTT-3.1.3-8].

During the CONNECT packet processing phase, if a Client provides a zero-byte ClientID in the payload, the Server has the option to accept this ClientID. However, if the Server chooses to accept a zero-byte ClientID, it MUST recognize this as a special case where no explicit ClientID is provided. In this scenario, the Server is REQUIRED to generate and assign a unique ClientID to the Client. The Server MUST then process the CONNECT packet as if the Client had originally supplied this assigned ClientID, and MUST include the assigned ClientID in the CONNACK packet response. This ensures proper session state management and prevents ClientID conflicts while maintaining protocol compliance. [MQTT-3.1.3-6]

When a Server determines that the Client Identifier (ClientID) provided in a CONNECT packet is invalid—such as when it violates the allowed character set, length restrictions, or other Server-specific validation criteria—the Server MAY send a CONNACK packet with Reason Code 0x85 (Client Identifier not valid) as specified in Section 4.13 (Handling errors). Following this response, the Server MUST terminate the Network Connection. This rule applies during the connection establishment phase, immediately after the Server evaluates the ClientID, and ensures that invalid ClientIDs do not result in unintended session state or resource allocation.

The Session Expiry Interval property in the DISCONNECT packet MUST be encoded as a Four Byte Integer representing the interval duration in seconds, immediately following the property identifier byte (0x11). This rule applies when a client includes the Session Expiry Interval in a DISCONNECT packet to request modification of the session's expiration time. The expected outcome is that the server will update the session's expiry interval to the specified value, unless the client previously established the session with a zero expiry interval in the CONNECT packet—in which case including any non-zero value constitutes a Protocol Error, and the server MUST respond with a DISCONNECT packet containing Reason Code 0x82 (Protocol Error). Additionally, this property MUST NOT be included more than once in the DISCONNECT packet, and servers are prohibited from sending this property in any DISCONNECT packet.

In the DISCONNECT packet variable header properties, the Session Expiry Interval property (identifier 0x11) must appear at most once. Including this property multiple times constitutes a Protocol Error. This rule applies during packet validation when processing DISCONNECT packets, regardless of whether the packet is sent by client or server. When violated, the receiver must treat the packet as malformed and respond with a DISCONNECT packet containing Reason Code 0x82 (Protocol Error) as specified in section 4.13. Note that servers are additionally prohibited from including this property in DISCONNECT packets entirely, and clients cannot set a non-zero Session Expiry Interval in DISCONNECT if the original CONNECT specified a zero value.

In the CONNACK packet's variable header properties, if the Session Expiry Interval property (identifier 0x11) is not present, the server must use the Session Expiry Interval value that was specified by the client in the original CONNECT packet. This rule applies during the connection establishment phase when the server is processing the CONNECT packet and generating the CONNACK response. The expected outcome is that session state retention duration defaults to the client's requested value when the server does not explicitly override it. This behavior requires that a valid Session Expiry Interval was previously provided in the CONNECT packet, and the server must not include this property more than once in the CONNACK packet as that would constitute a protocol error.

The Session Expiry Interval property in the DISCONNECT packet is identified by the byte value 17 (0x11), followed by a four-byte integer representing the interval in seconds. This property must not be included more than once, as doing so constitutes a Protocol Error. If absent, the Session Expiry Interval from the CONNECT packet is used. Importantly, servers MUST NOT send this property in a DISCONNECT packet [MQTT-3.14.2-2]. Additionally, if the client's original CONNECT packet specified a Session Expiry Interval of zero, the client must not set a non-zero value in the DISCONNECT packet. If a server receives such an invalid DISCONNECT packet with a non-zero interval when the original was zero, it must treat this as a Protocol Error and respond with a DISCONNECT packet containing Reason Code 0x82 (Protocol Error), as specified in section 4.13.

In the CONNACK packet properties, the Receive Maximum property (identifier 33/0x21) must appear at most once and must have a non-zero value. This rule applies when the server specifies flow control limits during connection establishment. If violated, the connection must be terminated with a Protocol Error. The property is only valid when present in the CONNACK packet from server to client, and when included, it overrides the default value of 65,535 to limit concurrent QoS 1 and QoS 2 publications the server will handle for the client.

The server MUST include the Session Expiry Interval property in the CONNACK packet when it has overridden the client's requested session expiry value from the CONNECT packet. This property explicitly informs the client of the actual session expiry interval that the server will enforce, which may differ from the client's original request due to server policies or limitations. This rule applies during connection establishment after the server processes the CONNECT packet and determines the final session expiry duration. The expected outcome is that the client receives definitive confirmation of the session lifetime that will govern the connection, enabling proper session management. This rule only applies when the server modifies the client's requested session expiry interval; if the server accepts the client's value, this property may be omitted and the client's original value remains in effect.

The Session Expiry Interval property (identifier 0x11) in the CONNACK packet MUST be followed by a four-byte integer representing the session expiry time in seconds. This property MUST NOT appear more than once in the CONNACK packet - including it multiple times constitutes a Protocol Error. When present, this property indicates that the server is overriding the Session Expiry Interval value provided by the client in the CONNECT packet. If this property is absent from the CONNACK packet, the server MUST use the Session Expiry Interval value specified in the CONNECT packet. For detailed specifications regarding Session Expiry Interval behavior, including how the server determines the value and its implications for session persistence, refer to section 3.1.2.11.2.

The Server MUST use the Receive Maximum property value, which is specified as a two-byte integer in the CONNACK packet with identifier 33 (0x21), to limit the number of concurrent QoS 1 and QoS 2 publications it will process for the Client. This rule applies during connection establishment when the Server sends the CONNACK packet to the Client. The expected outcome is that the Server will not accept more than the specified number of unacknowledged QoS 1 or QoS 2 publications from the Client at any given time. This rule does not apply if the property is absent (defaulting to 65,535), and it specifically excludes QoS 0 publications from this flow control mechanism. The Server MUST treat multiple occurrences of this property or a zero value as a Protocol Error.

The Receive Maximum property (identifier 33/0x21) specifies the maximum number of concurrent QoS 1 and QoS 2 publications that a Server is willing to process for a Client. This rule applies during the CONNACK packet exchange when establishing an MQTT v5 connection. It is important to note that this property only governs flow control for QoS 1 and QoS 2 publications and explicitly does not apply to QoS 0 publications, which may be sent by the Client without being limited by this mechanism. The property must be included at most once with a non-zero value, defaulting to 65,535 if absent, and duplicate or zero values constitute a Protocol Error.

The Assigned Client Identifier property (Identifier 0x12) in the CONNACK packet MUST be followed by a UTF-8 encoded string representing the server-assigned client identifier. This property applies when the client connects with a zero-length Client Identifier in the CONNECT packet, requiring the server to assign and return a unique identifier. The server MUST ensure the assigned identifier is not currently used by any other session and MUST include this property exactly once in the CONNACK response. Including this property multiple times constitutes a protocol error. The client must use this assigned identifier for the current session.

The Assigned Client Identifier property (Identifier: 18/0x12) is used by the Server to assign a unique Client Identifier when the Client connects with a zero-length Client Identifier in the CONNECT packet. This property MUST contain a single UTF-8 encoded string value representing the assigned identifier, and it is a Protocol Error to include this property more than once in the CONNACK packet. The Server MUST include this property in the CONNACK response when a zero-length Client Identifier is received, and the assigned identifier MUST be unique across all active sessions on the Server [MQTT-3.2.2-16]. This ensures that Clients without pre-configured identifiers can establish properly identified sessions while maintaining session uniqueness requirements.

In the CONNACK packet properties, the Assigned Client Identifier property (0x12) MUST appear at most once. This rule applies when a client connects with a zero-length Client Identifier in the CONNECT packet, and the server assigns a unique identifier for the client session. If this property is included multiple times in the CONNACK packet, it constitutes a Protocol Error, and the connection MUST be terminated. The server MUST provide this property exactly once when assigning a client identifier due to a zero-length client identifier in the CONNECT packet, ensuring the assigned identifier is unique and not currently used by any other session.

When a client sends a CONNECT packet with a zero-length Client Identifier, the server MUST assign and return a unique Client Identifier in the CONNACK packet's Assigned Client Identifier property. This assigned identifier must be newly generated and not currently used by any other session on the server. The server includes this property only once per CONNACK packet, and its presence indicates that the server has assigned the client identifier due to the client's use of a zero-length identifier in the initial CONNECT request.

When a Client connects with a zero-length Client Identifier in the CONNECT packet, the Server MUST assign and return a unique Client Identifier in the CONNACK packet's Assigned Client Identifier property. This assigned identifier MUST be newly generated and MUST NOT conflict with any existing active sessions on the Server, ensuring each client session maintains a distinct identity throughout its lifecycle. The Server MUST include this property exactly once in the CONNACK response when a zero-length Client Identifier is received.

When a Client establishes a connection by sending a CONNECT packet with a zero-length Client Identifier, the Server MUST include an Assigned Client Identifier property in the CONNACK packet. This assigned identifier MUST be a unique, newly generated value not currently associated with any other active session on the Server. The rule applies specifically during the connection establishment phase when the Server processes the CONNECT packet and prepares the corresponding CONNACK response. This ensures that clients without pre-defined identifiers receive a server-assigned unique identity for session management purposes.

When constructing a CONNACK packet in response to a Client's CONNECT request, the Server MUST evaluate whether including the Reason String property would cause the total packet size to exceed the Maximum Packet Size declared by the Client in the CONNECT packet. If including this property would result in such an overflow, the Server MUST omit the Reason String property entirely from the CONNACK packet [MQTT-3.2.2-19]. This rule ensures that the Server respects the Client's packet size limitations while maintaining protocol compliance, even if it means sacrificing optional diagnostic information. The Server must perform this size validation before finalizing the CONNACK packet construction.

The Reason String property in the UNSUBACK packet (identified by byte 0x1F) contains a UTF-8 encoded human-readable diagnostic message provided by the Server to give additional context about the unsubscribe operation. This rule applies when the Server includes the Reason String in an UNSUBACK response, and requires that Clients treat this string as informational content for debugging and logging purposes only. The Client MUST NOT attempt to parse or interpret the Reason String content for programmatic decision-making. The Server MUST ensure this property is included only once per UNSUBACK packet and MUST NOT include it if doing so would exceed the Maximum Packet Size specified by the Client in the CONNECT packet.

In the MQTT v5 PUBREL packet, the Reason String property (identifier 0x1F) is an optional human-readable diagnostic string that MUST NOT be included more than once in the PUBREL Variable Header properties. This rule applies during packet construction when the sender includes optional properties in the PUBREL packet, which is part of QoS 2 message delivery flow. If multiple Reason String properties are detected, the receiver MUST treat this as a Protocol Error and close the connection. The rule applies regardless of whether the Reason String content is identical or different across multiple instances, and is enforced in addition to the requirement that the Reason String must not cause the packet to exceed the receiver's Maximum Packet Size.

During the CONNACK packet exchange, if the Server does not include the Maximum Packet Size property in the CONNACK variable header, the Client may send MQTT control packets of any size, subject only to the inherent protocol constraints imposed by the remaining length encoding scheme (which limits packets to 256 MB) and fixed header sizes. This rule applies when the Server wishes to indicate it can handle packets up to the maximum allowed by the protocol specification. The absence of this property signifies that no additional server-imposed packet size restrictions exist beyond the fundamental protocol limitations. This behavior is conditional on the Client having successfully established a connection and received a valid CONNACK packet from the Server.

In the CONNACK packet properties, if the Maximum Packet Size property (identifier 0x27) is present, it MUST be followed by a Four Byte Integer that specifies the maximum allowable packet size the Server will accept. This rule applies when the Server includes this property in the CONNACK packet during connection establishment. The expected outcome is that the Client MUST NOT send any MQTT Control Packet whose total size exceeds this specified limit. Prerequisites include: the property MUST NOT appear more than once, the value MUST NOT be zero, and if omitted, no Server-imposed size limit applies beyond inherent protocol constraints. If the Client violates this by sending an oversized packet, the Server MUST treat it as a Protocol Error and respond with a DISCONNECT packet using Reason Code 0x95 (Packet too large).
req_fields: Four Byte Integer specifying maximum packet size

rule: The Maximum Packet Size property (identifier 0x27) in the CONNACK packet variable header MUST be included at most once and MUST NOT be set to zero. This property, when present, specifies the maximum allowable total packet size (including all headers and payload) that the Server will accept from the Client. If this property is omitted, no Server-imposed size limit applies beyond the inherent protocol constraints of remaining length encoding and header sizes. Including multiple Maximum Packet Size properties or specifying a value of zero constitutes a Protocol Error. The Client MUST adhere to this size limit in all subsequent packets sent to the Server. If the Server receives a packet exceeding this limit, it MUST treat this as a Protocol Error and send a DISCONNECT packet with Reason Code 0x95 (Packet too large).

The Maximum Packet Size property in the CONNACK packet specifies the largest MQTT Control Packet (defined in section 2.1.4 as the total number of bytes including fixed header, variable header, and payload) that the Server will accept. This rule applies during connection establishment when the Server includes this property in the CONNACK response. If present, the Client MUST NOT send any packet exceeding this size limit. If the Server receives an oversized packet, it MUST treat this as a Protocol Error and disconnect with Reason Code 0x95 (Packet too large). The property must appear at most once with a non-zero value; multiple occurrences or a zero value constitute Protocol Errors. When absent, packet size is only constrained by protocol encoding limits.
