During the connection establishment phase, the Client MAY send MQTT Control Packets immediately after transmitting the CONNECT packet without waiting for a CONNACK response. However, the Client MUST recognize that if the Server ultimately rejects the connection (by either closing the Network Connection or sending a CONNACK with a Reason Code ≥ 0x80), any data packets sent prior to receiving the CONNACK—except for AUTH packets—will be discarded and not processed by the Server. This rule applies specifically to the period between CONNECT transmission and CONNACK reception, and the Client implementation should account for this potential data loss when designing its connection state management.

After the Server successfully validates the CONNECT packet format, performs authentication and authorization checks, and confirms that all connection requirements are met (including handling any existing client session takeover and Clean Start processing), it MUST respond by sending a CONNACK packet with a Reason Code of 0x00 (Success) to indicate successful connection establishment. This response occurs only after all mandatory validation steps succeed and triggers the start of message delivery and Keep Alive monitoring. The Server MUST NOT send this success response if any validation check fails, and instead should close the Network Connection, optionally sending an appropriate error CONNACK with a Reason Code ≥ 0x80 before closing.

When a PUBLISH packet includes a Message Expiry Interval property, the Server MUST track the elapsed time since receiving the message. If the specified interval expires before the Server can initiate delivery to any matching subscriber, the Server MUST delete its stored copy of that message for the affected subscriber. This rule applies specifically when the Server holds undelivered messages for subscribers that were not available at the time of publication, ensuring expired messages are not retained indefinitely. The Message Expiry Interval must be present in the original PUBLISH packet for this expiration mechanism to apply, and the Server must recalculate the remaining interval when forwarding the message to subsequent subscribers as specified in [MQTT-3.3.2-6].

When a Server rejects a CONNECT request (due to validation failures, authentication/authorization issues, or other connection restrictions), it MUST immediately cease processing any subsequent data packets received from the Client, with the sole exception of AUTH packets which may continue to be processed. This rule applies specifically after the Server has determined that the CONNECT packet is invalid or unauthorized, and before the Network Connection is closed. The expected outcome is that the Server will either close the connection or send an appropriate CONNACK with a Reason Code ≥ 0x80, while ignoring all non-AUTH client transmissions during this rejection phase. This behavior is required regardless of whether the Client sends additional control packets before receiving CONNACK, and serves to prevent unauthorized data processing while still allowing authentication continuation via AUTH packets.

During the connection validation phase after receiving a CONNECT packet, if the Server determines that the ClientID in the incoming CONNECT packet matches an active Client currently connected to the Server, the Server MUST immediately send a DISCONNECT packet to the existing Client with Reason Code 0x8E (Session taken over) as specified in section 4.13 and MUST terminate the Network Connection of the existing Client [MQTT-3.1.4-3]. This session takeover mechanism ensures that only one active connection exists per ClientID at any given time, with the new connection taking precedence over the existing one. If the existing Client had a Will Message configured, it will be published according to the Will Message delivery rules specified in section 3.1.2.5, particularly if the Will Delay Interval is 0 or the Session Expiry Interval is 0, or if the new connection has Clean Start set to 1.

During the MQTT v5 protocol session, after a Server has established a connection with a Client and included a Maximum QoS value in the CONNACK packet, the Server must validate that all subsequent PUBLISH packets from the Client adhere to this QoS limit. If the Server receives a PUBLISH packet where the QoS value (as defined in byte 1, bits 2-1) exceeds the specified Maximum QoS, the Server MUST treat this as a protocol violation and respond by sending a DISCONNECT packet with Reason Code 0x9B (QoS not supported), following the error handling procedures outlined in section 4.13. This rule applies only when a Maximum QoS has been explicitly communicated by the Server in the CONNACK response and ensures that Clients do not attempt to use QoS levels beyond what the Server supports.

In the MQTT v5 protocol, the QoS field in the PUBLISH packet fixed header (byte 1, bits 2-1) defines the delivery assurance level for Application Messages. This rule applies during packet processing when a PUBLISH packet is received by either a Client or Server. The QoS bits combination 1,1 is reserved and invalid—a PUBLISH packet MUST NOT have both QoS bits set to 1 [MQTT-3.3.1-4]. If such a packet is received, it is considered a Malformed Packet, and the receiver MUST respond by sending a DISCONNECT packet with Reason Code 0x81 (Malformed Packet) as specified in section 4.13. This rule is independent of any negotiated Maximum QoS and applies to all PUBLISH packets regardless of the connection state.

When a Server or Client receives a PUBLISH packet with both QoS bits set to 1 (reserved value), which constitutes a Malformed Packet violation of the protocol specification, the receiving entity MUST terminate the connection by sending a DISCONNECT packet with Reason Code 0x81 (Malformed Packet) as defined in section 4.13. This rule applies specifically to the QoS field validation during PUBLISH packet processing and ensures protocol compliance by properly handling invalid QoS combinations that are explicitly prohibited by the specification.

The Topic Name, which identifies the information channel for the published payload data, MUST be the first field in the PUBLISH packet Variable Header. This requirement applies to all PUBLISH packets and ensures proper parsing and routing of the message. The Topic Name must be a UTF-8 encoded string and must not contain wildcard characters. If a Topic Alias is used, the Topic Name may be omitted only if the alias is present; otherwise, a zero-length Topic Name constitutes a protocol error.

When constructing a PUBLISH packet, the Topic Name field in the Variable Header MUST NOT contain any wildcard characters (such as '+' or '#'). This restriction applies to all PUBLISH packets sent by both clients and servers, as wildcards are only permitted in Subscription Topic Filters for matching purposes. If a PUBLISH packet contains wildcard characters in the Topic Name, it constitutes a protocol violation and MUST be rejected by the receiving entity. This rule ensures that published messages are directed to specific, well-defined topics rather than ambiguous patterns, maintaining predictable message routing throughout the MQTT system.

The Topic Name, which identifies the information channel for published payload data and must appear as the first field in the PUBLISH packet Variable Header, MUST be encoded as a UTF-8 string in compliance with section 1.5.4 [MQTT-3.3.2-1]. This requirement applies whenever a PUBLISH packet is constructed, ensuring that the topic name is correctly formatted for processing by both clients and servers. Adherence to this rule guarantees interoperability and proper message routing, while failure to comply results in a protocol error. Note that the topic name must not contain wildcard characters and, if zero-length, must be accompanied by a valid Topic Alias to avoid errors.

In the MQTT v5 protocol, the QoS field in the PUBLISH packet fixed header (byte 1, bits 2-1) defines the delivery assurance level for application messages. The valid QoS values are 0 (at most once), 1 (at least once), and 2 (exactly once), while the combination where both QoS bits are set to 1 (binary 11) is explicitly reserved and prohibited. This rule applies whenever a Server or Client processes an incoming PUBLISH packet during normal protocol operation. If such a packet is received with both QoS bits set to 1, it MUST be considered a Malformed Packet. The receiving entity MUST then terminate the connection by sending a DISCONNECT packet with Reason Code 0x81 (Malformed Packet), as specified in section 4.13 (Handling errors). This validation occurs independently of any other QoS-related restrictions, such as the Maximum QoS negotiated during connection establishment.

When a Server forwards a PUBLISH packet to a subscribing Client, the Topic Name in that packet MUST correspond to the Client's Subscription Topic Filter as defined by the topic matching rules in Section 4.7. This rule applies specifically to message forwarding from Server to Client and ensures that subscribers only receive messages matching their subscriptions. The Server MAY perform topic name mapping, but the resulting Topic Name must still satisfy the matching criteria relative to the Client's original subscription. This requirement does not apply to the original PUBLISH packet sent from a Client to the Server, and Topic Aliases may be used to optimize packet size, provided the Topic Name is valid and non-zero in length when no Topic Alias is present.

The Topic Name in a PUBLISH packet's Variable Header identifies the information channel for payload data and must be a UTF-8 encoded string that does not contain wildcard characters. When a sender uses a Topic Alias (as defined in section 3.3.2.3.4) to reduce packet size, the Topic Name may be omitted. However, if no Topic Alias is provided, the Topic Name must be present and non-empty. A Protocol Error occurs if the Topic Name field has zero length and there is no corresponding Topic Alias to identify the publication channel, as this would leave the destination topic undefined.

To reduce the size of the PUBLISH packet, the sender can use a Topic Alias in place of the full Topic Name. This rule applies when a client or server sends a PUBLISH packet and has previously established a Topic Alias mapping for that topic. The expected outcome is that subsequent PUBLISH packets for the same topic can use the shorter Topic Alias identifier instead of the full UTF-8 encoded string, reducing packet overhead while maintaining the same semantic meaning. This optimization requires that a valid Topic Alias has been previously negotiated and that the Topic Name is not zero length unless a Topic Alias is provided, as described in section 3.3.2.3.4.

The Payload Format Indicator property in the PUBLISH packet's variable header specifies the format of the message payload. When present, this property consists of a one-byte identifier (0x01) followed by a one-byte value. If the value is 0 (0x00), it indicates that the payload consists of unspecified bytes, which is functionally equivalent to omitting the Payload Format Indicator property entirely. This rule applies when a client or server includes the Payload Format Indicator in a PUBLISH packet, and the expected outcome is that receivers interpret the payload as arbitrary binary data without any specific encoding constraints. There are no prerequisites beyond including the property; however, if the property is omitted, the payload is also treated as unspecified bytes by default.

When a Server forwards a PUBLISH packet to a subscribing Client, the Topic Name in the forwarded packet MUST match the Client's Subscription Topic Filter according to the matching process defined in section 4.7 [MQTT-3.3.2-3]. However, the Server is permitted to perform topic name mapping, which may result in the Topic Name in the forwarded PUBLISH packet being different from the Topic Name in the original PUBLISH packet received from the publishing Client. This mapping capability allows Servers to implement features such as topic normalization, aliasing, or translation while maintaining proper subscription matching. The mapped Topic Name MUST still be a valid UTF-8 encoded string without wildcard characters and MUST correctly identify the information channel for payload delivery to the subscribing Client.

When a PUBLISH packet contains a Payload Format Indicator property, the Server MUST preserve and forward this property unchanged to all subscribers receiving the Application Message, regardless of the payload content or the Server's own validation of the format. This rule applies during message distribution when the Server relays the Application Message from the publisher to matching subscribers. The expected outcome is that subscribers receive the original payload format indication as set by the publisher, enabling them to perform their own format validation if needed. This requirement applies only when the Payload Format Indicator property is present in the original PUBLISH packet from the client. [MQTT-3.3.2-4]

The DUP flag in the PUBLISH packet fixed header (byte 1, bit 3) governs message delivery tracking. When set to 0, this indicates the initial transmission attempt of this specific PUBLISH packet by either Client or Server. This rule applies during packet transmission when QoS 1 or 2 is used, as QoS 0 messages must always have DUP=0. The expected outcome is that receivers can identify first transmission attempts, though they cannot assume message uniqueness since different packet identifiers may carry duplicate application messages. Prerequisites include valid packet construction and QoS level compliance, with the flag being independently determined for outgoing packets regardless of incoming packet state.

The Subscription Identifier property (identifier 11/0x0B) allows associating a numeric identifier with a subscription when a PUBLISH packet is sent as a result of matching that subscription. This property MUST contain a Variable Byte Integer value between 1 and 268,435,455 - a value of 0 constitutes a Protocol Error. When a publication matches multiple subscriptions, multiple Subscription Identifier properties may be included in any order. This mechanism enables receivers to identify which specific subscriptions triggered the message delivery, facilitating application-level message routing and processing logic.

When a Server receives a PUBLISH packet from a publishing Client, the DUP flag value from that incoming packet MUST NOT be propagated to subscribers. Instead, the Server MUST independently determine the DUP flag value for each outgoing PUBLISH packet based solely on whether that specific transmission to a subscriber is a retransmission attempt. This rule applies to all QoS levels (0, 1, and 2) and ensures that the DUP flag accurately reflects the transmission status between the Server and each subscriber, independent of the original publisher's transmission status. The outgoing DUP flag MUST be set to 1 only when the Server is retransmitting the packet to a subscriber, and to 0 for initial transmissions.

The DUP flag in the PUBLISH packet fixed header MUST be set to 0 for all messages with QoS level 0 [MQTT-3.3.1-2]. This requirement applies because QoS 0 messages are sent exactly once without retransmission mechanisms, making the DUP flag irrelevant for this QoS level. When a Client or Server sends a PUBLISH packet with QoS 0, it must initialize the DUP flag to 0 to indicate this is the first and only transmission attempt. This rule ensures protocol consistency since QoS 0 messages cannot be retransmitted, and any DUP flag value other than 0 would be invalid. The requirement applies during PUBLISH packet construction before transmission, regardless of whether the sender is a Client or Server.

The DUP flag in the PUBLISH packet fixed header MUST be set to 1 by either the Client or Server whenever they attempt to re-deliver a previously transmitted PUBLISH packet [MQTT-3.3.1-1]. This rule applies specifically to QoS 1 and QoS 2 messages during retransmission scenarios, such as when an expected acknowledgment is not received within the required timeframe. The flag serves as an indicator to the receiver that this may be a duplicate transmission attempt, though receivers cannot assume they have previously processed this specific packet. When initially sending a PUBLISH packet, the DUP flag MUST be set to 0, and for QoS 0 messages, the DUP flag MUST always remain 0 regardless of delivery attempts. Importantly, the DUP flag pertains to the MQTT Control Packet retransmission, not to the application message content, and servers MUST independently determine the DUP flag value when forwarding PUBLISH packets to subscribers based solely on whether the outgoing packet is a retransmission.

When a receiver processes a PUBLISH packet containing a Payload Format Indicator property, it MAY optionally validate that the actual payload content matches the declared format. If the Payload Format Indicator specifies UTF-8 Encoded Character Data (value 0x01), the receiver MAY verify that the payload contains well-formed UTF-8 as defined by RFC 3629. If validation is performed and the payload format is found to be invalid, the receiver SHOULD send a PUBACK (for QoS 1), PUBREC (for QoS 2), or DISCONNECT packet with Reason Code 0x99 (Payload format invalid) as specified in section 4.13. This validation is optional, and receivers are not required to perform it. The rule applies during message processing after receiving a PUBLISH packet with a Payload Format Indicator property, and the receiver must have successfully parsed the property value (either 0x00 for unspecified bytes or 0x01 for UTF-8 data) before considering validation.

When using QoS 1, a Client may receive a PUBLISH packet with the DUP flag set to 0 that contains a duplicate Application Message previously received, but with a different Packet Identifier. This scenario can occur because the DUP flag indicates retransmission of the specific MQTT Control Packet, not duplication of the Application Message content. Clients must handle such cases by recognizing that identical Application Messages can arrive in distinct packets with different identifiers, and should process the message content appropriately while managing packet acknowledgment separately. This behavior applies during normal QoS 1 message delivery and retransmission flows, and requires that the Client implement duplicate detection based on Application Message content rather than relying solely on the DUP flag or Packet Identifier.

The Subscription Identifier property in the SUBSCRIBE packet MUST be a Variable Byte Integer with a valid value between 1 and 268,435,455. This rule applies when the client includes a Subscription Identifier property in the SUBSCRIBE packet's variable header. The identifier is stored with any subscriptions created or modified by this packet and is used for message routing. A value of 0 constitutes a Protocol Error, as does including multiple Subscription Identifier properties in the same packet. If this property is omitted, the subscription is stored without an identifier.

When a Server forwards a PUBLISH packet to subscribers, the DUP flag in the outgoing packet MUST be set independently of the DUP flag value from the incoming PUBLISH packet. This rule applies specifically during message forwarding by the Server and requires that the outgoing DUP flag value be determined solely based on whether the outgoing packet is a retransmission from the Server's perspective. For initial transmissions, the DUP flag MUST be set to 0, and for retransmissions, it MUST be set to 1. This ensures that the DUP flag accurately reflects the delivery status from the forwarding entity rather than propagating the delivery status from the original publisher. This rule applies to QoS 1 and QoS 2 messages only, as QoS 0 messages MUST always have DUP flag set to 0 [MQTT-3.3.1-2].

The Subscription Identifier property (identifier 0x0B) in the PUBLISH packet MUST be followed by a Variable Byte Integer that encodes the subscription identifier value. This property applies when a server forwards a message to a client that matches one or more of the client's subscriptions, and it MUST contain a value between 1 and 268,435,455 (inclusive). If multiple subscriptions match the publication, the server MAY include multiple Subscription Identifier properties in any order. A value of 0 constitutes a Protocol Error and MUST result in connection termination. The client receiving this property MUST use the identifier(s) to determine which specific subscription(s) triggered the message delivery.

When implementing QoS 2 message delivery, which ensures exactly-once delivery semantics, developers must understand that Packet Identifiers play a critical role in tracking message state throughout the multi-step acknowledgment process. This rule applies specifically to the Variable Header of QoS 2 PUBLISH packets, where Packet Identifiers are mandatory. Section 2.2.1 provides detailed specifications about Packet Identifier assignment, uniqueness requirements, and reuse conditions. The expected outcome is proper message tracking and prevention of duplication or loss through the two-phase acknowledgment sequence (PUBLISH→PUBREC→PUBREL→PUBCOMP). Prerequisites include: the message must be assigned QoS level 2, the Packet Identifier must be currently unused, and the protocol flow must follow the state transitions defined for both sender and receiver roles.

When processing a SUBSCRIBE packet's properties, the Subscription Identifier property (identifier 0x0B) must contain a Variable Byte Integer value between 1 and 268,435,455. If the Subscription Identifier value is exactly 0, this constitutes a Protocol Error that must be detected during packet validation. This validation occurs when parsing the SUBSCRIBE packet's variable header properties before processing subscriptions. Upon encountering this error, the connection must be closed with an appropriate DISCONNECT packet indicating the protocol violation. This rule applies specifically to the Subscription Identifier property and does not affect other properties in the SUBSCRIBE packet.

When a PUBLISH packet is generated because a message matches multiple client subscriptions, the packet MUST include multiple Subscription Identifier properties (each with values from 1 to 268,435,455) corresponding to all matching subscriptions. The order in which these identifiers appear in the packet has no semantic meaning and implementations MUST NOT assign significance to their sequence. This rule applies during message delivery when the server determines subscription matches, and requires that all valid subscription identifiers for matching subscriptions be included without imposing any ordering constraints.

When a Server forwards an Application Message in a PUBLISH packet, it MUST preserve both the content and the original sequence of all User Properties from the received message, ensuring that multiple name-value pairs appear in the same order as they were originally provided by the publishing Client [MQTT-3.3.2-18]. This requirement applies specifically during message forwarding between Clients and is essential for applications that rely on the sequential integrity of User Properties for correct interpretation.

In the AUTH packet's variable header properties, the User Property (identifier 0x26) may be included multiple times to represent distinct name-value pairs, where each occurrence consists of a UTF-8 string pair. This allows the sender to provide multiple diagnostic or informational attributes. The rule applies when constructing AUTH packets for authentication exchange, provided that including all User Properties does not cause the packet size to exceed the receiver's specified Maximum Packet Size. When followed, the recipient must process all name-value pairs, including those with duplicate names, as valid authentication-related metadata.

The PUBLISH packet's Payload, which carries the application-specific message content, is permitted to have a zero length. This is determined by calculating the Payload length from the Remaining Length field in the Fixed Header minus the Variable Header length. A zero-length Payload is valid in any PUBLISH operation and indicates the transmission of an empty application message, which must be processed normally by the receiver without being treated as malformed.

A PUBACK packet MUST be sent by a receiver in response to a PUBLISH packet that specifies QoS level 1, confirming receipt of the message. This acknowledgment mechanism ensures reliable message delivery in the protocol flow. The rule applies only when the PUBLISH packet contains a valid Packet Identifier and QoS=1 flag. Upon receiving a valid PUBLISH packet with QoS 1, the receiver MUST send a PUBACK containing the same Packet Identifier, completing the QoS 1 message delivery handshake.

When a Server forwards an Application Message to a Client in a PUBLISH packet, it MUST preserve all User Properties exactly as received, including their values, order, and any duplicate name occurrences, without modification or filtering. This rule applies specifically to message forwarding scenarios where the Server acts as an intermediary, ensuring that application-specific metadata remains intact throughout the message delivery process. The User Properties are defined as UTF-8 string pairs and may appear multiple times with the same name. This behavior guarantees that application-layer name-value tags are transmitted transparently between the original publisher and the final subscriber, regardless of the Server's implementation.

In the SUBACK packet's variable header, when the User Property identifier (0x26) is present, it must be immediately followed by a UTF-8 String Pair consisting of a name and value. This property enables the server to include supplementary diagnostic or informational metadata in the subscription acknowledgement. The rule applies during SUBACK packet construction when the server includes user properties, but only if their inclusion does not cause the packet size to exceed the client-specified Maximum Packet Size. Multiple User Property entries are permitted within a single SUBACK packet, including duplicate names, to convey multiple name-value pairs. The UTF-8 String Pair must maintain proper encoding and length formatting as specified by the MQTT v5 string data type requirements.

When a PUBLISH packet includes a Response Topic property (identifier 0x08), the value of this property MUST be encoded as a UTF-8 String as specified in section 1.5.4 [MQTT-3.3.2-13]. This requirement applies during the construction of PUBLISH packets that implement request/response patterns, where the Response Topic serves as the destination for response messages. The UTF-8 encoding ensures consistent topic name interpretation across all MQTT implementations. This rule is conditional upon the inclusion of a Response Topic property and must be followed whenever this property is present. The server MUST preserve and forward the Response Topic unchanged to all subscribers, maintaining the integrity of the request/response communication channel.

In the PUBLISH packet's variable header properties, the Response Topic property (identifier 0x08) MUST appear at most once. If this property is included multiple times in a single PUBLISH packet, it constitutes a Protocol Error. This rule applies during packet validation when a PUBLISH packet is received, and the presence of duplicate Response Topic properties MUST cause the receiver to treat the packet as malformed and close the connection. The Response Topic property, when present, indicates that the message is a request and provides the topic name where responses should be published, but this functionality is only valid when the property appears exactly zero or one times per PUBLISH packet.

When a PUBLISH packet includes a Response Topic property (identifier 0x08), the UTF-8 encoded string value MUST NOT contain any wildcard characters ('+' or '#') [MQTT-3.3.2-14]. This restriction applies during packet validation when a client or server processes an outgoing PUBLISH packet containing a Response Topic. The Response Topic serves as the destination for response messages in request/response patterns, and prohibiting wildcards ensures unambiguous routing of responses to specific topics. If a Response Topic contains wildcard characters, the receiver MUST treat this as a malformed packet and close the connection as specified in section 4.13. This rule applies specifically to the Response Topic property and does not restrict wildcard usage in the main PUBLISH topic name.

The Response Topic property (identifier 0x08) in a PUBLISH packet MUST be followed by a UTF-8 Encoded String that serves as the Topic Name for response messages. This property applies when implementing request/response patterns in MQTT v5, where the presence of a Response Topic identifies the message as a request. The expected outcome is that recipients of such messages will publish their responses to this specified topic. Prerequisites include: the Response Topic MUST be a valid UTF-8 string per section 1.5.4, MUST NOT contain wildcard characters, MUST appear only once in the properties, and servers MUST forward it unaltered to all subscribers. If Correlation Data is present in the request, it SHOULD be included in the response publication for message correlation.

When a PUBLISH packet contains a Response Topic property, the Server MUST forward this property unchanged to all subscribers that receive the Application Message, preserving the exact UTF-8 Encoded String value as provided by the publisher. This rule applies during message distribution when the Server delivers messages to subscribers, ensuring that any subscriber receiving a request message has access to the designated topic for sending responses. The Response Topic must be a valid UTF-8 string without wildcard characters and must appear only once in the properties. This behavior enables the request-response pattern defined in section 4.10, where subscribers can use the unaltered Response Topic to publish their response messages. [MQTT-3.3.2-15]

When a client receives a PUBLISH packet containing a Response Topic property (indicating it is a request message), and that request message includes Correlation Data, the client MUST include the same Correlation Data as a property in the response PUBLISH packet sent to the specified Response Topic. This ensures the response can be correlated with the original request, maintaining request-response pairing integrity. This rule applies specifically to MQTT v5 request-response flows where both Response Topic and Correlation Data properties are utilized, and the receiver is generating a response message.

When a client receives an Application Message containing a Response Topic property, it indicates that the message is a request expecting a response. The receiving client MUST use the exact Response Topic value (which is a UTF-8 encoded string without wildcard characters) as the Topic Name in a PUBLISH packet when sending the response. If the original request message included Correlation Data, the responding client SHOULD include the same Correlation Data property in the response PUBLISH packet to enable request-response correlation. This mechanism enables MQTT-based request-response patterns where the Response Topic serves as the designated channel for responses, and the Server MUST preserve the Response Topic property unchanged when delivering the original message to subscribers.

When a Client sends a response PUBLISH packet using the Response Topic received in a request message, it MUST include the Correlation Data property from the original request unaltered. This applies specifically to MQTT v5 request-response flows where both Response Topic and Correlation Data properties are present in the original request. The Correlation Data must be copied exactly as received, without modification, to enable the original requester to correlate the response with the specific request. This requirement ensures proper request-response correlation and maintains protocol integrity, as any alteration would break the correlation mechanism and constitute a protocol violation.

The Properties field in the PUBACK packet's Variable Header must be preceded by its length, encoded as a Variable Byte Integer. This rule applies when the PUBACK packet includes properties and the Remaining Length field is 4 or greater, indicating sufficient space for properties. If the Remaining Length is less than 4, no Property Length field is present, and the properties length is implicitly 0. The encoded length must accurately represent the total byte count of all subsequent properties, ensuring correct parsing and alignment with the MQTT v5 packet structure.

When an Application Message contains both a Response Topic and Correlation Data properties, the receiving client that wishes to respond MUST use the Response Topic value as the Topic Name in its PUBLISH packet. Additionally, the client SHOULD include the same Correlation Data value unaltered in the response PUBLISH packet to enable the original sender to correlate the response with its initial request. This mechanism facilitates request-response patterns in MQTT v5, where the Correlation Data serves as a unique identifier that the requester can use to match responses to specific requests. The rule applies only when both properties are present in the received message and the receiver intends to send a response.

In the MQTT v5 PUBLISH packet properties, the Correlation Data property (identifier 0x09) must appear at most once. This property, which consists of binary data following the identifier byte, is used by the request sender to correlate response messages with their original requests. If a PUBLISH packet contains multiple Correlation Data properties, this constitutes a Protocol Error. The rule applies during packet validation when processing PUBLISH packets, particularly those used in request-response patterns. When followed correctly, the server must forward the Correlation Data unaltered to all subscribers [MQTT-3.3.2-16], and responders should include it unchanged in their response publications. This rule is conditional on the presence of Correlation Data in a PUBLISH packet and is essential for maintaining the integrity of request-response correlation mechanisms.

When a PUBLISH packet contains Correlation Data (property identifier 0x09) as part of a request-response flow, the Server MUST preserve the exact binary content of this data and forward it without modification to all subscribers that receive the Application Message [MQTT-3.3.2-16]. This rule applies specifically when the Server is distributing a message that includes both Response Topic and Correlation Data properties, ensuring that the correlation mechanism remains intact throughout message delivery. The expected outcome is that subscribers receive the identical Correlation Data that was originally provided by the requester, enabling proper request-response correlation at the application level. This requirement is conditional upon the presence of valid Correlation Data in the original PUBLISH packet and assumes the message is being delivered to authorized subscribers according to topic matching rules.

The Properties length in the PUBCOMP packet Variable Header is encoded as a Variable Byte Integer. This rule applies during the decoding of PUBCOMP packets in QoS 2 message flows. When the Remaining Length field (which indicates total packet size excluding fixed header) is less than 4 bytes, this indicates there is insufficient space for any properties data. In this case, the Property Length field is omitted from the packet and must be interpreted as having a value of 0, meaning no properties are present. This condition occurs when the PUBCOMP packet contains only the required Packet Identifier in its Variable Header without any optional properties.

The PUBACK packet in MQTT v5 must not contain a Payload section. This rule applies when a server acknowledges receipt of a PUBLISH packet with QoS level 1, or when a client acknowledges such a PUBLISH packet from the server. The expected outcome is a minimal acknowledgment packet that only includes the fixed header and PUBACK variable header (containing the Packet Identifier and Reason Code). This design ensures efficient protocol operation by eliminating unnecessary payload data in acknowledgment packets. The rule applies regardless of the Reason Code value in the PUBACK variable header, and compliance is mandatory for all MQTT v5 implementations.

In the CONNACK packet's Variable Header, Byte 2 contains the Connect Reason Code, which indicates the outcome of the connection attempt. This field applies when a server processes a well-formed CONNECT packet but cannot complete the connection establishment, or when the connection is accepted. The server MUST use one of the standardized Connect Reason Code values (as defined in Table 3-1) and MAY send a CONNACK packet with the appropriate code to inform the client of the result. If the Reason Code is 128 or greater (indicating an error), the server MUST close the network connection immediately after sending the CONNACK. Prerequisites include receiving a syntactically valid CONNECT packet; however, the server may choose to close the connection without sending a CONNACK for security reasons in certain error scenarios.

The PUBACK packet Fixed Header structure is defined in Figure 3-10 and governs the format of acknowledgment packets for QoS 1 PUBLISH messages. This rule applies when a server or client sends a PUBACK to confirm receipt of a PUBLISH packet with QoS level 1. The expected outcome is a properly formatted fixed header where Byte 1 contains the MQTT Control Packet type value 4 (identifying it as a PUBACK) along with reserved bits, and Byte 2 contains the Remaining Length field encoded as a Variable Byte Integer indicating the length of the Variable Header. Prerequisites include successful receipt of a valid PUBLISH packet with QoS 1, and the PUBACK must be sent in direct response to such a packet to complete the QoS 1 message delivery handshake.
res_fields: Byte 1: MQTT Control Packet type value 4
Byte 2: Remaining Length field encoded as Variable Byte Integer

rule: The Connect Reason Code is a mandatory field in the CONNACK packet's variable header that indicates the outcome of a connection attempt. This rule applies when a server receives a well-formed CONNECT packet but cannot complete the connection establishment. The server MUST include one of the predefined Connect Reason Code values from the specification table, which range from 0x00 (Success) to 0x9F (Connection rate exceeded). If the server sends a CONNACK with a Reason Code of 128 (0x80) or greater, indicating a failure, it MUST subsequently close the network connection. This mechanism ensures that clients receive standardized, meaningful feedback about connection status while allowing servers to handle security concerns by optionally withholding detailed error information in sensitive scenarios.

When a Server receives a well-formed CONNECT packet but cannot complete the connection establishment, it MAY send a CONNACK packet with an appropriate Connect Reason Code. If the Server chooses to send a CONNACK packet containing a Reason Code value of 128 (0x80) or greater, which indicates a connection failure condition, the Server MUST immediately close the Network Connection after sending the CONNACK packet. This requirement applies specifically to failure scenarios where the Server has determined the connection cannot proceed, such as authentication failures, protocol violations, or server resource limitations, and ensures that the connection is properly terminated when the client cannot be successfully connected.

When a Server receives a well-formed CONNECT packet but encounters a condition preventing successful connection establishment, it MAY send a CONNACK packet with a Connect Reason Code from the specified table to indicate the failure reason. This applies during the initial connection handshake after the Server has validated the packet structure but before completing the connection. The Server MUST select an appropriate Connect Reason Code value that accurately reflects the failure condition. If the Reason Code is 128 or greater, the Server MUST subsequently close the Network Connection. This rule governs the Server's optional error notification mechanism when connection completion fails despite valid packet format, providing clients with specific failure information while allowing Servers to maintain security by potentially omitting CONNACK in sensitive scenarios.

The Unspecified error reason code (value 128, hex 0x80) is used by the server when it cannot complete a connection request but either cannot disclose the specific failure reason or no other predefined reason code applies. This rule applies during the connection establishment phase when the server receives a well-formed CONNECT packet but encounters an unspecified or undisclosable error condition. When this reason code is sent in the CONNACK packet, the server MUST subsequently close the network connection as per protocol requirements for reason codes ≥128. This serves as a catch-all for connection failures where security considerations prevent detailed error disclosure or where the specific error condition isn't covered by other reason codes.

The Connect Reason Code with value 0 (0x00) named "Success" indicates that the MQTT connection has been accepted by the server. This code applies when the server successfully processes a well-formed CONNECT packet from a client and establishes the connection. The expected outcome is that the client can proceed with normal MQTT operations. Prerequisites include: the CONNECT packet must be properly formatted, the client must meet all server authentication and authorization requirements, and the server must support the requested protocol version and features. This reason code is sent in the CONNACK packet's variable header as the second byte, and when received, the client should maintain the network connection and begin MQTT session operations.

Value 129 (Hex 0x81), Reason Code "Malformed Packet" - This code is used when the server receives a CONNECT packet that contains structurally invalid or unparsable data, such as incorrect packet formatting, invalid UTF-8 encoding, or malformed variable-length fields. This rule applies during the initial connection handshake when the server processes the CONNECT packet from the client. When this reason code is sent in the CONNACK packet, the server MUST immediately close the network connection as required by [MQTT-3.2.2-7]. The prerequisite is that the server has received a CONNECT packet but cannot parse its contents according to the MQTT v5 protocol specification, indicating a fundamental protocol violation rather than a semantic error.

Value 131 (Hex 0x83), Reason Code name "Implementation specific error" - This reason code applies when a Server receives a well-formed CONNECT packet that is syntactically valid according to the MQTT specification, but cannot be processed due to server-specific implementation constraints or policies. The Server MUST send this CONNACK packet when rejecting a valid connection for implementation-specific reasons (such as resource limitations, configuration restrictions, or internal policy violations not covered by other reason codes). Following transmission of this reason code (which is ≥128), the Server MUST immediately close the network connection as required by [MQTT-3.2.2-7]. This allows servers to reject connections for legitimate operational reasons while maintaining protocol compliance, without revealing specific internal implementation details to the client.

The Unsupported Protocol Version reason code (value 132, hex 0x84) is used when a server receives a well-formed CONNECT packet but cannot accept the connection due to an incompatible MQTT protocol version specified in the client's request. This rule applies during the connection establishment phase immediately after the server validates the CONNECT packet structure. When triggered, the server MUST send a CONNACK packet containing this reason code and then close the network connection, as required for all reason codes ≥128. The prerequisite for this rule is that the server has successfully parsed a syntactically correct CONNECT packet but identified that the Protocol Version field contains a value not supported by the server implementation. This ensures clients receive clear feedback when attempting to connect with outdated or unsupported MQTT protocol versions.

Value 130 (Hex 0x82), Reason Code name Protocol Error, Description: The data in the CONNECT packet violates the MQTT v5 protocol specification in a way that makes it non-conformant, even though the packet structure itself may be syntactically correct. This applies when the server receives a CONNECT packet that is well-formed but contains values, flags, or combinations of properties that are explicitly disallowed by the specification (e.g., invalid property combinations, unsupported protocol features used incorrectly, or contradictory settings). When this rule is triggered, the server MUST send a CONNACK packet with this reason code and then close the network connection immediately, as required by [MQTT-3.2.2-7]. This rule applies only after the server has successfully parsed the CONNECT packet (distinguishing it from Malformed Packet errors) and before any authentication or authorization checks.

The Connect Reason Code value 135 (0x87) with name "Not authorized" indicates that the Client's connection request was rejected due to authorization failure. This code applies when the Server receives a well-formed CONNECT packet but determines the Client lacks necessary permissions to establish a connection, based on implementation-specific authorization policies (such as access control lists or security configurations). When this code is used, the Server MUST send it in the CONNACK packet and subsequently close the Network Connection, as required for all Reason Codes ≥128. This ensures secure termination of unauthorized connection attempts while providing the Client with a specific failure reason.

Value 134 (Hex 0x86), Reason Code name "Bad User Name or Password". This reason code applies when the Server receives a well-formed CONNECT packet but rejects the connection due to invalid authentication credentials. Specifically, the Server does not accept either the User Name or Password provided by the Client in the CONNECT packet. When this reason code is used (value ≥ 128), the Server MUST close the Network Connection immediately after sending the CONNACK packet [MQTT-3.2.2-7]. This rule applies during the connection establishment phase when the Server performs authentication validation, and it requires that the CONNECT packet includes User Name and/or Password fields as specified in the CONNECT packet structure.

Value 133 (Hex 0x85), Reason Code name "Client Identifier not valid" - This reason code applies when a Server receives a well-formed CONNECT packet but rejects the connection because the Client Identifier, while syntactically valid per MQTT string encoding rules, violates server-specific policies (such as being blacklisted, containing prohibited patterns, or exceeding length limits). This rule applies during the connection establishment phase after the Server validates the CONNECT packet structure but before accepting the connection. When this reason code is used, the Server MUST send a CONNACK packet and then close the network connection immediately, as this is a non-success reason code (≥128). Prerequisites include: the CONNECT packet must be well-formed, the Client Identifier must pass MQTT string validation, but the Server's implementation-specific validation must fail.

When a server receives a well-formed CONNECT packet but determines the client has been administratively banned, it MAY send a CONNACK packet with Reason Code 0x8A (Banned) in the Variable Header. This code specifically indicates the client has been prohibited from connecting due to administrative action, such as security policy violations or manual intervention. As this is a failure code (≥128), the server MUST subsequently close the network connection [MQTT-3.2.2-7]. This mechanism allows servers to explicitly inform clients about administrative bans while maintaining protocol compliance.

When a server receives a well-formed CONNECT packet but cannot process the connection request due to temporary resource constraints or high load, it MAY respond with a CONNACK packet containing reason code 0x89 (Server busy). This code indicates the server is temporarily unavailable and the client should retry the connection later. Following MQTT-3.2.2-7, since this reason code value (137) is greater than 128, the server MUST close the network connection immediately after sending the CONNACK packet. This rule applies during the connection establishment phase when the server validates the CONNECT packet but encounters transient operational limitations preventing connection acceptance.

The Server unavailable reason code (value 136, hex 0x88) is used when an MQTT Server receives a well-formed CONNECT packet but cannot process the connection request due to the server being temporarily or permanently unavailable. This applies during the connection establishment phase when the server determines it cannot accept the connection for reasons such as maintenance, overload, or system failure. When this reason code is sent in the CONNACK packet, the server MUST subsequently close the network connection as per [MQTT-3.2.2-7] since this is a reason code of 128 or greater. The client should interpret this as a terminal connection failure and may attempt reconnection after a reasonable delay or seek alternative servers if available.

This rule governs the server's response when a client's CONNECT packet specifies an authentication method that is either unsupported by the server or incompatible with the current authentication context. The rule applies during the connection establishment phase after the server receives a well-formed CONNECT packet but cannot proceed due to authentication method mismatch. When this rule is triggered, the server MUST send a CONNACK packet with reason code 0x8C (Bad authentication method) and subsequently close the network connection, as this is a failure code with value ≥128. This response requires that the server has received a valid CONNECT packet containing authentication method information that conflicts with server capabilities or current session state.

When a server receives a well-formed CONNECT packet but cannot complete the connection due to exceeding an implementation or administrative limit (such as maximum concurrent connections, bandwidth quotas, or resource allocation thresholds), it MUST respond with a CONNACK packet containing Reason Code 151 (0x97) "Quota exceeded". This code applies specifically when server-side constraints prevent connection establishment despite valid client credentials and packet format. Following MQTT-3.2.2-7, since this is a code ≥128, the server MUST subsequently close the network connection. This mechanism allows servers to enforce operational limits while providing clients with specific diagnostic information about connection failures.

This rule governs the server's response when a client's CONNECT packet contains a valid but unacceptable Will Topic Name. The rule applies during connection establishment when the server processes a well-formed CONNECT packet but rejects the Will Topic Name due to server-specific restrictions (such as topic naming policies, access controls, or administrative settings). When this rule is triggered, the server MUST send a CONNACK packet with Reason Code 0x90 (Topic Name invalid) and then close the network connection, as this is a non-success reason code (≥128). Prerequisites include: the CONNECT packet must be syntactically valid, the Will Topic Name must be properly formatted according to MQTT specification, but the server must determine that the topic name is not permitted for use as a Will Topic in the current context.

Value 149 (Hex 0x95), Reason Code name "Packet too large" - This reason code applies when a server receives a well-formed CONNECT packet that exceeds the maximum permissible packet size configured by the server. This rule governs the server's response during the connection establishment phase, specifically in the CONNACK packet's variable header. When this condition is met, the server MUST send a CONNACK packet with this reason code and then close the network connection, as required by [MQTT-3.2.2-7] for reason codes 128 or greater. The prerequisite is that the CONNECT packet must be syntactically correct but violates the server's size constraints, distinguishing it from malformed packet errors.

This rule governs the server's response when a client attempts to establish a connection with a Will Message that requests retention, but the server does not support retained messages. The rule applies during the connection establishment phase when the server processes a well-formed CONNECT packet and determines that the Will Retain flag is set to 1 while retained messages are unsupported. When this condition is met, the server MUST send a CONNACK packet with Reason Code 0x9A (154) and then close the network connection, as this is an error code of 128 or greater. The prerequisite is that the CONNECT packet must be syntactically valid but contains a Will Retain flag set to 1, which conflicts with the server's capability to support retained messages.

When a server receives a well-formed CONNECT packet but cannot complete the connection due to temporary service unavailability or load balancing requirements, it MAY send a CONNACK packet with reason code 0x9C (156) "Use another server". This code indicates the client should temporarily redirect to an alternative server. The server MUST close the network connection after sending this CONNACK since reason codes ≥128 require connection termination [MQTT-3.2.2-7]. This applies during the initial connection handshake when the server determines it cannot serve the client temporarily but expects service to resume later, typically used for maintenance, overload protection, or server migration scenarios.

When a server receives a well-formed CONNECT packet but cannot complete the connection due to unsupported QoS level in the Will QoS field, it MUST respond with a CONNACK packet containing Reason Code 155 (0x9B) "QoS not supported". This applies specifically when the server's implementation does not support the QoS value specified in the Will QoS parameter of the CONNECT packet. After sending this CONNACK with a Reason Code ≥ 128, the server MUST close the network connection. The client should interpret this as a permanent failure and either adjust its Will QoS settings or connect to a different server that supports the requested QoS level.

Value 153 (Hex 0x99), Reason Code name "Payload format invalid" - This reason code applies when a client's CONNECT packet includes a Will Message with a Payload Format Indicator property that specifies a particular data format (such as UTF-8 encoded character data), but the actual Will Payload content does not conform to that specified format. The server MUST send this reason code in the CONNACK packet and then close the network connection, as this is a protocol violation. This validation occurs during the connection establishment phase when the server processes the CONNECT packet and validates the Will Message properties against the actual payload content. Prerequisites include: the CONNECT packet must be well-formed, the client must have included a Will Message with both a Payload Format Indicator property and a Will Payload, and the server must support payload format validation.

Value 159 (Hex 0x9F), Reason Code name "Connection rate exceeded", Description "The connection rate limit has been exceeded." This reason code applies when a server receives a well-formed CONNECT packet but cannot complete the connection due to exceeding an implementation-specific or administrative connection rate threshold. The server MUST send this CONNACK packet with a reason code ≥ 128 and then immediately close the network connection as required by [MQTT-3.2.2-7]. This mechanism allows servers to protect against excessive connection attempts while providing clients with specific feedback about rate limiting conditions.

Value 157 (Hex 0x9D), Reason Code "Server moved" - This code is used by a server in the CONNACK packet when it receives a well-formed CONNECT packet but cannot complete the connection because the client should permanently redirect to a different server. This applies during the initial connection handshake when the server determines it is no longer the appropriate endpoint for the client's persistent connection needs. When this reason code is sent (≥128), the server MUST close the network connection immediately after sending the CONNACK packet. The client receiving this code should update its configuration to permanently use the alternative server specified in the response properties (if provided) and should not attempt to reconnect to the original server for future sessions. This differs from the temporary redirection code (0x9C) in that it indicates a permanent server relocation.

When a server responds to a CONNECT packet with a CONNACK packet, it MUST populate the Connect Reason Code field in the variable header exclusively with one of the standardized Connect Reason Code values defined in the MQTT v5 specification. This rule applies regardless of whether the connection is accepted or rejected, and ensures that clients receive consistent, interpretable status information. The server MUST NOT use any values outside the specified range (0-0x9F) for this purpose. If the server sends a CONNACK with a Reason Code of 128 or greater (indicating failure), it MUST subsequently close the network connection as required by [MQTT-3.2.2-7].

When a Server receives a well-formed CONNECT packet but cannot complete the connection, it MAY send a CONNACK packet with a Connect Reason Code indicating the failure. Reason Code 0x80 (Unspecified error) is used in two specific scenarios: (1) when the Server identifies the failure reason but opts not to disclose it to the Client for security or privacy reasons, or (2) when the failure does not correspond to any other defined Reason Code. This rule applies during the connection establishment phase after the Server validates the CONNECT packet structure. If the Server sends a CONNACK with Reason Code 0x80 (or any code ≥ 128), it MUST subsequently close the Network Connection as per [MQTT-3.2.2-7]. The use of this code ensures that the Server can handle connection failures gracefully without exposing sensitive implementation details.

When a Server receives a CONNECT packet and identifies an error condition, it MAY choose to immediately close the Network Connection without sending a CONNACK packet. This behavior applies specifically during the connection establishment phase before any CONNACK response is transmitted. The expected outcome is enhanced security by preventing potential information disclosure about the server's MQTT capabilities or specific error conditions to unauthorized or malicious clients. This rule applies when the Server determines that sending a CONNACK could compromise security, such as when operating on public networks or when the connection attempt appears unauthorized or suspicious. This is an optional security measure that takes precedence over the normal CONNACK response mechanism.

A PUBREC packet MUST be sent by a receiver in response to a valid PUBLISH packet with QoS level 2, confirming receipt and initiating the second phase of the four-step QoS 2 handshake. This applies only when the PUBLISH packet specifies QoS 2 and passes validation checks. The expected outcome is that the sender, upon receiving PUBREC, will proceed to send a PUBREL packet to continue the message delivery guarantee process.

The PUBREC packet is the second packet in the QoS 2 protocol exchange, sent by the receiver in response to a PUBLISH packet with QoS level 2. This acknowledgment confirms successful receipt of the message and initiates the second phase of the four-step handshake, ensuring reliable message delivery by preventing duplicate processing while maintaining the message in a pending state until the exchange completes.

The Variable Header of the PUBACK Packet, which is sent by the receiver to acknowledge receipt of a PUBLISH packet with QoS 1, must contain the following fields in strict order: the Packet Identifier from the original PUBLISH packet being acknowledged, a PUBACK Reason Code indicating the outcome of the publish operation, the Property Length indicating the size of subsequent properties, and the Properties themselves. This structure applies when a client or server receives a PUBLISH packet with QoS level 1 and needs to confirm processing. The expected outcome is proper acknowledgment of message receipt, enabling the sender to clear the message from its retransmission queue. Prerequisites include successful receipt of a valid PUBLISH packet with QoS 1 and correct packet identifier matching between PUBLISH and PUBACK packets.

The PUBACK packet Variable Header structure specifies the exact byte-level layout for acknowledging receipt of a QoS 1 PUBLISH packet. This structure applies when a server or client sends a PUBACK response after processing a PUBLISH packet, and must contain: Byte 1 as the Packet Identifier MSB, Byte 2 as the Packet Identifier LSB (together forming the 16-bit identifier matching the original PUBLISH), Byte 3 as the PUBACK Reason Code indicating the acknowledgment outcome, and Byte 4 as the Property Length specifying the size of subsequent properties. The expected outcome is proper identification of the acknowledged message and communication of its processing status. Prerequisites include receiving a valid PUBLISH packet with QoS level 1 and successfully parsing its packet identifier, with properties following this header structure as defined in section 2.2.2.

In the MQTT v5 protocol, when constructing a PUBACK packet to acknowledge receipt of a PUBLISH message, the Reason String property (identifier 0x1F) may be optionally included to provide human-readable diagnostic information. This rule specifies that including the Reason String property more than once within the same PUBACK packet constitutes a Protocol Error. This applies during packet construction before transmission, and if violated, the receiver must treat it as a malformed packet, which may result in connection termination. The rule applies regardless of whether the Reason String values are identical or different, and compliance is required whenever the property is used, provided the packet size does not exceed the receiver's Maximum Packet Size limit.

When constructing a PUBACK packet, the sender MAY include a Reason String property (identifier 0x1F followed by a UTF-8 encoded diagnostic string) to provide human-readable diagnostic information to the receiver. However, this property MUST be omitted if its inclusion would cause the total PUBACK packet size to exceed the Maximum Packet Size established by the receiver during connection negotiation. This size validation MUST be performed before packet transmission to ensure compliance with the receiver's stated capacity limits. The sender MUST NOT truncate or modify the Reason String to fit within size constraints; instead, the entire property must be excluded when space constraints prevent its full inclusion. This rule applies specifically to PUBACK packet construction following publish receipt and takes precedence over the diagnostic value of the Reason String property.

In the AUTH packet's variable header properties, the User Property (identifier 0x26) may be included multiple times to represent distinct name-value pairs, where each occurrence consists of a UTF-8 string pair. This allows the sender to provide multiple diagnostic or informational attributes. The rule applies when constructing AUTH packets for authentication exchange, provided that including all User Properties does not cause the packet size to exceed the receiver's specified Maximum Packet Size. When followed, the recipient must process all name-value pairs, including those with duplicate names, as valid authentication-related metadata.

In the SUBACK packet's variable header, when the User Property identifier (0x26) is present, it must be immediately followed by a UTF-8 String Pair consisting of a name and value. This property enables the server to include supplementary diagnostic or informational metadata in the subscription acknowledgement. The rule applies during SUBACK packet construction when the server includes user properties, but only if their inclusion does not cause the packet size to exceed the client-specified Maximum Packet Size. Multiple User Property entries are permitted within a single SUBACK packet, including duplicate names, to convey multiple name-value pairs. The UTF-8 String Pair must maintain proper encoding and length formatting as specified by the MQTT v5 string data type requirements.

When constructing a PUBACK packet, the sender MAY include User Property fields to provide diagnostic or other supplementary information. However, before including any User Property, the sender MUST verify that the total packet size including all properties does not exceed the Maximum Packet Size established by the receiver during connection negotiation. If adding a User Property would cause the PUBACK packet to exceed this limit, the sender MUST omit that User Property entirely. This size validation applies to each User Property individually during packet assembly, and the sender should prioritize essential properties when approaching the size limit. The rule ensures that PUBACK packets remain within the receiver's declared capacity while maintaining protocol compliance [MQTT-3.4.2-3].

In the UNSUBACK packet's variable header properties, the User Property (identifier 0x26) may contain multiple name-value pairs where the same property name can be repeated with different or identical values. This allows for representing multiple instances of diagnostic or supplementary information under the same property name. This rule applies when the Server includes User Properties in the UNSUBACK response, provided that the total packet size does not exceed the Client's specified Maximum Packet Size. When implemented, receivers must process all occurrences of the repeated property name rather than treating duplicates as errors.

In the MQTT v5 protocol, the PUBREC packet's Variable Header includes an optional Properties section whose length must be encoded as a Variable Byte Integer. This rule applies when processing a PUBREC packet during QoS 2 message flow, specifically in the "Publish received" phase. The expected outcome is correct parsing of the Properties length, enabling proper handling of subsequent properties. A prerequisite is that the Remaining Length field must be at least 4 bytes; otherwise, the Property Length is implicitly 0 and no length bytes are present.

In the PUBLISH packet's Variable Header Properties, the Topic Alias property (identifier 0x23) must appear at most once. Including multiple instances of this property in a single PUBLISH packet constitutes a Protocol Error. This rule applies during packet construction and validation when the sender includes topic alias mappings to optimize payload size. When violated, the receiver must treat the packet as malformed and close the network connection as specified in the MQTT v5 protocol error handling requirements. This restriction exists to prevent ambiguity in topic alias resolution and ensure consistent packet processing.

In the MQTT v5 PUBREL packet Variable Header, the Properties length is normally encoded as a Variable Byte Integer. However, when the Remaining Length field of the PUBREL packet is less than 4 bytes, this indicates that the packet contains only the fixed header and Packet Identifier, with no space for Properties. In this case, the Properties section is omitted entirely, no Property Length field is present, and the effective Property Length value is treated as 0. This rule applies during PUBREL packet parsing when implementing QoS 2 message flow, and ensures proper handling of minimal PUBREL packets that carry only essential delivery control information without additional properties.

A Topic Alias is an integer value greater than 0 that serves as a substitute for the full Topic Name in PUBLISH packets, reducing packet size when long or repetitive Topic Names are used within a Network Connection. This mechanism applies during the PUBLISH packet exchange after a Topic Alias mapping has been established by the sender including both a non-zero length Topic Name and the Topic Alias in a previous PUBLISH packet. Once mapped, subsequent PUBLISH packets may use the Topic Alias with a zero-length Topic Name, and the receiver must resolve it to the previously associated Topic Name. The sender may update the mapping by sending another PUBLISH with the same Topic Alias and a different non-zero length Topic Name. Topic Alias values must not exceed the Topic Alias Maximum negotiated during connection establishment, and all mappings are connection-specific and must not persist beyond the current Network Connection.

The Topic Alias mechanism allows replacing long Topic Names with shorter integer identifiers in PUBLISH packets, reducing packet size when the same topics are reused within a single Network Connection. This applies when a sender establishes a Topic Alias mapping by including both a non-zero length Topic Name and a Topic Alias value in a PUBLISH packet. Once mapped, subsequent PUBLISH packets can use the Topic Alias with a zero-length Topic Name, and the receiver will substitute the mapped Topic Name. The expected outcome is optimized bandwidth usage without compromising message routing accuracy. Prerequisites include: the Topic Alias must be a non-zero value within the negotiated maximum limits (as established in CONNECT/CONNACK), mappings are connection-specific and non-persistent, and the sender must not include duplicate Topic Alias properties in a single packet.

When a receiver processes a PUBLISH packet that contains both a non-zero length Topic Name and a valid Topic Alias value (greater than 0 and within the negotiated Topic Alias Maximum), it must perform two actions: first, process the PUBLISH message normally according to standard PUBLISH handling rules, and second, establish or update a Topic Alias mapping by associating the specified Topic Alias value with the provided Topic Name. This mapping enables subsequent PUBLISH packets within the same Network Connection to use this Topic Alias with a zero-length Topic Name. The receiver must not carry forward this mapping to other Network Connections, and the mapping remains valid only for the duration of the current connection.

After a Topic Alias mapping has been established at the receiver (by previously receiving a PUBLISH packet containing both a non-zero length Topic Name and the corresponding Topic Alias value), a sender may transmit subsequent PUBLISH packets that include only that Topic Alias value and a zero-length Topic Name field. The receiver must then substitute the mapped Topic Name associated with that Topic Alias when processing the packet. This optimization reduces packet size for repeated publications to the same topic, but requires that the Topic Alias value is non-zero, falls within the negotiated Topic Alias Maximum limits, and that the mapping was previously established within the current Network Connection.

Topic Alias mappings are established within a single Network Connection to reduce PUBLISH packet size by substituting integer aliases for topic names. These mappings are transient and connection-specific. When a Network Connection is terminated or replaced, the receiver MUST discard all previously established Topic Alias mappings and MUST NOT reuse or carry them forward to any subsequent Network Connection, even if reconnecting to the same peer. This ensures that each connection starts with a clean alias state and prevents potential misinterpretation of topic references across separate sessions [MQTT-3.3.2-7].

When establishing or updating a Topic Alias mapping within an MQTT v5 session, the sender MUST include both a non-zero length Topic Name and a valid Topic Alias value in the PUBLISH packet. This rule applies during the initial setup or modification of Topic Alias mappings to enable subsequent PUBLISH packets to use the alias in place of the full topic name. The receiver MUST process the packet normally and MUST store the mapping between the provided Topic Alias and Topic Name for the duration of the Network Connection. This mapping allows future PUBLISH packets from the same sender to use the Topic Alias with a zero-length Topic Name, reducing packet size. Prerequisites include: the Topic Alias value MUST be non-zero, MUST NOT exceed the negotiated Topic Alias Maximum (as established in CONNECT/CONNACK), and MUST NOT be duplicated within the same packet.

When a receiver has previously established a Topic Alias mapping for a given integer value (greater than 0 and within the negotiated Topic Alias Maximum), and receives a PUBLISH packet containing that Topic Alias with a zero-length Topic Name field, the receiver MUST process the packet as if it contained the full Topic Name associated with that Topic Alias mapping. This rule applies only after a valid Topic Alias mapping has been established through a prior PUBLISH packet containing both a non-zero length Topic Name and the corresponding Topic Alias, and only within the same Network Connection where the mapping was created. The expected outcome is that the receiver reconstructs the complete PUBLISH message using the mapped Topic Name, enabling efficient message processing while maintaining semantic equivalence to a full-length Topic Name transmission.

When using the Topic Alias property in a PUBLISH packet, the sender MUST NOT specify a Topic Alias value of 0, as this value is reserved and invalid for topic alias mapping. This rule applies whenever a sender includes the Topic Alias property in any PUBLISH packet, regardless of whether it is establishing a new alias mapping or using an existing one. Following this rule ensures that receivers can properly resolve topic aliases to their corresponding topic names, as the value 0 cannot be used to identify any valid topic mapping. This restriction applies in all scenarios where topic aliases used and is independent of the Topic Alias Maximum negotiated during connection establishment.

Within a single Network Connection, if a sender has previously established a Topic Alias mapping by sending a PUBLISH packet with a non-zero length Topic Name and a specific Topic Alias value, the sender may update that mapping by sending a subsequent PUBLISH packet over the same connection using the same Topic Alias value but with a different non-zero length Topic Name. Upon receiving such a packet, the receiver must update its Topic Alias mapping to associate the specified Topic Alias with the new Topic Name provided. This mechanism allows dynamic remapping of Topic Aliases during the connection lifetime, but does not permit reusing a Topic Alias value for multiple mappings simultaneously. The Topic Alias must be valid (non-zero and within the negotiated maximum), and the Topic Name must be non-zero in length for mapping creation or modification.

After a successful connection establishment where the Server has provided a Topic Alias Maximum value in the CONNACK packet, a Client MUST NOT use any Topic Alias value exceeding this maximum in any PUBLISH packet sent during the network connection. This restriction applies throughout the connection lifetime and ensures the Client respects the Server's advertised capacity for topic alias mappings. Violating this constraint constitutes a protocol error, which may result in connection termination by the Server. The rule applies specifically when the Client chooses to use topic aliasing for PUBLISH packets and is conditional upon the Server having communicated a non-zero Topic Alias Maximum during the connection handshake. [MQTT-3.3.2-9]

When a Client receives a PUBLISH packet from a Server, it MUST accept all Topic Alias values greater than 0 and less than or equal to the Topic Alias Maximum value that the Client originally specified in its CONNECT packet. This requirement applies specifically to incoming PUBLISH packets where the Server uses Topic Aliases to reference previously established topic mappings. The Client must process these aliases as valid references to their corresponding topic names, provided the alias values fall within the negotiated range. This rule ensures proper handling of topic alias mappings that were established during the connection lifetime and prevents the Client from rejecting valid alias values that comply with the previously agreed-upon maximum limit.

During an established MQTT session, when a Server sends PUBLISH packets using Topic Aliases to reduce packet size, it MUST NOT assign any Topic Alias value exceeding the Topic Alias Maximum value that the Client previously specified in its CONNECT packet. This rule applies specifically to the Server's PUBLISH packet generation after the CONNECT/CONNACK handshake is complete, ensuring that the Server respects the Client's declared capacity for Topic Alias mappings. Compliance prevents protocol errors and maintains session integrity, while violation constitutes a protocol error that may lead to connection termination. This requirement is conditional on the Client having provided a valid Topic Alias Maximum value in its CONNECT packet; if none was provided, the Server MUST NOT use Topic Aliases in its PUBLISH packets.

The Topic Alias mappings used by the Client and Server are independent from each other. Therefore, when a Client sends a PUBLISH packet containing a Topic Alias value of 1 to a Server, and the Server sends a PUBLISH packet with a Topic Alias value of 1 to that same Client, these aliases will generally refer to different Topics. This independence applies during the lifetime of the Network Connection and requires that both Client and Server maintain separate Topic Alias mappings according to their respective Topic Alias Maximum values exchanged during connection establishment.

When processing PUBLISH packets from a Client, a Server MUST accept all Topic Alias values that are greater than 0 and less than or equal to the Topic Alias Maximum value that the Server previously specified in its CONNACK packet. This rule applies after the Server has established a Network Connection with the Client and returned a CONNACK containing a Topic Alias Maximum property. The expected outcome is that the Server will properly handle valid Topic Alias values within the negotiated range, enabling efficient topic name substitution.
