When a Server sends a PUBLISH packet to a Client as a result of matching subscriptions, it MUST include the Subscription Identifier(s) that were originally specified in the SUBSCRIBE packet(s) that caused the message delivery. This applies specifically when the Client has made overlapping subscriptions with Subscription Identifiers, and the Server MUST include all relevant Subscription Identifiers either in a single PUBLISH packet (if sending one copy) or in each respective PUBLISH packet (if sending multiple copies). The PUBLISH packet MUST NOT contain any Subscription Identifiers other than those received in the triggering SUBSCRIBE packets, and Client-to-Server PUBLISH packets MUST NOT contain Subscription Identifiers at all.

When a Client has multiple active subscriptions with wildcard-based Topic Filters that overlap, a single published message may match multiple subscription filters. In such cases, the Server MUST deliver the message to the Client using the highest QoS level among all matching subscriptions. Additionally, the Server MAY deliver duplicate copies of the message—one for each additional matching subscription—using the QoS level specific to each subscription. If Subscription Identifiers were provided in the overlapping subscriptions, the Server MUST include all relevant identifiers in the PUBLISH packet(s) according to the delivery method used. This rule applies during the PUBLISH packet processing phase when distributing Application Messages to Clients with active subscriptions.

When a Server receives an Application Message from a publishing Client, it MUST use PUBLISH packets to deliver that message to every connected Client that has an active subscription with a Topic Filter matching the message's topic name. This rule applies during message distribution after the Server has received and validated a PUBLISH packet from a publishing Client. The expected outcome is that each subscribed Client receives the message with appropriate QoS handling, Subscription Identifiers (if present in the original subscription), and topic alias processing as specified. Prerequisites include: the receiving Clients must have established valid subscriptions, the message topic must match the subscription filters, and all protocol-level validations (such as QoS limits, receive maximum constraints, and topic alias rules) must be satisfied.

When a Client has multiple overlapping subscriptions that match the same PUBLISH message, the Server MUST deliver at least one copy of the message to the Client using the maximum QoS among all matching subscriptions. Additionally, the Server MAY deliver further copies of the message—one for each additional matching subscription—where each copy respects the specific QoS level of that individual subscription. This optional behavior applies when a Client's subscriptions contain wildcards or overlapping topic filters that result in multiple matches for the same message, and it allows the Server to provide separate message deliveries per subscription, each with its own QoS handling and potential inclusion of distinct Subscription Identifiers as required by the protocol.

When a Client has multiple overlapping subscriptions (where a single published message matches multiple Topic Filters) and has specified one or more Subscription Identifiers in the corresponding SUBSCRIBE packets, the Server MUST include all applicable Subscription Identifiers in the PUBLISH packet(s) delivered to the Client. This applies during message distribution when the Server sends Application Messages matching the Client's subscriptions. The Server MUST include Subscription Identifiers for all matching subscriptions that had identifiers specified, regardless of whether it sends a single consolidated PUBLISH packet or multiple individual PUBLISH packets. This ensures the Client can identify which specific subscriptions triggered the message delivery, maintaining correlation between subscriptions and received messages.

When a Client has multiple overlapping subscriptions with different QoS levels that match the same PUBLISH message, the Server MUST deliver a single copy of the message to the Client using the highest QoS level among all matching subscriptions. This rule applies during message distribution from Server to Client when wildcard subscriptions result in multiple subscription matches for the same message. The expected outcome is that the Client receives the message with the maximum QoS guarantee available through its subscriptions, ensuring optimal delivery reliability without duplicate messages at different QoS levels. This behavior requires that the Client has active subscriptions with overlapping topic filters and that the published message matches multiple of these filters.

This rule governs the handling of unsolicited PUBLISH packets received by an MQTT v5 Client when the QoS level exceeds the Client's configured Maximum QoS. It applies specifically when a Client receives an Application Message that was not requested through any active subscription (e.g., due to protocol error, misconfiguration, or malicious activity). The Client MUST detect this condition by comparing the received packet's QoS against its Maximum QoS setting, and if the received QoS is higher, the Client MUST immediately terminate the connection by sending a DISCONNECT packet with Reason Code 0x9B (QoS not supported) as specified in section 4.13. This ensures protocol compliance and prevents processing of messages that exceed the Client's QoS capabilities. The rule assumes the Client has already established a valid connection with a defined Maximum QoS parameter and applies throughout the connection lifetime whenever unsolicited PUBLISH packets are received.

When a Server sends a PUBLISH packet to a Client as a result of matching subscriptions, the Subscription Identifiers included in the PUBLISH packet MUST be exactly those that were received in the SUBSCRIBE packets that caused this message to be delivered. This rule applies specifically to Server-to-Client PUBLISH packets resulting from subscriptions and ensures that Clients only receive Subscription Identifiers they previously specified. Any Subscription Identifier in a PUBLISH packet that does not correspond to an identifier from a SUBSCRIBE packet that matched the publication constitutes a Protocol Error. Note that this rule does not apply to Client-to-Server PUBLISH packets, which MUST NOT contain any Subscription Identifiers.

When a Client has multiple overlapping subscriptions that match the same publication and uses the same Subscription Identifier for more than one of these subscriptions, the Server MUST include all matching Subscription Identifiers in the PUBLISH packet. In such cases, the PUBLISH packet will contain multiple identical Subscription Identifiers, which is permitted behavior. This rule applies when the Server is delivering a message that matches multiple subscriptions from the same Client, and the Client has assigned identical Subscription Identifiers to those subscriptions. The expected outcome is that the Client receives the Subscription Identifiers corresponding to all matching subscriptions, allowing it to identify which subscriptions triggered the message delivery. This behavior is subject to the condition that the Client has previously established these subscriptions with identical Subscription Identifiers via SUBSCRIBE packets.

When a Client has multiple overlapping subscriptions with identical Subscription Identifiers that match the same published message, the Server MUST include all matching Subscription Identifiers in the resulting PUBLISH packet(s). This occurs specifically when the Server delivers a single copy of the message to the Client, as required by [MQTT-3.3.4-4], and the Client has used the same Subscription Identifier value for multiple subscriptions that all match the publication. The resulting PUBLISH packet will contain multiple identical Subscription Identifier entries, which the Client MUST accept as valid. This behavior only applies to Server-to-Client PUBLISH packets and requires that the overlapping subscriptions were previously established through valid SUBSCRIBE packets containing the identical Subscription Identifiers.

When a Server delivers a single copy of a PUBLISH message to a Client that has multiple overlapping subscriptions matching the message, and the Server chooses not to send duplicate messages for each matching subscription, it MUST include all Subscription Identifiers from those matching subscriptions that specified a Subscription Identifier in the SUBSCRIBE packet. This applies specifically when the Client has multiple subscriptions with Subscription Identifiers that all match the published message topic, and the Server opts to send only one PUBLISH packet instead of multiple copies. The Subscription Identifiers must be included as properties in the PUBLISH packet, though their ordering within the packet is not significant. This ensures the Client can correlate the received message with the specific subscriptions that caused its delivery, even when receiving a consolidated message instead of individual copies for each subscription.

When a Server sends multiple PUBLISH packets for the same Application Message to a Client due to overlapping subscriptions, and the Client has specified Subscription Identifiers for those subscriptions, the Server MUST include the corresponding Subscription Identifier in each PUBLISH packet that results from a subscription with a Subscription Identifier. This applies specifically when the Server chooses to send separate copies of the message for each matching subscription rather than consolidating them into a single PUBLISH packet. The expected outcome is that each PUBLISH packet clearly identifies which specific subscription triggered its delivery, enabling the Client to correlate incoming messages with their originating subscriptions. This rule only applies when: (1) multiple Client subscriptions match the published message, (2) the Server elects to send multiple PUBLISH packets rather than a single consolidated packet, and (3) the matching subscriptions have associated Subscription Identifiers.

When a Client sends a PUBLISH packet to a Server, the packet MUST NOT include any Subscription Identifier property. This rule applies specifically to the client-to-server publication flow, where the Client is originating application messages for distribution to subscribers. The Subscription Identifier mechanism is designed exclusively for server-to-client communications, allowing Clients to identify which subscription(s) triggered message delivery when multiple subscriptions match. Including a Subscription Identifier in client-originated PUBLISH packets would be a protocol violation, as this property should only be present in PUBLISH packets sent from Server to Client that result from matching subscriptions. This restriction ensures proper protocol semantics where Subscription Identifiers are only used to correlate delivered messages back to their originating subscriptions.

When a PUBLISH packet is being delivered to a client through a shared subscription, the server MUST include only those Subscription Identifiers that were originally specified by that receiving client in its SUBSCRIBE packet. This rule applies specifically to shared subscription scenarios where multiple clients may subscribe to the same topic filter through a shared subscription group. The server MUST NOT include Subscription Identifiers from other clients in the shared subscription group, even if they also match the publication. This ensures that each client receiving the message only sees its own subscription context, maintaining proper message isolation within shared subscription mechanisms.

When a recipient (either Client or Server) receives a PUBLISH packet, the specific response actions and message handling procedures are determined by the QoS level specified in the packet header, as detailed in section 4.3. This rule applies during normal message delivery flow after successful subscription matching and governs how the recipient must process the message based on QoS 0 (at most once), QoS 1 (at least once), or QoS 2 (exactly once) delivery semantics. The expected outcome is proper message acknowledgment and delivery according to the QoS guarantees, including sending appropriate response packets (PUBACK, PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP) as required. Prerequisites include valid subscription matching, proper Topic Alias handling if present, and adherence to flow control limits for QoS 1 and 2 messages.

When processing a PUBLISH packet containing a Topic Alias, the receiver must validate the alias value against the established Maximum Topic Alias limit. This rule applies during the PUBLISH packet processing phase, after connection establishment and topic alias capability negotiation. If the Topic Alias value is 0 or exceeds the Maximum Topic Alias value configured for the connection, this constitutes a Protocol Error. In such cases, the receiver must immediately terminate the connection by sending a DISCONNECT packet with Reason Code 0x94 (Topic Alias invalid) as specified in section 4.13. This validation ensures that topic aliases remain within the negotiated bounds and prevents invalid alias references in message routing.

When processing a PUBLISH packet containing a Topic Alias for which no prior mapping exists, if the packet includes a Topic Name with non-zero length, the receiver MUST use that Topic Name to process the message and MUST establish a new Topic Alias mapping by associating the specified Topic Alias with the provided Topic Name. This rule applies specifically during the initial establishment of Topic Alias mappings and ensures that subsequent PUBLISH packets with the same Topic Alias can use zero-length Topic Names, provided the mapping remains valid.

During MQTT v5 communication, when a Client sends QoS 1 or QoS 2 PUBLISH packets to the Server, it MUST NOT exceed the Receive Maximum value (established during connection setup) for unacknowledged packets. This limit applies specifically to PUBLISH packets for which the Client has not yet received a corresponding PUBACK, PUBCOMP, or PUBREC response with a Reason Code of 128 or greater (indicating successful processing). This flow control mechanism prevents network congestion and ensures reliable message delivery by limiting the number of in-flight QoS 1 and QoS 2 messages. The rule applies throughout the session once the CONNACK is received and remains in effect until all pending acknowledgments are processed. If this limit is exceeded, the Server MUST respond with a DISCONNECT packet containing Reason Code 0x93 (Receive Maximum exceeded).

During the connection establishment phase, before a Client has received a CONNACK packet from the Server, the Client MUST NOT send any QoS 1 or QoS 2 PUBLISH packets. This rule applies because the Client has not yet established the Server's Receive Maximum value, which defines the maximum number of unacknowledged QoS 1 and QoS 2 publications allowed in flight. If the Client violates this by sending such PUBLISH packets prematurely, it risks exceeding the Server's actual Receive Maximum limit, which may cause the Server to disconnect the Client with Reason Code 0x93 (Receive Maximum exceeded). The expected behavior is for the Client to wait until after successful CONNACK reception, where it learns the Server's flow control parameters, before initiating any reliable message publications.

This rule governs flow control for QoS 1 and 2 PUBLISH packets in MQTT v5. It applies when a Server receives PUBLISH packets from a Client during an active connection. The rule triggers when the number of unacknowledged QoS 1 and 2 PUBLISH packets (those for which the Server has not yet sent PUBACK or PUBCOMP responses) exceeds the Receive Maximum value negotiated during connection establishment. When this limit is exceeded, the Server MUST immediately terminate the connection by sending a DISCONNECT packet with Reason Code 0x93 (Receive Maximum exceeded) as specified in section 4.13. This enforcement ensures proper flow control and prevents resource exhaustion on the Server side. Note that this rule does not apply to QoS 0 PUBLISH packets, and the Receive Maximum value is specific to the current Network Connection.

During MQTT v5 flow control, when a Client has established a Receive Maximum value through the CONNECT/CONNACK exchange, the Client has the discretion to send fewer QoS 1 or QoS 2 PUBLISH packets than the Receive Maximum limit, even when it has additional messages queued for transmission. This applies specifically to unacknowledged PUBLISH packets (those pending PUBACK, PUBREC, or PUBCOMP responses) and allows the Client to implement application-level pacing or resource management strategies. The expected outcome is that the Client maintains compliance with the protocol's flow control requirements while optimizing its transmission behavior based on local conditions, without triggering the "Receive Maximum exceeded" error condition. This rule applies throughout the session once the Receive Maximum is established and is particularly relevant when the Client needs to manage network bandwidth, processing load, or message prioritization.

When a Client has reached its Receive Maximum limit for unacknowledged QoS 1 or QoS 2 PUBLISH packets, it MUST NOT delay the transmission of any other control packet types (such as CONNECT, SUBSCRIBE, UNSUBSCRIBE, PINGREQ, or DISCONNECT) while waiting for acknowledgements. This rule applies specifically during flow control scenarios where the Client has sent the maximum permitted number of unacknowledged PUBLISH packets and ensures that non-PUBLISH protocol operations can proceed without interruption. The expected outcome is that essential connection management and control operations remain responsive even when PUBLISH flow control is active. This rule is conditional on the Client having reached its Receive Maximum limit for QoS 1 or QoS 2 PUBLISH packets without receiving corresponding PUBACK, PUBREC, or PUBCOMP acknowledgements. [MQTT-3.3.4-8]

During the flow control of QoS 1 and QoS 2 PUBLISH packets from Server to Client, the Server MUST NOT exceed the Receive Maximum value (established during connection setup) for in-flight messages. This applies specifically to PUBLISH packets for which the Server has not yet received successful acknowledgments (PUBACK for QoS 1, or PUBREC/PUBCOMP for QoS 2) from the Client, where "successful" means the acknowledgment packet contains a Reason Code less than 128. If the Client receives more than Receive Maximum unacknowledged QoS 1 or QoS 2 PUBLISH packets, it MUST respond with DISCONNECT using Reason Code 0x93 (Receive Maximum exceeded). This flow control mechanism ensures reliable message delivery while preventing network congestion and resource exhaustion, and applies throughout the connection lifetime after the CONNACK exchange is complete.

The DISCONNECT packet in MQTT v5 must not contain any payload data. This rule applies when a client or server sends a DISCONNECT packet to gracefully terminate an MQTT session. The expected outcome is that the receiver processes the disconnect notification without attempting to parse any payload content. This requirement applies unconditionally to all DISCONNECT packets in the protocol, regardless of whether the disconnect is initiated by client or server, and ensures consistent handling across implementations. Any DISCONNECT packet containing payload data MUST be treated as malformed according to the protocol specification.

This rule governs the processing of Topic Aliases in PUBLISH packets, which are used to optimize bandwidth by allowing topic names to be replaced with numeric aliases after initial mapping. The rule applies when a PUBLISH packet contains a valid Topic Alias (non-zero and within the Maximum Topic Alias limit established during connection setup).

Prerequisites: The Topic Alias must be valid (non-zero and ≤ Maximum Topic Alias), and the receiver must have successfully processed the packet through prior validation steps.

Processing logic:
1. If the receiver has an existing mapping for the Topic Alias:
   a) When the packet contains a zero-length Topic Name, the receiver MUST use the previously mapped Topic Name for message processing
   b) When the packet contains a non-zero length Topic Name, the receiver MUST use the new Topic Name for processing and MUST update its Topic Alias mapping to this new value

2. If no mapping exists for the Topic Alias:
   a) When the packet contains a zero-length Topic Name, this constitutes a Protocol Error - the receiver MUST send a DISCONNECT with Reason Code 0x82 (Protocol Error) and terminate the connection
   b) When the packet contains a non-zero length Topic Name, the receiver MUST use that Topic Name for processing and MUST establish a new Topic Alias mapping using the provided Topic Name

Expected outcome: Proper message routing using either existing or newly established topic mappings, with protocol-compliant error handling for invalid alias usage scenarios.

This rule governs flow control for QoS 1 and QoS 2 message delivery by enforcing the Receive Maximum limit negotiated during connection establishment. The rule applies when a Client receives PUBLISH packets from the Server and maintains an active count of unacknowledged QoS 1 and QoS 2 messages. Specifically, if the number of received QoS 1 and QoS 2 PUBLISH packets for which the Client has not yet sent corresponding PUBACK (for QoS 1) or PUBCOMP (for QoS 2) responses exceeds the Receive Maximum value, the Client MUST immediately terminate the connection by sending a DISCONNECT packet with Reason Code 0x93 (Receive Maximum exceeded). This enforcement ensures that message flow does not overwhelm the Client's processing capacity and maintains protocol integrity. The rule applies throughout the session after CONNACK is received and requires that the Client track pending acknowledgments against the established Receive Maximum limit.

When a Server has reached its Receive Maximum limit for unacknowledged QoS 1 or QoS 2 PUBLISH packets sent to a Client, it MUST NOT delay the transmission of any other packet types (such as CONNACK, SUBACK, UNSUBACK, PINGRESP, or DISCONNECT) while waiting for acknowledgements. This rule applies specifically during flow control scenarios where the Server has sent the maximum permitted number of unacknowledged PUBLISH packets to a Client. The expected outcome is that non-PUBLISH packet processing and transmission continue unimpeded, ensuring timely delivery of control packets and maintaining protocol responsiveness even when PUBLISH flow control is active. This rule only applies when the Server has actually reached its Receive Maximum limit for unacknowledged QoS 1 or QoS 2 PUBLISH packets. [MQTT-3.3.4-10]

In the SUBACK packet payload, each Reason Code MUST correspond to a specific Topic Filter from the SUBSCRIBE packet being acknowledged, maintaining the exact same order as the Topic Filters appeared in the original subscription request. This rule applies when the server processes a valid SUBSCRIBE packet and generates the corresponding SUBACK response. The expected outcome is that clients can precisely map each subscription result to its original request, enabling proper subscription state management. Prerequisites include: the SUBSCRIBE packet must contain one or more Topic Filters, the packet must pass initial validation, and the server must generate a SUBACK packet in response. Each Reason Code indicates the subscription outcome for its corresponding Topic Filter, whether successful (with granted QoS level) or failed (with specific error code), and there must be exactly one Reason Code per Topic Filter.

The UNSUBACK packet payload MUST contain a list of one-byte unsigned Reason Codes, with exactly one Reason Code corresponding to each Topic Filter in the UNSUBSCRIBE packet being acknowledged. The order of Reason Codes in the UNSUBACK packet MUST match the order of Topic Filters in the UNSUBSCRIBE packet [MQTT-3.11.3-1]. The Server MUST use valid Unsubscribe Reason Code values (including Success, No subscription existed, Unspecified error, Implementation specific error, Not authorized, Topic Filter invalid, or Packet Identifier in use) for each Topic Filter [MQTT-3.11.3-2]. This rule applies when a Server responds to a valid UNSUBSCRIBE packet, and the resulting payload provides the Client with per-Topic Filter unsubscribe status, enabling proper subscription state management.

When a Server processes a SUBSCRIBE packet containing multiple Topic Filters, it MUST generate a SUBACK packet where each Reason Code in the payload corresponds exactly in sequence to the Topic Filters from the SUBSCRIBE packet. This sequential matching ensures that the Client can correctly associate each subscription outcome (including granted QoS levels or error conditions) with its respective Topic Filter. This rule applies specifically during the subscription acknowledgement phase after the Server receives a valid SUBSCRIBE packet and before it sends the corresponding SUBACK response. The Server MUST maintain the original Topic Filter ordering from the SUBSCRIBE packet and assign one valid Subscribe Reason Code for each filter, ensuring consistent interpretation of subscription results by the Client [MQTT-3.9.3-1].

When a Server processes a SUBSCRIBE packet, it MUST respond with a SUBACK packet containing Reason Codes that indicate the outcome for each requested Topic Filter. For successful subscriptions (Reason Codes 0x00, 0x01, and 0x02), the granted QoS level specified in the SUBACK MAY be lower than the QoS level originally requested in the SUBSCRIBE packet. This downgrade can occur due to Server policy, implementation limitations, or administrative restrictions. The Client MUST honor the granted QoS level for subsequent message deliveries on that subscription, even if it differs from the requested QoS. This rule applies specifically during the subscription acknowledgement phase after a valid SUBSCRIBE packet has been received and processed by the Server.

The SUBACK packet payload MUST contain a list of Subscribe Reason Codes that correspond one-to-one with the Topic Filters in the SUBSCRIBE packet being acknowledged, maintaining the same order [MQTT-3.9.3-1]. When the server accepts a subscription but limits the maximum Quality of Service (QoS) level, it MUST use Reason Code 0x00 ('Granted QoS 0') to indicate that the subscription is accepted but the maximum QoS level that will be delivered to the client is QoS 0. This applies when the server processes a valid SUBSCRIBE packet and either cannot support the requested QoS level or chooses to downgrade it, resulting in the client receiving published messages at most once (QoS 0) for this subscription, even if higher QoS levels were requested. The granted QoS level may be lower than what was originally requested by the client.

When a client sends a SUBSCRIBE packet containing one or more topic filters, the server responds with a SUBACK packet containing a corresponding list of reason codes. The reason code value 1 (0x01), named 'Granted QoS 1', indicates that the subscription request for the associated topic filter has been accepted, but the server will deliver messages with a maximum Quality of Service level of 1, even if the client requested a higher QoS. This code applies when the server can support the subscription but imposes QoS limitations, possibly due to server configuration, policy restrictions, or resource constraints. The granted QoS level must be reflected in all subsequent message deliveries for this subscription, and the reason code must appear in the SUBACK payload in the same position as the corresponding topic filter from the SUBSCRIBE packet.

When a Server processes a SUBSCRIBE packet, it MUST respond with a SUBACK packet containing Reason Codes that indicate the outcome for each requested Topic Filter. For successful subscriptions (Reason Codes 0x00, 0x01, and 0x02), the granted QoS level specified in the SUBACK MAY be lower than the QoS level originally requested in the SUBSCRIBE packet. This downgrade can occur due to Server policy, implementation limitations, or administrative restrictions. The Client MUST honor the granted QoS level for subsequent message deliveries on that subscription, even if it differs from the requested QoS. This rule applies specifically during the subscription acknowledgement phase after a valid SUBSCRIBE packet has been received and processed by the Server.

When a Server processes a SUBSCRIBE packet and cannot accept a subscription request, it MUST include a Reason Code of 128 (0x80) in the SUBACK payload for each rejected Topic Filter where the rejection reason is either unspecified or doesn't match any other defined Reason Code. This code indicates an unspecified error condition where the Server either cannot or chooses not to disclose the specific reason for rejection. The code MUST appear in the SUBACK payload in the same position as the corresponding Topic Filter in the original SUBSCRIBE packet, following the one-to-one mapping requirement between Topic Filters and Reason Codes. This applies when the subscription fails but none of the more specific error codes (such as authorization failures, quota limits, or feature unsupported errors) are appropriate.

Value 2 (Hex 0x02) with name 'Granted QoS 2' and description 'The subscription is accepted and any received QoS will be sent to this subscription'. This reason code is used in the SUBACK packet payload to indicate that the corresponding topic filter from the SUBSCRIBE packet has been successfully accepted by the server, and the client will receive messages published to that topic at the same QoS level as they were originally published, up to QoS 2. This applies when the server supports and grants the full QoS 2 capability for the subscription, allowing the client to receive messages with the publisher's intended delivery assurance level without downgrading. The reason code must appear in the SUBACK payload in the same order as the corresponding topic filter appeared in the SUBSCRIBE packet.

Value 131 (Hex 0x83) with name 'Implementation specific error' and description 'The SUBSCRIBE packet is syntactically valid and conforms to protocol rules, but the Server cannot accept the subscription due to implementation-specific constraints or policies not covered by other reason codes. This reason code MUST be included in the SUBACK payload for each affected Topic Filter, maintaining the same order as in the original SUBSCRIBE packet. When this code is returned, the subscription for the corresponding Topic Filter is rejected, and the Client should not expect to receive messages matching that filter. This condition applies only after the Server has validated the SUBSCRIBE packet structure and authorization but encountered internal limitations such as resource constraints, configuration restrictions, or other server-specific business logic.'

The Reason Code value 135 (Hex 0x87) with name 'Not authorized' indicates that the Client lacks the necessary permissions to subscribe to a specific topic filter. This code applies when the Server processes a SUBSCRIBE packet and determines that the Client's authorization credentials do not permit the requested subscription. The Server MUST include this Reason Code in the SUBACK packet payload, maintaining the same order as the corresponding Topic Filters in the SUBSCRIBE packet. When this code is returned, the subscription for that specific topic filter is rejected, and the Client will not receive any messages published to that topic. This authorization check occurs after the Server validates the SUBSCRIBE packet format but before granting any subscriptions, and the outcome is independent of QoS level requests.

Value 143 (Hex 0x8F) with name 'Topic Filter invalid' and description 'The Topic Filter is correctly formed but is not allowed for this Client'. This reason code is used in the SUBACK packet payload to indicate that a subscription request was syntactically valid but rejected due to server-side restrictions specific to the client. It applies when the server processes a SUBSCRIBE packet and determines that the client lacks permission to subscribe to the specified topic filter, even though the filter follows MQTT syntax rules. The server MUST include one reason code per topic filter in the SUBACK, maintaining the same order as in the SUBSCRIBE packet. When this code is returned, the subscription is not established, and the client should not retry the same topic filter without addressing the underlying authorization or policy restriction.

Value 158 (Hex 0x9E) with name 'Shared Subscriptions not supported' and description 'The Server does not support Shared Subscriptions for this Client'. This reason code applies when a Client attempts to subscribe to a Shared Subscription topic filter (beginning with "$share/") but the Server either does not implement Shared Subscriptions or has disabled them for this Client. The rule applies during the SUBACK packet generation phase, where the Server MUST include one reason code per topic filter in the same order as received in the SUBSCRIBE packet. When this reason code is returned, the corresponding Shared Subscription request is rejected and will not be established. This outcome occurs regardless of whether the Client is otherwise authorized or the topic filter is valid, as the fundamental capability is unavailable.

Value 145 (Hex 0x91) with name 'Packet Identifier in use' and description 'The specified Packet Identifier is already in use'. This reason code applies when a client sends a SUBSCRIBE packet with a Packet Identifier that is currently associated with an outstanding subscription request that has not yet been fully acknowledged. The server MUST include this reason code in the SUBACK packet for each Topic Filter in the corresponding SUBSCRIBE packet, maintaining the same order as the original subscription request. When this error occurs, the subscription request is rejected, and the client should use a different Packet Identifier for subsequent subscription requests. This condition typically arises when the client reuses Packet Identifiers before previous operations have completed.

Value 161 (Hex 0xA1) with name 'Subscription Identifiers not supported' and description 'This reason code indicates that the Server does not support Subscription Identifiers, a feature introduced in MQTT v5 that allows associating a numeric identifier with subscriptions for message routing. When a Client includes a Subscription Identifier in its SUBSCRIBE packet and the Server cannot process it, this code MUST be returned for each affected Topic Filter in the corresponding SUBACK packet, and the entire subscription request is rejected. This applies specifically when the Client attempts to use Subscription Identifiers but the Server lacks implementation support or has disabled this capability. The outcome is that no subscription is established, and the Client should not retry the same subscription with Subscription Identifiers to this Server.'

In the SUBACK packet payload, each Reason Code corresponds to a Topic Filter from the SUBSCRIBE packet in the same order. When a Reason Code applies to the entire subscription request rather than individual Topic Filters (such as 0x91 "Packet Identifier in use"), the server MUST replicate that same Reason Code for every Topic Filter in the SUBSCRIBE packet. This ensures the SUBACK payload maintains one-to-one correspondence with the SUBSCRIBE Topic Filters while consistently communicating subscription-level errors across all requested subscriptions.

The Reason String property (identifier 0x1F) in the AUTH packet variable header contains a UTF-8 encoded string that provides human-readable diagnostic information about the authentication exchange. This string is intended solely for human interpretation and troubleshooting purposes. Implementations MUST treat the Reason String as opaque diagnostic text and MUST NOT attempt to parse or extract structured data from its content. This rule applies whenever an AUTH packet includes the Reason String property. The sender MUST ensure that including this property does not cause the AUTH packet to exceed the receiver's specified Maximum Packet Size, and MUST NOT include multiple Reason String properties in the same AUTH packet as this constitutes a Protocol Error.

In the AUTH packet's variable header properties, the Reason String property (identifier 0x1F) must appear at most once. If a sender includes multiple instances of this property in a single AUTH packet, it constitutes a Protocol Error. This rule applies during the authentication exchange phase when either the client or server sends an AUTH packet, and the receiver must treat such duplication as a malformed packet, potentially leading to connection termination. The Reason String property remains optional and is subject to the Maximum Packet Size constraint, but its single occurrence, when present, is mandatory for protocol compliance.

When a client sends a SUBSCRIBE packet containing one or more topic filters using wildcard characters (such as '+' or '#'), and the server does not support wildcard subscriptions, the server MUST include Reason Code 162 (0xA2) in the SUBACK payload for each corresponding topic filter. This reason code indicates that the subscription request is rejected specifically due to the server's lack of wildcard subscription support. The server MUST maintain the order of reason codes to match the order of topic filters in the original SUBSCRIBE packet. When this rule applies, the client's subscription for that particular topic filter is not established, and no messages matching that filter will be delivered to the client. This condition applies only when the server configuration explicitly prohibits wildcard subscriptions, regardless of the client's authorization status or the validity of the topic filter syntax.

When constructing a DISCONNECT packet, the sender MUST evaluate whether including the Reason String property would cause the total packet size to exceed the Maximum Packet Size established by the receiver during connection negotiation. If such inclusion would result in exceeding this limit, the sender MUST omit the Reason String property entirely from the DISCONNECT packet. This rule applies during the packet construction phase prior to transmission and ensures compliance with the receiver's declared capacity constraints, while maintaining the diagnostic purpose of the Reason String as a human-readable, non-parsed element. The rule is conditional upon the receiver having previously specified a Maximum Packet Size during the CONNECT/CONNACK exchange.

When a Server responds to a SUBSCRIBE packet with a SUBACK packet, it MUST include exactly one Subscribe Reason Code for each Topic Filter received in the SUBSCRIBE packet, maintaining the same order as the Topic Filters [MQTT-3.9.3-1]. The Reason Code for each Topic Filter MUST be selected from the defined Subscribe Reason Codes (0x00-0x02 for successful subscriptions with granted QoS levels, or 0x80-0xA2 for various error conditions). This requirement applies after the Server has processed all Topic Filters in the SUBSCRIBE packet and is preparing the SUBACK response. The expected outcome is that each Topic Filter's subscription status is clearly communicated to the Client through its corresponding Reason Code, enabling the Client to understand which subscriptions were accepted (and at what QoS level) or rejected (with specific error indications). This rule applies regardless of whether the Reason Code is specific to individual Topic Filters or applies to all Topic Filters in the packet (such as when a Packet Identifier error occurs).

In the AUTH packet's variable header properties, the User Property (identifier 0x26) may be included multiple times to represent distinct name-value pairs, where each occurrence consists of a UTF-8 string pair. This allows the sender to provide multiple diagnostic or informational attributes. The rule applies when constructing AUTH packets for authentication exchange, provided that including all User Properties does not cause the packet size to exceed the receiver's specified Maximum Packet Size. When followed, the recipient must process all name-value pairs, including those with duplicate names, as valid authentication-related metadata.

When constructing a DISCONNECT packet, the sender MAY include one or more User Properties (identifier 0x26) as UTF-8 string pairs to provide diagnostic or other supplementary information. However, during packet assembly, the sender MUST verify that the cumulative size of all User Properties, combined with other packet components, does not exceed the Maximum Packet Size established by the receiver during connection negotiation. If including any User Property would cause the total DISCONNECT packet size to surpass this limit, the sender MUST omit that property entirely. This rule applies specifically during the DISCONNECT packet formulation phase and ensures protocol compliance with receiver-advertised size constraints [MQTT-3.14.2-4].

When a Server sends a SUBACK packet in response to a SUBSCRIBE packet, it MUST include exactly one Reason Code for each Topic Filter present in the original SUBSCRIBE packet. This requirement applies after the Server has processed the SUBSCRIBE packet and is constructing the SUBACK response. The Reason Codes MUST appear in the same order as their corresponding Topic Filters in the SUBSCRIBE packet, and each code MUST be selected from the defined Subscribe Reason Codes (including QoS grants, errors, and implementation-specific codes). This ensures that the Client can accurately map each subscription request to its outcome, whether successful or failed. This rule applies regardless of whether the Reason Code is specific to individual Topic Filters or applies to the entire subscription request (such as packet identifier errors), in which case the same code MUST be repeated for all Topic Filters.

In the DISCONNECT packet variable header properties, the User Property is identified by byte 38 (0x26) and consists of a UTF-8 String Pair representing a name-value pair. This property provides optional diagnostic or informational metadata and may appear multiple times within the same packet to convey multiple name-value pairs, including duplicate names. The sender MUST ensure that including User Property entries does not cause the DISCONNECT packet size to exceed the receiver's specified Maximum Packet Size; if it would, the sender MUST omit the User Property entirely. This rule applies when constructing DISCONNECT packets and ensures proper metadata handling while maintaining protocol size constraints.

In the UNSUBACK packet payload, each Reason Code MUST correspond positionally to a specific Topic Filter from the UNSUBSCRIBE packet being acknowledged, maintaining the same order as in the original request. This mapping applies when the server processes an UNSUBSCRIBE packet and generates the corresponding UNSUBACK response, with the outcome being that each Topic Filter's unsubscribe operation status is clearly indicated by its respective Reason Code. Prerequisites include: the UNSUBSCRIBE packet must contain valid Topic Filters, and the server MUST assign one of the defined Unsubscribe Reason Codes (such as Success, No subscription existed, or implementation-specific errors) for each filter, even when errors apply to all filters collectively.

The UNSUBACK packet payload MUST contain a list of one-byte unsigned Reason Codes, with exactly one Reason Code corresponding to each Topic Filter in the UNSUBSCRIBE packet being acknowledged. The order of Reason Codes in the UNSUBACK packet MUST match the order of Topic Filters in the UNSUBSCRIBE packet [MQTT-3.11.3-1]. The Server MUST use valid Unsubscribe Reason Code values (including Success, No subscription existed, Unspecified error, Implementation specific error, Not authorized, Topic Filter invalid, or Packet Identifier in use) for each Topic Filter [MQTT-3.11.3-2]. This rule applies when a Server responds to a valid UNSUBSCRIBE packet, and the resulting payload provides the Client with per-Topic Filter unsubscribe status, enabling proper subscription state management.

When a Server processes an UNSUBSCRIBE packet containing multiple Topic Filters, it MUST generate an UNSUBACK packet where the Reason Codes in the payload maintain the exact sequential order of the corresponding Topic Filters from the UNSUBSCRIBE packet. This ordering requirement ensures that the Client can correctly map each acknowledgement result to its original unsubscribe request. The rule applies specifically when the UNSUBSCRIBE packet contains multiple Topic Filters and the Server is sending the UNSUBACK response. Each Reason Code MUST correspond to the unsubscribe outcome for the Topic Filter at the same position in the original UNSUBSCRIBE packet, regardless of whether the outcome varies between different Topic Filters. [MQTT-3.11.3-1]

The UNSUBACK payload MUST contain one-byte unsigned Unsubscribe Reason Codes that correspond to each Topic Filter in the UNSUBSCRIBE packet, maintaining the same order. The server MUST assign one of the defined reason codes for each filter, indicating the outcome of the unsubscribe request. Valid codes include: 0x00 (Success - subscription deleted), 0x11 (No subscription existed), 0x80 (Unspecified error), 0x83 (Implementation specific error), 0x87 (Not authorized), 0x8F (Topic Filter invalid), and 0x91 (Packet Identifier in use). This applies after the server processes an UNSUBSCRIBE packet and sends the UNSUBACK response, ensuring clients receive specific feedback for each unsubscribe operation.

In the MQTT v5 protocol, when a server processes an UNSUBSCRIBE packet and sends an UNSUBACK response, it must include a Reason Code for each Topic Filter in the original unsubscribe request, maintaining the same order as specified in the UNSUBSCRIBE packet. The Reason Code value 0x00 ('Success') applies when the server has successfully deleted the specified subscription for the client. This code is used only after the server has validated the UNSUBSCRIBE packet, confirmed the client's authorization, and successfully removed the subscription. The outcome is that the client can consider the corresponding Topic Filter unsubscribed and will no longer receive matching publications. This rule applies during the unsubscribe acknowledgement flow and requires that a valid UNSUBSCRIBE packet with properly ordered Topic Filters has been received by the server.

When a server responds to an UNSUBSCRIBE packet by sending an UNSUBACK packet, it MUST include exactly one valid Unsubscribe Reason Code for each Topic Filter that was present in the original UNSUBSCRIBE request. These Reason Codes must be selected from the defined set of Unsubscribe Reason Code values (0x00, 0x11, 0x80, 0x83, 0x87, 0x8F, 0x91) and must appear in the same order as the corresponding Topic Filters in the UNSUBSCRIBE packet. This requirement applies regardless of whether the unsubscribe operation succeeded, failed, or encountered errors for individual Topic Filters, ensuring the client receives specific status information for each subscription removal attempt. [MQTT-3.11.3-2]

When a client sends an UNSUBSCRIBE packet containing one or more topic filters, the server must respond with an UNSUBACK packet containing a corresponding list of reason codes in the same order. The reason code value 17 (0x11) applies when the server receives a request to unsubscribe from a topic filter for which the client had no active subscription. This indicates that while the unsubscribe operation completed successfully from the server's perspective (since the desired end state of no subscription was already achieved), the client should be aware that no matching subscription existed to remove. This reason code is particularly useful for client-side state management, allowing clients to synchronize their subscription state with the server without treating this scenario as an error condition. The server must use this code only when the topic filter in the UNSUBSCRIBE packet is valid and properly formed, but no corresponding subscription was found for that client.

The UNSUBACK packet MUST include a Reason Code value of 135 (0x87) with the name 'Not authorized' when the Server determines that the Client lacks authorization to unsubscribe from one or more Topic Filters specified in the corresponding UNSUBSCRIBE packet. This rule applies during the unsubscribe acknowledgement phase, where the Server MUST match each Reason Code to the corresponding Topic Filter in the same order as received. When this Reason Code is used, it indicates that the unsubscribe request for the specific Topic Filter was rejected due to authorization failure, and the subscription state remains unchanged. This authorization check occurs after the Server validates the UNSUBSCRIBE packet format and before processing any unsubscribe operations, ensuring that only authorized Clients can modify their subscriptions.

The UNSUBACK packet payload MUST contain one Reason Code for each Topic Filter in the corresponding UNSUBSCRIBE packet, maintaining the same order [MQTT-3.11.3-1]. When the server encounters an unsubscribe failure where it cannot complete the request and either cannot disclose the specific reason or no other predefined Reason Code applies, it MUST use Reason Code 128 (0x80) with the name 'Unspecified error'. This code applies when the server processes an UNSUBSCRIBE packet but cannot determine the exact failure cause or chooses not to reveal it. The server MUST set this code for each affected Topic Filter in the response, and the client receiving this code should treat the unsubscribe operation as failed for the corresponding Topic Filter(s) without retrying the same request. This rule applies after the server validates the UNSUBSCRIBE packet structure but encounters an internal or unspecified error during unsubscribe processing.

When a server receives an UNSUBSCRIBE packet with a Packet Identifier that is already associated with an ongoing MQTT transaction, the server MUST respond with an UNSUBACK packet containing Reason Code 0x91 (Packet Identifier in use) for each Topic Filter in the original UNSUBSCRIBE packet. This applies when the client attempts to use a Packet Identifier value that is currently active for another pending request, violating the requirement that Packet Identifiers must be unique within ongoing communications. The server MUST maintain the one-to-one correspondence between UNSUBSCRIBE Topic Filters and UNSUBACK Reason Codes, even though this error condition relates to the packet-level identifier rather than individual topic subscriptions. Following this rule ensures proper protocol state management and prevents ambiguity in tracking client-server interactions.

The UNSUBACK packet payload MUST include one Reason Code for each Topic Filter in the corresponding UNSUBSCRIBE packet, maintaining the same order [MQTT-3.11.3-1]. When the server receives a valid UNSUBSCRIBE packet but encounters implementation-specific constraints preventing unsubscribe processing, it MUST use Reason Code 131 (0x83) for each affected Topic Filter. This applies during the UNSUBSCRIBE-UNSUBACK protocol flow when the packet structure and syntax are correct, but server-side implementation logic (such as resource limitations, configuration restrictions, or internal policy violations) prevents successful unsubscribe operation. The expected outcome is that the client receives acknowledgment that the unsubscribe request was rejected due to server implementation constraints, while the original subscriptions remain unchanged. This Reason Code should only be used when no other predefined Reason Code accurately describes the rejection reason, and the UNSUBSCRIBE packet itself passes basic validation checks.

When the UNSUBACK packet contains a Reason Code that applies to the entire unsubscribe operation rather than individual Topic Filters (such as 0x91 "Packet Identifier in use"), the server MUST replicate that same Reason Code for every Topic Filter entry in the UNSUBACK payload. This ensures the payload maintains the required one-to-one correspondence between Reason Codes and Topic Filters, preserving the order matching requirement specified in [MQTT-3.11.3-1]. This rule applies when the server encounters a global error condition during UNSUBSCRIBE packet processing that prevents evaluation of individual Topic Filters.

When a server processes an UNSUBSCRIBE packet and sends an UNSUBACK response, it must include exactly one Reason Code for each Topic Filter contained in the original UNSUBSCRIBE packet. The Reason Codes must appear in the same order as their corresponding Topic Filters, and each code must be selected from the defined set of Unsubscribe Reason Codes (0x00, 0x11, 0x80, 0x83, 0x87, 0x8F, 0x91). This applies regardless of whether the Reason Code is specific to individual Topic Filters (like subscription status) or applies globally to the entire request (such as Packet Identifier in use), in which case the same code is repeated for all Topic Filters. This ensures the client can accurately correlate unsubscribe results with each subscription it attempted to remove.

When a server processes an UNSUBSCRIBE packet, it must include a Reason Code for each Topic Filter in the corresponding UNSUBACK packet, with codes ordered to match the UNSUBSCRIBE Topic Filter sequence [MQTT-3.11.3-1, MQTT-3.11.3-2]. The Reason Code value 143 (0x8F), named 'Topic Filter invalid', applies when a Topic Filter is syntactically valid according to MQTT specification rules but is prohibited for the specific client due to server-side policies or configuration restrictions. This occurs during the UNSUBSCRIBE-UNSUBACK protocol flow when the server validates client permissions after receiving an UNSUBSCRIBE request. The expected outcome is that the server rejects the unsubscribe operation for that specific Topic Filter while processing other filters normally, and the client should not attempt to unsubscribe from that filter again unless the underlying authorization changes. This code differs from authorization errors (0x87) as it indicates the filter itself is disallowed rather than a general permission issue.

In the DISCONNECT packet variable header properties, the Session Expiry Interval property (identifier 0x11) must appear at most once. Including this property multiple times constitutes a Protocol Error. This rule applies during packet validation when processing DISCONNECT packets, regardless of whether the packet is sent by client or server. When violated, the receiver must treat the packet as malformed and respond with a DISCONNECT packet containing Reason Code 0x82 (Protocol Error) as specified in section 4.13. Note that servers are additionally prohibited from including this property in DISCONNECT packets entirely, and clients cannot set a non-zero Session Expiry Interval in DISCONNECT if the original CONNECT specified a zero value.

When processing a DISCONNECT packet, if the Session Expiry Interval property is not present in the packet's variable header, the Session Expiry Interval value previously established in the CONNECT packet during session initiation must be used. This rule applies specifically to client-initiated DISCONNECT packets where session state preservation is being negotiated. The expected outcome is that the session expiry timing continues to be governed by the originally agreed-upon interval from the CONNECT handshake. This behavior is conditional on the session having been initially established with a non-zero Session Expiry Interval in the CONNECT packet, and it must not be applied when the server is sending a DISCONNECT packet, as servers are prohibited from including this property.

The Session Expiry Interval property in the DISCONNECT packet MUST be encoded as a Four Byte Integer representing the interval duration in seconds, immediately following the property identifier byte (0x11). This rule applies when a client includes the Session Expiry Interval in a DISCONNECT packet to request modification of the session's expiration time. The expected outcome is that the server will update the session's expiry interval to the specified value, unless the client previously established the session with a zero expiry interval in the CONNECT packet—in which case including any non-zero value constitutes a Protocol Error, and the server MUST respond with a DISCONNECT packet containing Reason Code 0x82 (Protocol Error). Additionally, this property MUST NOT be included more than once in the DISCONNECT packet, and servers are prohibited from sending this property in any DISCONNECT packet.

The Session Expiry Interval property in the DISCONNECT packet is identified by the byte value 17 (0x11), followed by a four-byte integer representing the interval in seconds. This property must not be included more than once, as doing so constitutes a Protocol Error. If absent, the Session Expiry Interval from the CONNECT packet is used. Importantly, servers MUST NOT send this property in a DISCONNECT packet [MQTT-3.14.2-2]. Additionally, if the client's original CONNECT packet specified a Session Expiry Interval of zero, the client must not set a non-zero value in the DISCONNECT packet. If a server receives such an invalid DISCONNECT packet with a non-zero interval when the original was zero, it must treat this as a Protocol Error and respond with a DISCONNECT packet containing Reason Code 0x82 (Protocol Error), as specified in section 4.13.

The AUTH packet is used bidirectionally between Client and Server to facilitate extended authentication mechanisms like challenge/response authentication. This packet exchange occurs only after successful initial authentication method negotiation, where the CONNECT packet specified an authentication method. The AUTH packet must maintain the same authentication method as established in the CONNECT handshake - sending an AUTH packet with a different authentication method constitutes a Protocol Error and terminates the connection. This mechanism enables multi-step authentication flows beyond simple credential exchange while ensuring authentication method consistency throughout the session.

When a Server receives a DISCONNECT packet from a Client that contains a Session Expiry Interval property in violation of protocol requirements—specifically if the Client had previously established a session with a zero Session Expiry Interval in the CONNECT packet and now attempts to set a non-zero value in DISCONNECT—the Server MUST treat this as a malformed packet and respond by sending a DISCONNECT packet with Reason Code 0x82 (Protocol Error) as specified in section 4.13, thereby terminating the connection without processing the invalid DISCONNECT request.

When a Client sends a DISCONNECT packet containing a non-zero Session Expiry Interval property, but the original CONNECT packet had established a Session Expiry Interval of zero, the Server MUST treat this as a protocol violation. In this scenario, the Server SHALL NOT process the DISCONNECT packet as a normal disconnect request and instead MUST respond with a DISCONNECT packet containing Reason Code 0x82 (Protocol Error) as specified in section 4.13, effectively rejecting the invalid disconnect attempt while maintaining the session state according to the originally negotiated zero expiry interval.

The Session Expiry Interval property (identifier 0x11) MUST NOT be included in a DISCONNECT packet sent by the Server [MQTT-3.14.2-2]. This rule applies specifically to Server-originated DISCONNECT packets and governs the protocol behavior by prohibiting servers from attempting to modify session persistence settings during disconnection. When followed, the expected outcome is that the server properly terminates the connection without altering the session expiry configuration established during the CONNECT handshake. The prerequisite for this rule is that a valid MQTT session exists, and the server is initiating a controlled disconnection. Violation of this rule would constitute a protocol error, though the specification does not explicitly define client behavior upon receiving such an invalid property from the server.

The AUTH packet is used for extended authentication exchanges (e.g., challenge/response) between the Client and Server. This rule applies when either party sends an AUTH packet during the connection lifecycle. A Protocol Error occurs if the Authentication Method specified in the AUTH packet does not exactly match the Authentication Method declared in the initial CONNECT packet. This ensures authentication continuity and prevents mismatched security mechanisms. The rule is only applicable if the CONNECT packet included an Authentication Method; if no method was specified, sending any AUTH packet constitutes a Protocol Error.

When a Client sends a DISCONNECT packet, the Session Expiry Interval property must maintain consistency with the initial CONNECT packet. Specifically, if the Client previously established the session with a Session Expiry Interval of zero (indicating immediate session termination upon disconnection), it is a Protocol Error to specify a non-zero Session Expiry Interval in the DISCONNECT packet. This rule applies during the disconnection phase when the Client initiates session termination. If a Server receives such an invalid DISCONNECT packet, it must not process it as a valid disconnect request and should respond with a DISCONNECT packet containing Reason Code 0x82 (Protocol Error) as specified in section 4.13, effectively rejecting the malformed request while maintaining protocol state integrity.

The Variable Header of the AUTH Packet MUST contain the Authenticate Reason Code followed by Properties, in that order, when the packet is used for authentication exchange in MQTT v5. This structure applies during both initial and ongoing authentication flows, enabling clients and servers to indicate authentication status and exchange related metadata. The Authenticate Reason Code specifies the authentication outcome or next step, while Properties carry additional authentication data or context as defined in section 2.2.2. Proper adherence ensures interoperability and correct handling of extended authentication mechanisms.

In the MQTT v5 protocol, during the AUTH packet exchange for authentication, the Properties field within the Variable Header must have its total length encoded as a Variable Byte Integer. This encoding applies when the AUTH packet includes one or more properties, and it must be the first element in the Variable Header after the Authentication Reason Code. The Variable Byte Integer encoding ensures efficient representation of the properties length, which may range from 1 to 268,435,455 bytes. Proper adherence to this rule is required for the packet to be considered valid and correctly parsed by compliant MQTT clients and brokers.

The AUTH packet, used for authentication exchange in MQTT v5, must contain no Payload. This applies to all AUTH packets sent during the authentication flow, regardless of whether they are part of initial or subsequent authentication steps. When this rule is followed, the packet length calculation must reflect the absence of a Payload section, and receivers must not attempt to parse any Payload data from the packet. This requirement applies unconditionally to all AUTH packets in the protocol.

After a client or server sends a DISCONNECT packet to terminate an MQTT session, the sender MUST NOT transmit any further MQTT Control Packets over the same Network Connection. This rule applies immediately following the transmission of the DISCONNECT packet and remains in effect until the connection is closed. The expected outcome is a clean session termination where no additional protocol traffic flows on the connection. The sender MUST subsequently close the Network Connection, and upon receiving a DISCONNECT with Reason Code 0x00 (Success), the server MUST discard any associated Will Message without publishing it. The receiver SHOULD also close the Network Connection upon receipt of DISCONNECT.

The Authentication Method property in the AUTH packet MUST contain exactly one UTF-8 encoded string value identifying the authentication mechanism being used. This property is mandatory for AUTH packets and MUST appear exactly once in the properties section. If the client or server omits this property entirely, or includes multiple instances of it within the same AUTH packet, the receiver MUST treat this as a Protocol Error and close the network connection as specified in section 4.13. This validation occurs during packet parsing when processing the AUTH packet during authentication exchange.

Upon receiving a DISCONNECT packet, the receiving endpoint (client or server) SHOULD close the underlying Network Connection. This applies after the receiver has processed the DISCONNECT packet and any associated actions, such as discarding Will Messages when the Reason Code indicates normal disconnection. The expected outcome is termination of the network link, ensuring no further MQTT Control Packets are exchanged over that connection. This action should be taken regardless of whether the DISCONNECT was initiated normally or due to an error condition, though implementations may delay closure briefly to ensure all preceding packets are properly handled.

The Authentication Method property (identifier 0x15) MUST be followed by a UTF-8 Encoded String that specifies the name of the authentication method being used. This property MUST appear exactly once in the AUTH packet; omitting it or including it multiple times constitutes a Protocol Error. The authentication method name identifies the specific authentication mechanism and data format to be used during the extended authentication exchange, as defined in section 4.12. This property applies when initiating or continuing an authentication handshake using the AUTH control packet.

When a client initiates a clean disconnection by sending a DISCONNECT packet with Reason Code 0x00 (Success), the server MUST recognize this as an intentional termination of the connection and MUST discard any Will Message that was previously configured for this connection during the CONNECT handshake. This rule applies specifically when the server receives the DISCONNECT packet before the connection is closed, and ensures that the Will Message is not published to subscribers, thereby preventing unintended notifications when the client disconnects gracefully. The server must complete this action before closing the network connection as specified in section 3.1.2.5.

During the MQTT v5 authentication exchange, the AUTH packet's Fixed Header must have bits 3, 2, 1, and 0 set to 0, as these are reserved for future use. This rule applies whenever an AUTH packet is sent by either the client or server during enhanced authentication flows. If any of these bits are set to a non-zero value, the receiving party must treat the packet as malformed and immediately close the network connection. This validation occurs immediately upon receiving the AUTH packet, before processing any other packet contents, and is mandatory for all AUTH packets regardless of authentication method or direction.

In the MQTT v5 AUTH packet, the Authentication Data property (identifier 0x16) must appear at most once in the variable header. Including this property more than once constitutes a Protocol Error, which requires the receiver to close the network connection as specified in section 4.13. This rule applies during the extended authentication exchange when the client or server sends an AUTH packet, and the authentication method in use defines the format and interpretation of the Authentication Data.

The Authentication Data property (identifier 0x16) MUST be followed by Binary Data containing authentication information specific to the authentication method being used. This property MUST NOT appear more than once in an AUTH packet, as including it multiple times constitutes a Protocol Error. The contents and interpretation of this binary data are defined by the authentication method negotiated between client and server, and implementers should refer to section 4.12 for detailed guidance on extended authentication flows. This property applies when performing enhanced authentication exchanges and provides the necessary data to continue or complete the authentication process.

When processing an AUTH packet during MQTT v5 authentication exchange, the Client or Server MUST verify that bits 3, 2, 1, and 0 in the first byte of the Fixed Header are all set to 0, as these are reserved bits. If any of these reserved bits contain a non-zero value, the implementation MUST treat the packet as malformed and immediately close the Network Connection [MQTT-3.15.1-1]. This validation occurs immediately upon receiving the AUTH packet, before processing any other packet contents, to ensure protocol compliance and prevent potential security issues from malformed packet structures.

The Authentication Data property (identifier 0x16) contains binary data whose structure and interpretation are exclusively determined by the negotiated authentication method. This rule applies during the AUTH packet exchange when extended authentication is in progress, and the contents must conform to the requirements of the specific authentication mechanism being used (e.g., SCRAM, OAuth). Implementations must not include this property more than once in a single AUTH packet, as doing so constitutes a Protocol Error. For detailed specifications of supported authentication methods, refer to section 4.12.

The AUTH packet Fixed Header structure governs the initial authentication exchange format in MQTT v5. This rule applies when either the client or server initiates or continues an authentication handshake during connection establishment or re-authentication. Byte 1 must contain the MQTT Control Packet type value 15 (0xF0) in bits 7-4, with bits 3-0 reserved and required to be set to 0. Byte 2 contains the Remaining Length encoded as a Variable Byte Integer, representing the length of the Variable Header. Prerequisites include the use of MQTT v5 protocol and support for enhanced authentication mechanisms. The expected outcome is proper packet framing for authentication flows, where any violation (non-zero reserved bits or malformed structure) must result in treating the packet as malformed and closing the network connection [MQTT-3.15.1-1].
req_fields: Fixed Header Byte 1 (bits 7-4: Packet Type 15, bits 3-0: Reserved)

rule: Bits 0 and 1 of the Subscription Options byte in the SUBSCRIBE packet payload represent the Maximum QoS field, which specifies the highest Quality of Service level at which the Server is permitted to deliver Application Messages to the Client for this subscription. This rule applies during the subscription establishment phase when a Client sends a SUBSCRIBE packet to the Server. The expected outcome is that the Server will respect the specified QoS ceiling when forwarding messages matching the subscribed Topic Filter. A prerequisite is that the Client must include valid Subscription Options in the SUBSCRIBE payload. Note that setting the Maximum QoS field to value 3 constitutes a Protocol Error, as this value is reserved and invalid for QoS specification.

When a client subscribes with the No Local option (bit 2 of Subscription Options) set to 1, the server MUST NOT deliver any Application Messages published by connections using the same ClientID to that subscription. This prevents message loops when a client with multiple connections publishes messages that would otherwise be echoed back to itself. The rule applies during message forwarding after successful subscription establishment and requires that the publishing and subscribing connections share identical ClientIDs. If violated, it constitutes a protocol error [MQTT-3.8.3-3].

In the SUBSCRIBE packet payload, bits 0 and 1 of the Subscription Options byte represent the Maximum QoS field, which specifies the highest QoS level at which the Server can deliver Application Messages to the Client. Since QoS levels are only defined as 0, 1, and 2 in the MQTT protocol, it is a Protocol Error if the Maximum QoS field contains the value 3. This validation occurs when processing the SUBSCRIBE packet payload, and if violated, the Server must treat the packet as malformed and close the connection as specified by the protocol error handling rules.

The Maximum QoS field (bits 0-1 of the Subscription Options byte in the SUBSCRIBE packet payload) specifies the highest Quality of Service level that the Server is permitted to use when forwarding Application Messages to the Client for this subscription. This rule applies during the processing of SUBSCRIBE requests and governs the Server's message delivery behavior for the established subscription. The expected outcome is that the Server will never send messages with a QoS level exceeding the specified maximum, downgrading higher QoS publications if necessary. Prerequisites include valid SUBSCRIBE packet format and proper session establishment. Note that values 0, 1, and 2 are valid QoS levels, while value 3 constitutes a Protocol Error and must be rejected by the Server.

Bit 2 of the Subscription Options byte in the SUBSCRIBE packet payload represents the No Local option. When set to 1, this option prevents the server from forwarding Application Messages to any connection with the same ClientID as the publishing connection, effectively blocking local message loops. This rule applies during the processing of SUBSCRIBE requests and remains in effect for the duration of the subscription. However, setting the No Local bit to 1 on a Shared Subscription constitutes a Protocol Error and MUST be rejected by the server. This mechanism is particularly useful in bridging scenarios where a client acts as an intermediary between different MQTT servers.

When a client sends a SUBSCRIBE packet with a Shared Subscription (identified by a topic filter starting with $share/), the No Local option bit (bit 2 of the Subscription Options byte) MUST NOT be set to 1. If this occurs, the Server MUST treat it as a Protocol Error and close the connection. This restriction applies because Shared Subscriptions are designed to distribute messages among multiple subscribers in a consumer group, making the No Local option (which prevents message delivery to the publishing client) conceptually incompatible with the shared subscription model. The rule is enforced during the processing of the SUBSCRIBE packet payload before any subscription is established. [MQTT-3.8.3-4]

When a Client subscribes to a topic by setting the Retain As Published option (bit 3 of the Subscription Options byte) to 1 in a SUBSCRIBE packet, the Server MUST preserve the original RETAIN flag value from published Application Messages when forwarding them to that Client. This applies to all Application Messages delivered through the subscription after it is established, including both live messages and retained messages sent at subscription time. If this option is set to 0, the Server MUST set the RETAIN flag to 0 for all forwarded messages. This behavior is independent of the Retain Handling option and applies only if the Server supports retained messages and the subscription is successfully processed without protocol errors.

When a Client subscribes to a topic filter with the Retain As Published option set to 0 (bit 3 of the Subscription Options byte), the Server MUST forward Application Messages through this subscription with the RETAIN flag set to 0, regardless of the original RETAIN flag value from the publisher. This rule applies to all Application Messages forwarded after the subscription is established, except for retained messages sent during the initial subscription establishment (which retain their RETAIN flag as 1). This behavior ensures that subscribers can control whether they receive messages with retained status indicators, which is particularly useful in bridging scenarios where the Client acts as an intermediary between MQTT servers.

Bit 3 of the Subscription Options represents the Retain As Published option, which governs how the RETAIN flag is handled when Application Messages are forwarded to the subscriber. This rule applies during the processing of SUBSCRIBE packets when establishing or updating subscriptions. When set to 1, the Server MUST preserve the original RETAIN flag from the published message. When set to 0, the Server MUST set the RETAIN flag to 0 in forwarded messages. Retained messages sent during subscription establishment always have the RETAIN flag set to 1 regardless of this setting. This option is particularly useful for bridging scenarios where message metadata must be preserved across connections. The rule applies to all valid SUBSCRIBE packets and requires that the Server properly interpret and apply the Retain As Published setting for each subscription in the payload.

When a client establishes a subscription and there are no retained messages matching the subscribed Topic Filter, all valid values of the Retain Handling option (0, 1, and 2) produce identical behavior—no retained messages are sent to the client. This applies specifically during the initial subscription establishment phase and only when the server has no retained messages stored for that Topic Filter. The rule does not affect the handling of non-retained messages or retained messages that may be published after the subscription is active. This behavior ensures consistent client experience regardless of Retain Handling configuration when no pre-existing retained messages are available.

Bits 4 and 5 of the Subscription Options byte in the SUBSCRIBE packet payload represent the Retain Handling option, which governs how retained messages are handled when a subscription is established. This rule applies during the subscription setup phase when a client sends a SUBSCRIBE packet to the server. The Retain Handling option specifies whether retained messages matching the topic filter are sent immediately upon subscription: value 0 sends all retained messages, value 1 sends retained messages only for new subscriptions, and value 2 prevents sending retained messages entirely. This option only affects initial subscription establishment and has no impact on subsequent message flows. The server must validate that the value is not 3, as this constitutes a protocol error. This behavior applies to all standard MQTT subscriptions unless the server indicates it does not support retained messages, in which case all Retain Handling values produce equivalent results (no retained messages sent).

The Retain Handling option (bits 4-5 of the Subscription Options byte) governs the delivery of retained messages when a subscription is first established. This rule applies during the processing of a SUBSCRIBE packet, specifically when the Server evaluates whether to send existing retained messages matching the subscription's Topic Filter. The option supports three behaviors: value 0 sends all retained messages immediately upon subscription; value 1 sends retained messages only if this is a new subscription (not reusing an existing session); and value 2 prevents any retained messages from being sent at subscription time.
